parse.why: HCGPE ionic transport and stability metrics are explicitly reported: “The ionic conductivity of HCGPE reaches 4.8 mS cm−1 at 30 °C.”; “It is observed that the tLi+ of HCGPE (0.56) is slightly higher than the HCE (0.48)…”; “HCGPE shows an oxidation peak at ≈ 3.9 V… This interphase is ionically conductive and electronically insulated… confirmed by the absence of an oxidation peak at this voltage in the following LSV test.”

HCE (LiFSI/DME, LiFSI:DME=1.9) was used as the electrolyte in Li metal batteries. Reported for HCE: Li-ion transference number t_Li+ = 0.48 (Figure S15); activation energy for ionic conduction Ea = 0.18 eV (Arrhenius comparison); Li/Cu Coulombic efficiency ≈99.3% (Figure 3b); Li/Li symmetric cell short-circuited after ≈740 h at 2.0 mA cm^-2 / 1.0 mAh cm^-2 (Figure 3j); exchange current density for Li plating/stripping = 0.984 mA cm^-2 (Figure S22); deposited Li thickness ≈9.1 μm for 1 mAh cm^-2 (Figures 3e,h). LSV shows HCE has a lower onset oxidation potential than HCGPE (Figure 1h).

LAWTP is reported as a solid electrolyte. Its standalone performance shows a room-temperature ionic conductivity of 1.07 mS/cm: "The LAWTP sample has a room temperature ionic conductivity of 1.07 mS/cm" (Fig. 2c–2d).

PVDF-HFP-SN was used as the solid electrolyte in LFP/PVDF-HFP-SN/Li quasi-solid-state cells. At room temperature and 0.5 C, the initial discharge specific capacity is 128.35 mAh/g, and after 500 cycles a capacity of 114.30 mAh/g is maintained (89.05% retention) (Fig. 9, text). Its ionic conductivities are 1.07×10^-5 S/cm (30 °C) and 3.52×10^-5 S/cm (50 °C) (Fig. 4a–b), electrochemical stability window ~4.7 V (Fig. 4e), and Li-ion transference number 0.296 (Table 2).

PVDF-HFP-SN-0.05LAWTP is defined in Section 2.2 as a PVDF-HFP-SN-LAWTP solid composite electrolyte with 0.05 g LAWTP. Electrochemical characterizations (EIS/ionic conductivity and LSV) for various LAWTP contents including 0.05 g are shown in Fig. 4a–4b, but the text only reports numeric values for the 0.1 g sample; no specific numerical performance for PVDF-HFP-SN-0.05LAWTP is provided. Therefore metrics are left null.

PVDF-HFP-SN-0.1LAWTP is used as a solid composite electrolyte for Li-ion batteries. It shows optimum ionic conductivities of 3.03×10^-5 S/cm at 30°C and 1×10^-4 S/cm at 50°C (Fig. 4a–b). The electrochemical stability window (decomposition voltage) is 4.8 V (Fig. 4e). The Li+ transference number is 0.478 at 50°C (Table 2; Fig. 6c–d). In Li/Li symmetric cells, it maintains stable plating/stripping for 1000 cycles at 0.1 mA/cm^2 without short circuit and with lower, stable polarization (Fig. 7b). In LFP/PVDF-HFP-SN-0.1LAWTP/Li cells at 50°C and 0.5C, the initial specific capacity is 158.06 mAh/g and 133.82 mAh/g after 200 cycles (84.66% retention) (Fig. 8b,d). At room temperature, it delivers 142.69 mAh/g initially and 136.41 mAh/g after 500 cycles (95.60% retention; CE >99% after the initial cycle) (Fig. 9b,d). With 5 μL liquid electrolyte infiltration (same membrane under modified condition), ionic conductivity increases to 3.64×10^-4 S/cm at 30°C and 7×10^-4 S/cm at 50°C and t_Li+ is 0.323, while the stability window is ~4.5 V (Fig. 5; Fig. S5).

PVDF-HFP-SN-0.2LAWTP is one of the prepared solid composite electrolytes (PVDF-HFP-SN-LAWTP with 0, 0.05, 0.1, and 0.2 g LAWTP). Its application is as a Li-ion battery electrolyte (SS/SCE/SS and Li/SCE/Li cells; LSV, EIS, tLi+ tests). However, specific numeric performance values are only reported explicitly for the 0.1 g sample (e.g., ionic conductivity 3.03e-5 and 1e-4 S/cm at 30 and 50 °C; ESW 4.8 V; tLi+ = 0.478). For the 0.2 g sample, Fig. 4a–b include EIS/ionic-conductivity curves at 303 K across contents and the text notes conductivity decreases beyond 0.1 g, but no extractable numeric values are provided; thus metrics are left null.

Neat [HMG][FSI] (the OIPC) was evaluated as an electrolyte: its cyclic voltammetry at 75 °C showed a very wide electrochemical window (~8 V), with a cathodic limit about −4 V and an anodic limit about +4 V vs Pt (Fig. 7a: "The CV shows a very wide electrochemical window (~8 V)"). Additionally, the neat [HMG][FSI] melts at 87 °C, resulting in a significant increase in ionic conductivity (Section 4.2). No numeric conductivity value was reported for the neat sample.

For (PMMA)0.91(IM14)0.09, the paper reports only materials characterization (DSC/TGA and ssNMR) without application performance metrics. Evidence: "The solid samples ... (PMMA)0.91(IM14)0.09 ... Thermal analysis and solid-state NMR (ssNMR) were employed to comprehensively characterize the solid samples." (Experimental). Table 1 provides Tg and degradation temperatures (thermal properties), not application outputs. Electrolyte-oriented diffusion/PFG NMR data were collected for different compositions (e.g., (DBUH-IM14)0.60-(PMMA)0.40 and (DBUH-IM14)0.55-(Li)0.06-(PMMA)0.39), not for (PMMA)0.91(IM14)0.09: "considering the potential application of gels as safer electrolytes for energy storage devices, a ternary polymeric sample was prepared..." and "The self-diffusion coefficients of the (DBUH-IM14)0.60-(PMMA)0.40 and (DBUH-IM14)0.55-(Li)0.06-(PMMA)0.39 systems are presented..." Hence no applicable capacity/selectivity/breakthrough/regeneration data for (PMMA)0.91(IM14)0.09.

The specific solid sample (PMMA)0.91-(TFSI)0.09 is prepared and characterized by DSC/TGA and ssNMR ("The solid samples, referred to as ... (PMMA)0.91(TFSI)0.09 ... Thermal analysis and solid-state NMR (ssNMR) were employed..."). Table 1 reports Tg and degradation temperatures for (PMMA)0.91-(TFSI)0.09, which are material properties rather than application performance metrics. No electrochemical performance (e.g., ionic conductivity, Li+ transport, battery cycling) or other application metrics (catalysis/adsorption) are provided for this sample. Transport/diffusion analysis by PFG-NMR and gel-like electrolyte discussion are restricted to IM14-based systems, not the TFSI blend ("The self-diffusion coefficients ... are presented ... for IM14-based systems ..."). Therefore, no application performance data can be extracted for (PMMA)0.91-(TFSI)0.09.

For (PMMA)0.91-(TFO)0.09 the paper reports DSC/TGA (e.g., Tg=57°C; Td plateaus) and ssNMR observations only. No application testing or performance metrics (e.g., ionic conductivity, battery cycling, catalysis, adsorption) are given for this specific sample. The electrolyte-related diffusion data and Li-containing tests concern IM14-based gel-like systems, not the TFO-blended PMMA solid. Therefore, no application-specific values can be extracted for (PMMA)0.91-(TFO)0.09.

The study frames IM14-based gels as candidates for safer electrolytes for energy storage devices and explicitly investigates the gel-like sample (DBUH-IM14)0.60-(PMMA)0.40: “considering the potential application of gels as safer electrolytes for energy storage devices … Two IM14-gel-like samples, (DBUH-IM14)0.60-(PMMA)0.40 … were obtained …” and “The self-diffusion coefficients of the (DBUH-IM14)0.60-(PMMA)0.40 … are presented … at three different temperatures.” For (DBUH-IM14)0.60-(PMMA)0.40, apparent self-diffusion coefficients (m2 s-1) are reported: at 40°C DN-H = 3.08E-12 (w=0.75), DDBUH+ = 1.85E-12 (w=0.92), DIM14- = 1.23E-12; at 55°C DN-H = 7.55E-12 (w=0.94), DDBUH+ = 4.90E-12 (w=0.96), DIM14- = 3.51E-12; at 70°C DN-H = 1.51E-11 (w=0.97), DDBUH+ = 1.08E-11 (w=0.97), DIM14- = 7.54E-12. The text concludes that PMMA confinement significantly reduces ion diffusion relative to neat PILs, supporting its evaluation as a polymeric electrolyte matrix.

The material is an IM14-gel-like electrolyte sample for LIBs: “(DBUH-IM14)0.55-(Li)0.06-(PMMA)0.39” prepared by blending DBUH-LiIM14 with 10 wt.% PMMA (T > 100 °C). The study explores gels “as safer electrolytes for energy storage devices” and concludes PILs are “promising candidates for solid polymeric electrolytes in LIBs.” For this specific sample, PFG-NMR provided apparent self-diffusion coefficients (Table 2): at 40 °C: DN-H = 3.06E-12 m2.s-1 (w = 0.64), DDBUH+ = 2.01E-12 m2.s-1 (w = 0.86), DIM14- = 1.18E-12 m2.s-1 (w = 0.97); at 55 °C: DN-H = 6.47E-12 m2.s-1 (w = 0.87), DDBUH+ = 4.90E-12 m2.s-1 (w = 0.94), DIM14- = 3.14E-12 m2.s-1 (w = 0.98); at 70 °C: DN-H = 1.39E-11 m2.s-1 (w = 0.94), DDBUH+ = 1.16E-11 m2.s-1 (w = 0.95), DIM14- = 6.98E-12 m2.s-1 (w = 0.98). The Li+ self-diffusion coefficient measurement for (DBUH-IM14)0.55-(Li)0.06-(PMMA)0.39 “was not feasible due to its very short T2.”

No application/performance data are reported for monomer M1 (Pentafluorophenylacrylate). The quantitative conversions, kinetics, and reactivity discussed in the paper pertain to the polymer PM1 (poly(pentafluorophenylacrylate)) and related polymers (PM2–PM4). Mentions of M1 concern synthesis, characterization, stability, and solubility, not application metrics.

No application/performance data are reported for the monomer M2 (pentafluorophenylmethacrylate). The quantitative conversions and kinetics provided (e.g., Table 1, FT-IR studies) pertain to polymers PM1–PM4, notably PM2 (poly(pentafluorophenylmethacrylate)), not to the monomer M2 itself.

Table 1: "Hexylamine (1 equivalent) … PM1 (%) 99" (24 h, 50 °C, DMF). Table 2: for PM1 with Hexylamine (1 equivalent) k = 1.85 × 10^-3 s^-1, τ1/2 = 6 min (50 °C, THF). These data describe PM1’s polymer-analogous reaction performance toward hexylamine.

From Table 1 (NMR-analysis, 24 h at 50 °C in DMF): PM2 gives 65% conversion with hexylamine (1 eq.), 65% with N-hexylmethylamine (1 eq.), 0% with aniline (1.5 eq.), and 0% with 1-hexanol/triethylamine (1 eq. each or 7 eq. each). The Conclusions reiterate that primary amines show 65% conversion with PM2 under comparable conditions.

Table 2 explicitly reports for PFPE10-DMC (PFPE_E10-DMC) the maximum LiTFSI loading as 31% (2.70 mol L-1, r = 0.16). The text notes: 'LiTFSI salt was dissolved in the PFPE polymers to form an electrolyte... The maximum lithium salt-loading in the PFPE_E10 polymers, as shown in Table 2...' confirming this electrolyte solubility performance.

This material is a lithium solid electrolyte; ionic conductivity (σ) was measured for XL-POSS0.12-PFPE_0.30. The paper shows 'a typical impedance spectrum at 90 °C of the XL-POSS0.12-PFPE_0.3 electrolyte' (Fig. 2) and compares conductivities at w_LiTFSI = 0.30 (Fig. 7a). Text states: 'On average, σ of POSS_0.30 and PFPE_0.30 is higher than that of XL-POSS0.12-PFPE_0.30 by factors of 16.4 ± 6.4 and 4.7 ± 0.9, respectively.' and 'The conductivity of XL-POSS0.12-PFPE_0.30 is larger than that of XL-PFPE_0.30 by factors ranging from 10 to 100.' No absolute σ value for XL-POSS0.12-PFPE_0.30 at 90 °C is explicitly reported in text (only comparative factors and plots), so the conductivity value is left null per instruction. Thermal stability for this specific composition is given: 'onset temperature of electrolyte degradation (Td) ... 262 °C for the XL-POSS0.12-PFPE_0.30' (Fig. 6a), supporting its suitability as a solid electrolyte for lithium batteries.

The material XL-POSS0.13PFPE0.35 is explicitly presented as a crosslinked solid electrolyte sample (STEM of XL-POSS0.13PFPE0.35 in Fig. 3b; EDS map discussion indicates TFSI− anions localized in PFPE-rich domains). However, all reported quantitative ionic conductivity, Tg, and Td data are for other compositions (e.g., XL-POSS0.12-PFPE at various wLiTFSI), not specifically for wPOSS=0.13 and wLiTFSI=0.35. Therefore, scenario and target species (Li+ conduction in LiTFSI-based solid electrolyte) are extracted, while performance numbers are left null for this exact composition.

Fig. 7b reports that for XL-PFPE solid electrolytes the ionic conductivity at 90°C reaches a plateau of 6.5 ± 0.2 × 10^-5 S/cm for w_LiTFSI ≥ 0.40; thus XL-PFPE_0.40 falls within this plateau. Additionally, STEM/EDS for XL-PFPE_0.40 shows a homogeneous morphology with uniform S and F distributions, indicating uniform salt distribution (Figs. 3a, 4a).

PFP is blended with PEO to form a solid polymer electrolyte used in Li metal batteries. Key performance for the PFP-containing electrolyte (80PEO20PFP) includes: “the 80PEO20PFP polymer electrolyte exhibits a high lithium-ion transference number of 0.39 and ionic conductivity of 3.7 × 10^−4 S cm^−1 at 60 °C.” (Conclusion). Additional related evidence: oxidation potential increased to 4.4 V vs. Li+/Li for 80PEO20PFP (Fig. 3c text); Li|80PEO20PFP|Li cell maintained stable cycling >1700 h with ~95 mV overpotential at 60 °C (Fig. 5b text); Li|80PEO20PFP|LiFePO4 delivered 150 mAh g^−1 at 1C at 60 °C (Fig. 5c text); at 28 °C with SN-coated cathode, capacity 128.24 mAh g^−1 after 100 cycles (88% retention) with ~100% CE (Fig. 5d,e).

Additionally, the t_{Li+} of the 80PEO20PEP electrolyte was also tested and found to be approximately 0.18 (Fig. S5), higher than that of the PEO electrolyte which could be due to the abundant of carbonyl group in PEP.

FR-PU is used as a solid polymer electrolyte host for Li batteries. With 20 wt% LiTFSI (FR-PU-20% Li), the LiFePO4/FR-PU-20% Li/Li cell at 70 °C maintains a specific discharge capacity of 155.1 mAhg-1 and coulombic efficiency of 98.3% after 120 cycles at 0.2 C (Fig. 8c; Table 3). The FR-PU-20% Li electrolyte shows ionic conductivity up to 1.51 × 10^-4 S cm^-1 at 70 °C (Fig. 7a,b; Table 3) and an electrochemical stability window around 4.81 V vs. Li+/Li at 70 °C (Fig. 7c). These results directly evidence FR-PU’s application as a solid-state battery electrolyte transporting Li+.

Pure PU flame-retardancy data: Table 1 lists LOI 16.8%, Tp 402.4 °C, PHRR 55.4 W·g−1, THR 6.1 kJ·g−1, ID/IG 2.137. Text with Fig. 2a: 'Pure-PU burns violently with molten dripping after 5 s of ignition, and almost burns out after about 10 s of ignition.'

FR-PU solid electrolytes were prepared with LiTFSI mass fractions of 5%, 10%, 15%, 20% and 25% ('The mass ratio of FR-PU and LiTFSI are 95:5, 90:10, 85:15, 80:20 and 75:25... the flame-retardant polyurethane solid electrolytes with the mass fraction of lithium salt of 5%, 10%, 15%, 20% and 25% could be prepared'). The sample FR-PU-5% Li is explicitly listed (Table 2) as a solid electrolyte composition. Electrochemical measurements (ionic conductivity, LSV/CV) were performed on FR-PU solid electrolytes (Section 2.5.2; Fig. 7), but specific numeric ionic conductivity/electrochemical window values are only reported for FR-PU-20% Li (Table 3; Fig. 7c–d). Therefore, for FR-PU-5% Li we can confirm the application as a Li+ solid polymer electrolyte but no specific performance numbers are reported.

FR-PU-10% Li is one composition of the FR-PU solid electrolytes series prepared with LiTFSI (mass ratios 95:5, 90:10, 85:15, 80:20, 75:25), used as solid electrolytes in lithium batteries (Sections 2.3, 2.5.2). Ionic conductivity was measured for all compositions (Fig. 7a,b), but explicit numeric conductivity values are only reported for FR-PU-20% Li (Table 3, text in 3.4). No quantitative electrochemical/battery cycling data are provided specifically for FR-PU-10% Li; thus conductivity and other performance fields are left null.

FR-PU-15% Li is one of the prepared FR-PU solid polymer electrolytes (mass ratios 95:5, 90:10, 85:15, 80:20, 75:25 in Section 2.3), intended for lithium battery electrolyte use. Table 2 provides only thermal/mechanical data for FR-PU-15% Li (Td,5%, residue char, Tg, tensile strength, elongation). Electrochemical/battery performance values (ionic conductivity, electrochemical window, cycling) are reported specifically for FR-PU-20% Li (Fig. 7, Fig. 8, Table 3). The ionic conductivity vs. composition in Fig. 7a is shown as curves without numeric values for the 15% Li sample. Therefore, only the application scenario (solid polymer electrolyte for Li+ conduction) can be stated for FR-PU-15% Li, with no quantitative performance extracted.

FR-PU-20% Li is used as a solid polymer electrolyte for Li batteries. Its ionic conductivity at 70 °C is 1.51 × 10^-4 S cm^-1 (Fig. 7b; Table 3). Electrochemical stability window at 70 °C is ~4.81 V vs. Li+/Li (Fig. 7c). In LiFePO4/FR-PU-20% Li/Li cells at 70 °C, discharge capacities are 157.2, 142.6, 120.6, and 61.5 mAh g^-1 at 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, and 1 C, respectively, and 155.1 mAh g^-1 after 120 cycles at 0.2 C (Fig. 8; Table 3).

FR-PU-25% Li is explicitly prepared as a flame-retardant polyurethane solid electrolyte (mass fraction of lithium salt 25%) (Section 2.3: “the mass fraction of lithium salt of 5%, 10%, 15%, 20% and 25% could be prepared”). Table 2 lists FR-PU-25% Li among the FR-PU solid electrolytes (thermal/mechanical data). Ionic conductivity and battery performance values are only reported numerically for FR-PU-20% Li (Table 3 and Fig. 7c–d), and ionic conductivity vs. content for 25% Li appears only as a curve without specific readouts (Fig. 7a–b). Therefore, no quantitative electrochemical performance can be extracted for FR-PU-25% Li.

Ta-ESM (LLZTO-incorporated PVdF-HFP/PBMA electrospun membrane) is used as the electrolyte in Li/Ta-ESM/Li and Li/Ta-ESM/LiFePO4 cells. Rate performance reports discharge capacities of 178.63, 176.72, 173.04, 167.33 and 153.26 mAh g^-1 at 0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.5 and 1 C, respectively; thus capacity at 1 C is 153.26 mAh g^-1. Additional performance evidence: symmetric Li/Ta-ESM/Li shows stable stripping–plating for 567 h at 0.1 mA cm^-2; ionic conductivity 4.858×10^-3 S cm^-1 at RT (6.026×10^-3 S cm^-1 at 70 °C); Li+ transference number 0.61; electrochemical stability window >5 V; interfacial resistance 45–62 Ω cm^-2 over 12 days; electrolyte leakage 0.057%; porosity 64.1%; electrolyte uptake 582%.

Rate performance (ESM): “The discharge capacity of ESM based lithium-ion cell was found to be 171.07, 163.71, 155.57, 144.42 and 136.65 mA h g^{-1} at 0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.5 and 1 C, respectively.” Symmetric cell stability (ESM): “the LLZTO free electrospun electrolyte delivers lithium striping-plating with fluctuation in cycles for 213 h and beyond that cell got shorted.” The material ESM is used as an electrolyte in Li cells (Li/ESM/Li and Li/ESM/LiFePO4).

Li-ion battery electrolyte context. Solubility in PC at 25 °C: 0.518 mol dm^-3 (Table 1). Conductivity for 0.10 mol dm^-3 LDFBDB: 0.414 mS cm^-1 in PC, 2.167 mS cm^-1 in PC+DME, 1.124 mS cm^-1 in PC+EMC, 0.960 mS cm^-1 in PC+EC+EMC at 298.2 K (Table 2/Fig. 3). Conductivity for PC saturated solution: 1.18 mS cm^-1 (Table 1). Electrochemical oxidation potential: ~3.7 V vs Li+/Li for 0.1 mol dm^-3 in PC at 20 °C, 9 mV s^-1 (Fig. 4). Thermal decomposition onset: 170 °C in N2 (Fig. 2).

FLDFBDB is evaluated as a Li-ion battery electrolyte salt. Table 2 reports conductivities for 0.10 mol dm-3 FLDFBDB solutions at 298.2 K: PC 1.06 mS cm-1; PC + DME 3.46 mS cm-1; PC + EC + EMC 1.18 mS cm-1; PC + EMC 1.24 mS cm-1. Electrochemical stability order includes FLDFBDB (3.9 V vs Li+/Li), confirming electrolyte application context.

FLBDOB is evaluated as a Li-ion battery electrolyte salt. Conductivity at 0.10 mol dm-3 and 298.2 K (Table 2): PC 4.58 mS cm-1; PC + DME 5.94 mS cm-1. Electrochemical stability order gives FLBDOB ~4.0 V vs Li+/Li (Section 3.5, Fig. 4/text). Additional context: in PC at 25 °C, solubility S=0.275 mol dm-3 with σ=5.60 mS cm-1 (Table 1); thermal decomposition onset 262 °C (Section 3.2).

LBOB is evaluated as a Li-ion battery electrolyte salt: (i) Thermal stability: decomposition starts at 302 °C for LBOB (Fig. 2). (ii) Conductivity: at 0.1 mol dm−3, LBOB shows 5.56 mS cm−1 in PC and 6.79 mS cm−1 in PC+DME at 298.2 K (Table 2); in saturated PC solution at 25 °C, LBOB has σ = 6.76 mS cm−1 with solubility S = 0.362 mol dm−3 (Table 1). (iii) Electrochemical stability: oxidation potential ~4.5 V vs Li+/Li for LBOB in PC (Fig. 4). These results are explicitly for LBOB among the compared organoborate salts.

AOEM_2 (3-(2-(2-methoxyethoxy)ethoxy)prop-1-ene) is only described as a synthetic intermediate (Section 2.2.1) for preparing MFSM2 and DFSM2. All reported electrolyte performance and battery testing data pertain to MFSM2/DFSM2 (Table 1; Figs. 1–4; E1–E3 formulations use DFSM2). No application or performance data are provided for AOEM_2.

MFSM2 was evaluated as a fluorosilane electrolyte solvent for Li-ion batteries. Table 1 reports for MFSM2 (with 1 M LiTFSI): σ = 1.4 mS cm-1, oxidative decomposition potential = 4.5 V vs. Li+/Li, and reductive decomposition potential = 1.0 V vs. Li+/Li. The electrochemical windows of 1 M LiTFSI in fluorosilane solutions were measured by LSV. The paper states these properties make the fluorosilanes potential electrolyte solvents for LIBs.

DFSM2 is used as an electrolyte co-solvent for Li-ion batteries. Table 1 reports for 1M LiTFSI in DFSM2: ionic conductivity 1.8 mS cm-1 at room temperature; oxidative decomposition potential 5.0 V vs Li+/Li; reductive decomposition potential 1.2 V vs Li+/Li. In LCO/graphite full cells with E3 (1M LiPF6 EC/DFSM2/EMC (2/3/5 vol) + 5 wt% FEC), capacity retention after 135 cycles at 0.5C to 4.4 V is 92.5% vs 66.8% for E0. DFSM2-only electrolyte with 1M LiPF6 does not form a protective SEI on graphite (no Li intercalation plateau), requiring EC/EMC and FEC additives. DSC shows improved thermal stability with lithiated graphite and 4.4 V-charged LixCoO2 when DFSM2 is present.

E0 is defined as a commercial carbonate electrolyte: "E0 1M LiPF6 EC/EMC (3/7 in vol.)." In LCO/graphite full cells cycled at 0.5C with 4.4 V upper cutoff, capacity retention after 135 cycles is reported as 66.8% for E0 (vs. 92.5% for E3). Coulombic efficiencies for E0 in the same test are 81.4% (first cycle) and 99.6% (135th cycle). For graphite/Li half cells, the initial coulombic efficiency with E0 is 88.8%.

E1 is defined as: "E1 1M LiPF6 EC/DFSM2/EMC (2/3/5 in vol.)". For graphite/Li half cells: "For E1 electrolyte ... the discharge and charge capacity at the first cycle is 332.4 and 455.7 mAh g−1, respectively, and the initial coulombic efficiency (IEC) is 72.9%." Additionally, cycling data state: "Using the DFSM2 based electrolyte without any additive (E1), graphite/Li cell shows a gradually increased capacity, ranging from the first 287 to 304 mAh g−1 after 50 cycles at 0.2C rate."

E2 is defined as 1M LiPF6 EC/DFSM2/EMC (2/3/5 in vol.) + 5 wt% VC. In graphite/Li half cells, E2 shows poor cycling: capacity drops from ~330 mAh_g in the first three formation cycles at 0.1C to 200 mAh_g in the first cycle at 0.2C, and further to 75 mAh_g after 10 cycles at 0.2C; initial coulombic efficiency is 78.3%.

Electrochemical stability (LSV): “The oxidation potential of 1 M LiPF6 in FEC/TTE (3:7, by vol.) is observed at ∼5.08 V” (Fig. 2a). Li+ transference number (experiment): “The calculated tLi+ in the respective electrolytes were 0.426 for 1 M LiPF6 in FEC/TTE/EMC and 0.352 for 1 M LiPF6 in FEC/TTE” (equation (10) discussion; Table 2). Phase behavior for this composition: Table 1 lists “LiPF6 in FEC/TTE … Unstable; easy to have phase separation.”

Electrochemical stability window by LSV on Li||NMC111: "The oxidation potential of 1M LiPF6 in FEC/TTE/EMC (3:5:2, by vol.) can be extended to ~5.31 V" (Fig. 2a). Li+ transference number in Li||Li cell: "The calculated tLi+ ... were 0.426 for 1M LiPF6 in FEC/TTE/EMC" (Eq. 10 and text; Table 2 compares theoretical 0.411 and experimental 0.426). Diffusion coefficients at room temperature for this electrolyte (Table 2): Li+ = 2.124×10^-6 cm2/s; PF6− = 2.998×10^-6 cm2/s, supporting improved Li+ mobility. Phase behavior: EMC addition "resolves the phase separation" and yields a stable phase.

FBCP_20 (as FBCPE_20 when doped with LiTFSI at [EO]/[Li]=20:1) is part of the three solid polymer electrolytes studied and used to assemble LiFePO4/FBCPEs/Li cells; ionic conductivity of the three FBCPEs was measured (Fig. 6a), but specific numerical performance values reported in text are for FBCPE_30 only. Therefore, no quantitative metrics for FBCP_20 can be extracted.

For FBCP_30 used as LiTFSI-doped solid polymer electrolyte (FBCPE_30, [EO]/[Li] = 20:1), the paper reports: “FBCPE_30 shows a higher ionic conductivity (2.68 × 10^-4 S cm^-1) at 70 °C than FBCPE_20 and FBCPE_40.” The Li+ transference number is given as “t_Li+ was calculated to be 0.41.” Additional performance directly tied to FBCPE_30 includes an electrochemical stability window up to 4.9 V (LSV), stable Li plating/stripping for 600 h at 0.1 mA cm^-2 and 70 °C with ~0.04 V polarization in Li/FBCPE_30/Li, and full-cell results: LiFePO4/FBCPE_30/Li delivers 157.1 mAh g^-1 at 0.2 C (70 °C) with 95.8% retention after 250 cycles; NCM622/FBCPE_30/Li shows 131.2 mAh g^-1 at 0.1 C (70 °C) with ~91.5% retention after 20 cycles.

FBCP_40 (FBCPE_40 when doped with LiTFSI) is one of the three solid polymer electrolytes studied. The paper states: “The thin film morphologies of FBCPs mixed with LiTFSI ([EO]/[Li] = 20:1) were evaluated … AFM images of … (c) FBCPE_40. TEM images … (h, i) FBCPE_40.” It also reports that “The temperature-dependent ionic conductivities of the three FBCPEs … are plotted in Arrhenius coordinates in Fig. 6a… FBCPE_30 shows a higher ionic conductivity (2.68 × 10^-4 S cm-1) at 70 °C than FBCPE_20 and FBCPE_40.” However, no explicit numerical ionic conductivity (or other electrochemical metrics) is given for FBCPE_40 in the text, tables, or figure captions; values appear only as curves in Fig. 6a. Other quantified results (tLi+, ESW, Li symmetric cycling, LiFePO4/NCM622 cell data) are reported for FBCPE_30 only. Therefore, the application is extracted as solid-state Li+ electrolyte, with numeric fields left null due to lack of explicit values for FBCP_40.

DPPA0 is the GPE without TFOA. In NCM811|DPPA0|Li cells at 3C and 30 °C, the paper states: "the cells utilizing DPPA0 GPEs exhibits a precipitous decline in capacity to approximately 105 mAh g^-1 after 300 cycles, representing 62.89% of their initial capacity." Additional DPPA0-specific performance (not mapped to the given schema fields) includes: t_Li+ = 0.31 ("the t_Li+ of DPPA3 significantly increases to 0.61, compared to 0.31 for DPPA0"), decomposition voltage 4.63 V ("which is higher than the 4.63 V of DPPA0"), and in Li|DPPA0|Li symmetric cells an initial overpotential of 87.4 mV with short circuit at ~180 h at 0.2 mA cm^-2.

DPPA1 is defined as a gel polymer electrolyte with DPPA:TFOA=1:1: "DPPA and TFOA were weighed at a 1:1 ratio... to form the polymer electrolyte named DPPA1." All TFOA-containing GPE samples (including DPPA1) showed improved ionic transport: "all samples containing TFOA demonstrated lower electrochemical impedance and higher ionic conductivity than that of the pure DPPA GPEs." These GPEs are assembled and tested in Li||Li symmetric and NCM811||Li cells, indicating use as a battery electrolyte.

DPPA2 is a TFOA-modified DPPA gel polymer electrolyte (GPE) with DPPA:TFOA = 2:1: "DPPA2, DPPA3, DPPA4 and DPPA5 were prepared with ratios of 2:1, 3:1, 4:1 and 5:1 respectively." It is part of the DPPA–TFOA GPEs designed for lithium metal batteries (used in Li||Li symmetric cells and NCM811||Li cells). For the family of TFOA-containing samples (which includes DPPA2), the paper states: "all samples containing TFOA demonstrated lower electrochemical impedance and higher ionic conductivity than that of the pure DPPA GPEs". However, specific numeric performance values (e.g., ionic conductivity, Li+ transference number, cycling stability) are only reported for DPPA3; no quantitative metrics are provided for DPPA2.

DPPA3 is a gel polymer electrolyte (DPPA:TFOA=3:1) used in Li metal batteries. Reported metrics specific to DPPA3 include: ionic conductivity 2.52×10^-3 S/cm and t_Li+ = 0.61 at 30 °C; Li|DPPA3|Li symmetric cell stable for over 2000 h at 0.2 mA cm^-2; NCM811|DPPA3|Li cell delivers a specific capacity of 101.5 mAh g^-1 at 10C. No regeneration procedure/temperature or exact t50-type lifetime is provided; the stability time is given only as “over 2000 h,” so breakthrough is left null.

DPPA4 is defined as a DPPA-TFOA GPE with DPPA:TFOA=4:1 (section 2.4). The paper states that "all samples containing TFOA demonstrated lower electrochemical impedance and higher ionic conductivity than that of the pure DPPA GPEs" (Fig. 2 description), which applies to DPPA4. However, specific quantitative performance values (ionic conductivity, tLi+, cycling, LSV, etc.) are only reported for DPPA3 (and comparisons to DPPA0), with no DPPA4-specific numeric data provided.

DPPA5 is defined as a TFOA-modified gel polymer electrolyte with DPPA:TFOA = 5:1: “DPPA2, DPPA3, DPPA4 and DPPA5 were prepared with ratios of 2:1, 3:1, 4:1 and 5:1 respectively.” The study states generally that “all samples containing TFOA demonstrated lower electrochemical impedance and higher ionic conductivity than that of the pure DPPA GPEs,” but specific numeric performance and cell data were only reported for DPPA3 (e.g., conductivity, cycling), and the authors “chose the ratio of DPPA:TFOA = 3:1 for the subsequent experiments.” No DPPA5-specific numeric application metrics are provided.

FG-GPE-0 is a gel polymer electrolyte for Li metal batteries. Reported performance includes: (1) ionic conductivity at 30 °C: 3.42×10^-4 S cm^-1 for FG-GPE-0 (Fig. 2e); (2) electrochemical stability window: 4.54 V for FG-GPE-0 (Fig. 2a).

FG-GPE-3 is a gel polymer electrolyte for lithium metal batteries. Electrochemical data for this specific sample: (1) Ionic conductivity at 30°C: “the ion conductivity (σ) of FG-GPE-0, FG-GPE-3, FG-GPE-5, and FG-GPE-7 are 3.42 × 10^-4, 2.67 × 10^-3, 4.74 × 10^-3, and 2.24 × 10^-3 S cm^-1, respectively” (Fig. 2e). (2) Li+ transference number: “The calculated tLi+ for FG-GPE-3 and FG-GPE-5 were 0.55 and 0.68, respectively (Fig. S9).” (3) Electrochemical stability window: “the ESW values for FG-GPE-0, FG-GPE-3, FG-GPE-5, and FG-GPE-7 are 4.54 V, 4.55 V, 4.64 V, and 4.71 V, respectively” (Fig. 2a–d). (4) Symmetric Li|FG-GPE-3|Li: stable cycling for 4500 h at 0.05 mA cm^-2 with ~10 mV overpotential (Fig. 4a–d); at 0.1 mA cm^-2, a sudden voltage drop occurs after 1160 h (Fig. 4e–h). (5) LFP|FG-GPE-3|Li: capacity retention 65.2% after 1800 cycles at 3C and 30°C (Fig. 5c). (6) NCM811|FG-GPE-3|Li: initial coulombic efficiency 90.66% at 0.1C (Fig. 6b); capacity retention 64.6% after 400 cycles at 3C (Fig. 6c).

At 30°C, the σ and tLi+ of FG-GPE-5 reach 4.74 × 10^-3 S cm^-1 and 0.68 (Highlights; Fig. 2e, S7, S8; “The calculated tLi+ for FG-GPE-3 and FG-GPE-5 were 0.55 and 0.68”). ESW for FG-GPE-5 is 4.64 V (Fig. 2c). In full cells using FG-GPE-5: LFP|FG-GPE-5|Li shows 76.3% capacity retention after 1800 cycles at 3C (Fig. 5c); NCM811|FG-GPE-5|Li shows 80% retention after 400 cycles at 3C and 80.5% after 200 cycles at 5C (Highlights; Fig. 6c–d). Symmetric Li|FG-GPE-5|Li maintains stable overpotential for 2000 h at 0.1 mA cm^-2 (Fig. 4e–h).

At 30 °C, the ion conductivity (σ) of FG-GPE-0, FG-GPE-3, FG-GPE-5, and FG-GPE-7 are 3.42 × 10^−4, 2.67 × 10^−3, 4.74 × 10^−3, and 2.24 × 10^−3 S cm^−1, respectively (Fig. 2e, S7, and Fig. S8). The ESW values for FG-GPE-0, FG-GPE-3, FG-GPE-5, and FG-GPE-7 are 4.54 V, 4.55 V, 4.64 V, and 4.71 V, respectively.

ILPE-EMImTFSI is used as the electrolyte in Li/ILPE/LiFePO4 cells. Reported performance: “At each current density, ILPE-EMImTFSI delivered reversible discharge capacities of 143 mAh g−1 at 0.1 C, 133 mAh g−1 at 0.5 C, and 109 mAh g−1 at 1 C.” Also, “With an initially high discharge capacity of 143 mAh g−1 … the discharge capacity … increases with stable cycle properties for up to 50 cycles.” Additional electrolyte metrics (context): ionic conductivity 5.3×10−3 S cm−1 at 30 °C; anodic stability 5.5 V vs Li.

Used as a gel polymer electrolyte/separator in a lithium-ion capacitor (LIC). Table 1 reports the MnO2@PVDF/TBAC membrane has the smallest bulk resistance (RS = 1.02 Ω) and the largest ionic conductivity of 2.95×10^-3 S/cm, with interfacial resistance Rin = 124 Ω and electrochemical stability window Es = 3.9 V. Device performance with this membrane: specific capacitance 19.5 F/g at 0.5 C (Fig. 6d), rate capability 35 F/g at 0.1 C and ~25 F/g at 0.2 C (Fig. 7b), and capacity retention 67.20% after 10,000 cycles (Fig. 7a). Thermal shrinkage at 170 °C is 7.17% (Fig. 4c), indicating superior thermal stability. These data are explicitly linked to the MnO2@PVDF/TBAC LVNM.

F-GPE was used as the electrolyte in Li||LCO and Li||Gr cells and delivered battery-relevant performance. Key data directly tied to F-GPE include: (i) oxidation stability 5.6 V (LSV), t_Li+ = 0.51 at 25 °C, and ionic conductivities of 1.2, 7.6, and 12 mS/cm at −40, 30, and 60 °C, respectively; (ii) Li||Li symmetric cells cycled stably ~60 mV overpotential for 900 h at 0.1 mA/cm^2; (iii) Li||Cu plating/stripping CE_avg = 94.36% at 0.5 mA/cm^2; (iv) LCO||F-GPE||Li coin cells showed discharge capacities of 134.9, 133.3, 126.5, 119.6, and 105.2 mAh/g at 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1, and 2 C, and achieved 89.4 mAh/g at −40 °C (0.1 C; 72.1% of RT capacity); (v) LCO||F-GPE||Gr pouch cells retained 95.3% after 200 cycles at 0.5 C and 92.9% after 400 cycles at 1 C, with 0.83 Ah at −40 °C and 1.35 Ah at 60 °C. Capacity field here records the −40 °C discharge capacity (0.1 C) for LCO||F-GPE||Li, evidencing wide-temperature applicability.

LE (1 M LiPF6 in EP/EMC/FEC, 4:1:1 w/w) was used as a standalone electrolyte benchmark. Reported metrics specific to LE include: (i) average Coulombic efficiency for Li plating/stripping in Li||LE||Cu cells of 80.62% (Fig. 3c inset); (ii) Li+ transference number t_Li+ = 0.44 (Fig. S3, Table S1); (iii) oxidative stability window up to 4.8 V by LSV (Fig. S2); (iv) in Li||Li symmetric cells with LE, the highest overpotential reached ~200 mV and the cell short-circuited after 420 h due to dendrite growth (Fig. S6c); (v) desolvation activation energy Ea = 58.466 kJ mol^-1 from R_ct analysis in Li||Li (Fig. 4e). These are directly tied to LE, whereas most other performance data were for the gel (F-GPE).

PVDF blended with [PMPyr][TFSI] was evaluated as a solid polymer electrolyte for Li+ conduction: PVDF shows σi = 5.3 × 10^-5 S cm^-1 at 20 °C and 2.5 × 10^-4 S cm^-1 at 60 °C, activation energy 14 kJ/mol, and Li+ transference number tLi+ = 0.12 (Table 4). Cyclic voltammetry for the PVDF sample in 0–5 V shows very low currents (<1e-8 A), evidencing electrochemical stability (Fig. 4c). However, solid-state batteries were assembled with all samples except PVDF due to its low lithium transference number and high crystallinity, so no discharge capacity was reported for PVDF (Section 3.4: “excepting for the sample with PVDF polymer due to the low lithium transference number and high crystallinity degree”).

P(VDF-HFP) blended with 40 wt% [PMPyrr][TFSI] was used as a solid polymer electrolyte in Li/LFP half-cells. Fig. 5a reports an initial discharge capacity of 140 mAh/g at C/10 and room temperature for the P(VDF-HFP)-based sample. Table 4 lists ionic conductivity 6.2 × 10^-5 S/cm (20 °C), 2.4 × 10^-4 S/cm (60 °C), and Li+ transference number 0.35 for P(VDF-HFP). The cyclic voltammetry indicates electrochemical stability up to ~5 V for all samples, applicable to P(VDF-HFP).

Solid-state batteries with solid polymer electrolytes based on the developed samples were produced... Fig. 5a shows the discharge capacity value for the solid-state batteries during 50 cycles at C/10 rate... An initial discharge capacity value of 146, 140 and 133 mAh / g was achieved for P(VDF-TrFE), P(VDF-HFP) and P(VDF-TrFE-CFE) based samples, respectively.

- 31VPIF/OZ is used as a solid polymer electrolyte in Li metal batteries: Li | 31VPIF/OZ | Li (symmetric) and Li | 31VPIF/OZ | LFP (full cell), also tested with NCM523.
- Ionic conduction metrics: σ(25°C) = 3.58×10^-4 S/cm; t_Li+ = 0.52 at 40°C (Fig. 4(e); text: “to ensure normal operation … σ = 3.58×10^-4 S cm^-1 … t_Li+ reaching 0.52 for 31VPIF/OZ at 40°C”).
- Electrochemical stability: electrochemical window 5.4 V at 40°C (Fig. 4(d)).
- Li|Li symmetric cell: cycled 1000 h at 0.1 mA/cm^2 and 40°C without short circuit (Fig. 5(c)); also sustained continuous cycling for 600 h at room temperature (Fig. S21).
- Li|31VPIF/OZ|LFP at 40°C: 1C, 300 cycles, charge specific capacity 154.68 → 134.38 mA_h/g (86.88% retention), Coulombic efficiency ~100% (Fig. 6(c)). Rate performance at 40°C: 0.1/0.2/0.3/0.5/1/1.5C discharge capacities = 160.0/159.4/158.9/156.8/146.4/107.4 mA_h/g; returns to 159.63 mA_h/g at 0.1C (Fig. 6(d)).
- Li|31VPIF/OZ|LFP at 25°C: rate capacities 146.9/144.8/142.4/136.1/104.0 mA_h/g at 0.1/0.2/0.3/0.5/1C; returns to 147.1 mA_h/g at 0.1C (Fig. 6(e)). Long-term cycling at 25°C: 0.5C, 600 cycles, 147.08 → 134.58 mA_h/g (91.5% retention), CE ~100% (Fig. 6(g)).
- High-voltage compatibility: Li | 31VPIF/OZ | NCM523 shows stable cycling at 40°C and 0.3C for 70 cycles (Fig. S25).
- All metrics above explicitly refer to the optimized sample 31VPIF/OZ as stated in the Results and Conclusion sections.

TFF electrolyte is explicitly evaluated as a non-flammable electrolyte: “TFF does not burn after ignition with a SET value of 0 s g^-1” (self-extinguish test, Fig. 1b, Table S1). Additional performance with TFF in cells: oxidative current ~0 μA until 5.6 V in LSV; ~0 μA leakage current for 15 h at 4.4–4.6 V (Fig. 2a,b); LCO||Li achieves ~100 mA h g^-1 at 20C, and 141 mA h g^-1 at 0.5C after 300 cycles with 76% retention; at 5C, 74.9% capacity retention after 400 cycles (Fig. 2g,h, Fig. S9); Li||Li lifespan >2200 h at 0.5 mA cm^-2, 1 mA h cm^-2 (Fig. 4f); LCO||Gr pouch cell: 0.87 Ah with 95.5% retention after 150 cycles at 4.5 V (Fig. 5c). These data are reported for the TFF electrolyte (TEP–FEMC–FEC).

The paper explicitly prepares a TEP electrolyte as a comparator: "a TEP electrolyte of 1.5 mmol LiFSI dissolving in 1 mL TEP was prepared for comparison" (Section 2.1). It is used in lithium battery tests (e.g., Li||Gr and LCO||Gr), but the main text provides only qualitative observations for LiFSI-TEP, such as solvation structure similarity to LB-063 (Fig. 1e; "similar to LiFSI-TEP") and that graphite becomes cracked for LiFSI-TEP (Fig. S21), without reporting numeric electrochemical performance values for the TEP electrolyte. Therefore, no capacity/selectivity/breakthrough/regeneration numbers specific to the TEP electrolyte are extractable from the provided text.

LB-063 electrolyte is defined as a commercial carbonate electrolyte: 1 mmol LiPF6 in 1 mL EC/DMC (30/70 vol%) (Section 2.1). In Results, the commercial carbonate electrolyte (abbreviated as LB-063) shows a self-extinguish test (SET) value of 22.77 s g^-1 and continuous burning (Fig. 1b). Electrochemical stability: LB-063 exhibits obvious oxidative current starting at ~4.0 V in LSV (Fig. 2a), and increasing leakage current during 4.4–4.6 V holds (Fig. 2b). Rate/cycling: at 20 C the counterpart (LB-063) exhibits almost no capacity (Fig. 2g). Full-cell performance (LCO || Gr): after 200 cycles under 4.5 V, LB-063 delivers 18.92 mA h g^-1 with 13.42% retention (Fig. 5b). Symmetric Li||Li lifespan: LB-063 sustains only ~900 h at 0.5 mA cm^-2, 1 mA h cm^-2 (Fig. 4f). Fast-charging safety: temperature rises to 44.6 °C for LB-063 at 2 A to 4.5 V (Fig. 5g). These results support extracting the capacity metric 18.92 mA h g^-1 for LB-063 in LCO||Gr cells.

LLZTO 6 h is used as an active filler in ionogel electrolytes for lithium‑metal batteries. For IGs containing 15% LLZTO 6 h, the paper reports: (i) highest ionic conductivity among tested IGs; conclusions specify >0.4 mS cm^-1 at 20°C and >1 mS cm^-1 at 40°C. (ii) Oxidative stability (ESW) up to 5.27 V vs. Li+/Li. (iii) Stable Li|IG|Li stripping/plating with ~100 mV overpotential maintained for 600 h at 0.1 mA cm^-2. (iv) In Li|IG|LFP cells, specific discharge capacity 162 mA h g^-1 after 100 cycles, capacity retention 99.3% after 200 cycles, and average Coulombic efficiency 99.93%. These performance metrics are explicitly reported for the formulation with LLZTO 6 h (15 wt% relative to the polymer matrix).

Electrochemical stability window: "The insertion of the inorganic filler increased the oxidation potential from 4.65 to 4.98 V for 15% LLZTO 15 h and 24 h, while 15% LLZTO 6 h was stable up to 5.27 V." Cycling: "15% LLZTO 15 h displayed a large capacity retention of 83.3% after 200 cycles." Tests were conducted in Li|IG|SS for ESW and Li|IG|LFP cells (0.2C at 40 °C after formation) for cycling.

LLZTO 24 h is a silane-modified LLZTO nanoparticle used as an active filler in composite ionogel electrolytes for lithium‑metal batteries. In Li|IG|SS LSV, the insertion of the inorganic filler increased the oxidation potential to 4.98 V for 15% LLZTO 24 h (measured at 5 μA cm^-2), whereas 15% LLZTO 6 h reached 5.27 V (Fig. 5b). In symmetric Li|IG|Li stripping/plating, 'no filler' and 15% LLZTO 24 h show a rising overpotential at 0.05 mA cm^-2, and noisy voltage profiles at 0.1 mA cm^-2, indicating sluggish Li+ transport and uneven Li deposition (Fig. 6). EIS after cycling shows significant reductions of Rb and Rct for 15% LLZTO 15 h and 24 h, consistent with mossy lithium and possible soft short circuits (Fig. 7). In Li|IG|LFP cells, 15% LLZTO 24 h 'showed a rather stable cycling with a lower capacity delivered' (Fig. 8), but no explicit numeric capacity is reported for the 24 h sample. The ionic conductivity for 15% LLZTO 24 h is reduced compared to pristine LLZTO-based IGs, attributed to the thicker silane layer and lithium leaching; no explicit conductivity values are provided for 24 h.

application.target_species: EMITFSI-based precursor solution (12 wt% C1-4TFSI, 0.1 wt% AIBN in EMITFSI-based electrolyte)

parse.why: PDDATFSI porous membrane is used as the separator in coin cells: “...LiFePO4 as the cathode, Li foil as the anode and the PDDATFSI porous membrane as the separator.” The membrane shows high uptake of the precursor solution: “Such high porosity and superior wettability provide the PDDATFSI porous membrane with a higher uptake ability for the precursor solution (251.2%, Fig. S5d).” Supporting properties tied to this role include porosity 57.5% and contact angle 5.3° for the precursor on PDDATFSI.

Li-HPILSE is used as a solid electrolyte in Li-ion batteries (e.g., LiFePO4/Li-HPILSE/Li cells). Direct performance: “the prepared Li-HPILSE delivers a satisfied conductivity of 1.06×10^-3 Scm^-1 at 25 °C.” Electrochemical stability up to 4.4 V vs Li/Li+ is also reported.

Na-HPILSE was used as the solid electrolyte in Na0.9[Cu0.22Fe0.30Mn0.48]O2/Na-HPILSE/Na cells. The cell delivered specific reversible capacities of 101.0, 93.4, 82.5, 70.5 and 59.9 mAh g^-1 at 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.5 and 1 C, respectively, and recovered 95.9% of the original capacity when the current was switched back to 0.05 C. After 100 cycles at 0.1 C, the reversible capacity was 85.6 mAh g^-1 with 85.5% retention and near 100% coulombic efficiency. Additional Na-HPILSE properties: ionic conductivity 1.15×10^-3 S cm^-1 at 25 °C, Na+ transference number 0.22, electrochemical stability up to 4.6 V vs. Na/Na+, and stable Na/Na-HPILSE/Na symmetric cycling over 300 h at 0.1 mA cm^-2.

G2 is the second-generation commercial electrolyte used as a benchmark. In Li||LFP batteries: “after the first activation cycle at 0.1C… 154.8 mAh·g−1 (CE of 93.7%) for G2 electrolyte… After three active cycles, the discharge specific capacity of G2 electrolyte decays gradually, maintaining only 80.8% of its initial capacity and average CE of 98.2% after 100 cycles” (Fig. 10). Rate performance recovery: “compared with the specific capacity restoration of 92.5% for G2 electrolyte” when current returned from 5C to 0.2C (Fig. 12). Oxidative stability by LSV: “the potential … is much higher than the potential of G2 electrolyte (3.5 V)” (Fig. 2). In Li||Cu half-cells at 0.5 mA·cm−2, 1.0 mAh·cm−2: “G2 electrolyte at 86.7% [initial CE]… average CE of 91.2% within 80 cycles” and polarization voltage grew from “59 mV (10th) to 89 mV (50th)” (Fig. 3, Fig. 4). In Li||Li symmetric cells: at 0.5 mA·cm−2, polarization with G2 rises to 280 mV after 430 h and interfacial impedance to 22 Ω; at 1.0 mA·cm−2, “cycle life of G2 electrolyte drops to about 140 h,” polarization reaches 613 mV at 180 h before short circuit (Fig. 6). These results reflect G2’s application as a battery electrolyte; the capacity value reported above is taken from the Li||LFP cell measurement.

Li||Cu half-cells at 0.5 mA·cm^-2 and 1.0 mAh·cm^-2: “the initial CE of the F1 electrolyte is only 61.7% and the CE gradually decreases after 10 cycles…” (Fig. 3 text). Other potential performance metrics (oxidative limit, ionic conductivity, overpotential) are either qualitative for F1 or given only in curves without explicit F1 values, so omitted.

Oxidative stability: "although binary electrolyte F4 containing TMS and EMC demonstrates a lower oxidation potential, it can still be used as an electrolyte for 5 V high-voltage lithium metal batteries." Li||Cu cycling: "Since both TMS and EMC are unable to form a stable SEI film on the surface of lithium metal anode, there is an obvious 'diving' phenomenon for the CE of F4 electrolyte." Li||Li symmetric cell at 0.5 mA cm^-2: "F4 electrolyte demonstrates the shortest cycle life... the polarization voltage of F4 electrolyte increased significantly, up to the cut-off voltage at <50 h... with corresponding values of 125 Ω and 87.3 Ω at 1st cycle and 5th cycle." At 1.0 mA cm^-2: "the polarization voltage of F4 electrolyte dramatically increased to 1.4 V within 100 h." SEI composition evidence: Table 2 lists F for F4 as 7.07 at.% and text notes LiF proportion for F4 as 77.2% (from F 1s peak area ratio).

For F7: In Li||LFP cells, the initial discharge specific capacity is 155.7 mAh·g^-1 (CE 95.9%) at 0.1C, and after 100 cycles at 0.5C the capacity retention is 94.3% with an average CE of 99.8% (Fig. 10, Section 3.5). In Li||Cu half-cells at 0.5 mA·cm^-2 and 1.0 mAh·cm^-2, F7 shows an average CE of 95.9% over 130 cycles (>500 h) and an initial CE of 91.6%; at 1.0 mA·cm^-2 it cycles stably for 100 cycles with average CE 93.5% (Section 3.3). Polarization for F7 is 33 mV (10th) and 43 mV (50th) at 0.5 mA·cm^-2 and 1.0 mAh·cm^-2 (Fig. 4). In Li||Li symmetric cells, F7 maintains >400 h at 0.5 mA·cm^-2 and about 480 h at 1.0 mA·cm^-2 with stable ~60 mV polarization and low interfacial impedance (Fig. 6, Section 3.4).

The copolymer crosslinked-P(VEC-co-TFEMA) is used as the solid polymer electrolyte host (FPCSPE). FPCSPE 3-40 exhibited σLi+ = 5.02×10^-5 S/cm and tLi+ = 0.44 at room temperature, and an oxidation stability up to 5.7 V (Fig. 3e–f; Fig. 4a–b). In Li||FPCSPE 3-40||NCM811 cells (3.0–4.5 V, 25 °C), the battery delivered a reversible specific capacity of 218 mAh g^-1 at 0.1 C with ~99.6% coulombic efficiency and ~70% capacity retention after 300 cycles (Fig. 5d–e; Conclusion). A Li||FPCSPE 3-40||Li symmetric cell cycled >300 h at 0.1 mA/cm^2 with ~0.05 V overpotential (Fig. 4c). These results directly evidence the application of crosslinked-P(VEC-co-TFEMA) as a solid polymer electrolyte for Li+ conduction in lithium metal batteries.

DSLE2 is defined as a dual‑salt liquid electrolyte (LiTFSI:LiDFOB = 10:2): “The LiTFSI:LiDFOB molar ratio was set at 10:2, 8:4, 6:6, 4:8, and 2:10 for DSLE2, DSLE4, DSLE6, DSLE8, and DSLE10, respectively…” The paper reports only DSLE‑wide trends (e.g., “DSLEs and LiDFOB/FEC had significantly improved electrochemical windows (4.57–4.74 V)… DSLE4 had the best anodic stability… Therefore, DSLE4 was used for further investigation.”) and provides no DSLE2‑specific performance numbers or cell tests. Hence no quantitative metrics can be extracted for DSLE2.

DSLE4 is a dual-salt liquid electrolyte (LiTFSI:LiDFOB = 8:4, total salt concentration 1.2 M in FEC). It exhibited an anodic stability up to 4.74 V (LSV), higher than LE (3.97 V) and LiTFSI/FEC (4.23 V). In Li|Al tests (3.0–4.5 V), Al corroded with LiTFSI/FEC but remained intact with DSLE4. In Li|Cu half cells, DSLE4 achieved a CE of 95.18%. Symmetric Li|DSLE4|Li cells short-circuited after 938 h of cycling. In Li|LCO full cells (3.0–4.5 V, 1 C), DSLE4 delivered initial and average CEs of 91.97% and 99.45%, with 73% capacity retention after 300 cycles. The lithium-ion transference number for DSLE4 was 0.52.

DSLE6 is defined as a dual-salt liquid electrolyte with LiTFSI:LiDFOB = 6:6 at a total salt concentration of 1.2 M in FEC: "The LiTFSI:LiDFOB molar ratio was set at 10:2, 8:4, 6:6, 4:8, and 2:10 for DSLE2, DSLE4, DSLE6, DSLE8, and DSLE10, respectively, and the total salt concentration was maintained at 1.2 M". All DSLEs (including DSLE6) showed improved anodic stability relative to LE and LiTFSI/FEC: "DSLEs and LiDFOB/FEC had significantly improved electrochemical windows (4.57 - 4.74 V) compared to LE (3.97 V) and LiTFSI/FEC (4.23 V)." Specific performance metrics (e.g., CE, cycling stability, conductivity values) were reported for DSLE4 and AFPEs, but not individually for DSLE6. Therefore, no DSLE6-specific numeric performance could be extracted beyond its role as a Li-ion battery electrolyte.

DSLE8 is defined as a dual‑salt liquid electrolyte with LiTFSI:LiDFOB = 4:8 at 1.2 M total salt: "The LiTFSI:LiDFOB molar ratio was set at 10:2, 8:4, 6:6, 4:8, and 2:10 for DSLE2, DSLE4, DSLE6, DSLE8, and DSLE10, respectively, and the total salt concentration was maintained at 1.2 M…" The study reports general trends for the DSLE series (ionic conductivity decreases with increasing LiDFOB; electrochemical windows 4.57–4.74 V for DSLEs) but gives specific performance metrics only for DSLE4 and AFPE10: "The ionic conductivities of DSLEs were lower than that of LiTFSI/FEC and decreased with increasing LiDFOB content. … DSLEs … had significantly improved electrochemical windows (4.57–4.74 V)… DSLE4 had the best anodic stability (up to 4.74 V)." No DSLE8‑specific device performance (CE, cycling, energy density) is provided. Therefore, numeric fields are left null.

DSLE10 is defined as a dual-salt liquid electrolyte with LiTFSI:LiDFOB = 2:10: 'The LiTFSI:LiDFOB molar ratio was set at 10:2, 8:4, 6:6, 4:8, and 2:10 for DSLE2, DSLE4, DSLE6, DSLE8, and DSLE10, respectively'. The DSLEs as a group showed improved anodic stability: 'DSLEs and LiDFOB/FEC had significantly improved electrochemical windows (4.57 - 4.74 V) compared to LE (3.97 V) and LiTFSI/FEC (4.23 V)'. However, specific quantitative battery performance metrics (e.g., CE, capacity, energy density) were only reported for DSLE4 and AFPE10, not for DSLE10, so numeric fields are left null.

AFPE5 is one of three AFPEs formulated for use as electrolytes in Li-metal batteries. The text reports only its electrochemical window and ionic conductivity, without any cell-level performance. Relevant evidence: "Three AFPEs were prepared with 5, 10, and 20 vol.% ... denoted as AFPE5, AFPE10, and AFPE20, respectively." and "LSV tests were carried out ... AFPE5, AFPE10, and AFPE20 were stable up to 4.76 V, 4.89 V, and 4.95 V, respectively." and "EIS tests showed the AFPEs' ionic conductivities ... achieving 2.12 mS cm^-1, 1.88 mS cm^-1, and 1.31 mS cm^-1 for AFPE5, AFPE10, and AFPE20, respectively." It is also stated that "AFPE10, which balanced ionic conductivity and electrochemical stability, was used for further investigation." Thus, no battery cycling/CE/energy-density data are provided for AFPE5 specifically.

AFPE10 was used as electrolyte in Li metal batteries with LCO cathode. Rate performance (3 mg cm^-2 LCO): 194, 189, 182, 161, 142 mAh g^-1 at 0.2, 0.5, 1, 3, 5 C; 92% capacity retention after 500 cycles at 1 C with average CE 99.82% (cut-off 4.5 V). High-loading LCO (14 mg cm^-2) with 50 um Li: 174 mAh g^-1 at 0.5 C and 507 Wh kg^-1 energy density, 80% retention after 200 cycles (avg CE 99.83%). At 25 mg cm^-2, 546 Wh kg^-1 and 82% retention after 80 cycles. Li|Cu half-cell CE = 98.88%. Li|AFPE10|Li symmetric cell cycle life >1600 h. AFPE10 electrochemical window up to 4.89 V and ionic conductivity 1.88 mS cm^-1; t_Li+ = 0.61. A ~50 mAh Li|AFPE10|LCO pouch cell delivered 52.7 mAh initially, 95.57% initial CE, and 94% capacity retention after 50 cycles.

AFPE20 is one of the all-fluorinated polymer electrolytes (AFPE5/10/20). Reported for AFPE20 specifically: electrochemical stability up to 4.95 V (Fig. 1d: “AFPE5, AFPE10, and AFPE20 were stable up to 4.76 V, 4.89 V, and 4.95 V, respectively.”) and ionic conductivity 1.31 mS cm^-1 (Fig. 1e-f: “…achieving 2.12, 1.88, and 1.31 mS cm^-1 for AFPE5, AFPE10, and AFPE20, respectively.”). However, all battery cycling and CE performance were conducted with AFPE10; no direct LMB performance metrics are provided for AFPE20. Therefore scenario and species are noted, but capacity/selectivity/breakthrough/regeneration fields have no applicable data for AFPE20.

For c-GPE-50 used as the electrolyte in a Li||LFP battery, the cell "achieves a high capacity of 134 mAh g^-1 and an ultra-long lifespan of 2000 cycles with an excellent capacity retention of 78% at a high rate of 2 C." The study also reports high-voltage performance with Li||NMC622 (160 mAh g^-1 at 0.5 C; 80% retention after 300 cycles), supporting the role of c-GPE-50 as a battery electrolyte.

F-HV is used as a polymer electrolyte in Li metal batteries. It exhibits ionic conductivity of 1.97 mS cm^-1 at 30 °C and 4.64 mS cm^-1 at 90 °C (Fig. 2c). Electrochemical stability window is 4.9 V (Fig. 2b). Li||Li symmetric cells with F-HV show ~128 mV overpotential and 1200 h cycling (Fig. 3a). Li||LFP cells with F-HV at 1 C: 144.0 mAh g^-1 initial, 86.3% retention after 3000 cycles, avg CE 99.9% (Fig. 4a); at 2 C: 88.7% retention after 1500 cycles (Fig. 4b); high-rate capacities up to 132.8 mAh g^-1 at 5 C (Fig. 4c); at 60 °C: 156.6 mAh g^-1 initial, 81.3% retention after 300 cycles (Fig. 4d). Li||NCM811 at 4.3 V: 178.4 mAh g^-1 initial, 82.1% retention after 200 cycles, avg CE 99.5% (Fig. 5a); rate: 200.9→99.6 mAh g^-1 from 0.1→3 C (Fig. 5b,d). At 4.5 V: 186.3 mAh g^-1 initial, 82.3% retention after 150 cycles, avg CE 99.1% (Fig. 5c). At 60 °C: 197.3 mAh g^-1 initial, 88.4% retention after 50 cycles (Fig. 5e). Safety-related: self-extinguishing time 0 s g^-1 (Fig. 2e) and dimensional stability without shrinkage up to 200 °C (Fig. 2f).

PL is used as a solid-state electrolyte benchmark. Reported metrics for PL: ionic conductivity at 25°C is 0.5 mScm−1 (Figure 2c: “The ionic conductivities at 25°C for PL, PLL-15, and PLL-25 are 0.5, 1.2, and 0.6 mScm−1, respectively.”). Electrochemical stability window is 4.78 V (Figure 2e: “The electrochemically stable voltages of the PL, PLL-15, and PLL-25 are 4.78, 5.09, and 4.96 V, respectively.”). Critical current density is 0.65 mA cm−2 (Figure 2g: “The CCD values of PL, PLL-15, and PLL-25 are 0.65, 0.80, and 0.45 mA cm−2, respectively.”). Li||Li symmetric cells with PL show unstable voltage and rapid failure at 0.1 mA cm−2 (Figure 2h: “Li symmetric cells with PL and PLL-25 exhibit unstable voltage over the cycling period and experience rapid failure.”). In LFP||Li full cells, PL shows R_ohm = 114 Ω and R_p = 437 Ω (Figure 3a), rate/capacity examples: 1C = 125.3 mAh g−1; at 2C and 3C capacities drop to 74.0 and 48.1 mAh g−1 (Figure 3b), steady-state capacity ~128.7 mAh g−1 and capacity retention 81.5% at 1C cycling (Figure 3e).

“It is noteworthy that the PLL slurry without NH4HCO3 addition (PLL-0) suffers from serious gelation and fails to derive a flat and smooth film… Therefore, further investigations about PLL-0 will not be conducted in the following analysis.” No electrochemical/battery performance data were reported for PLL-0.

PLL-15 is used as a composite solid electrolyte in solid-state Li batteries. Reported metrics directly for PLL-15 include: room-temperature ionic conductivity 1.2 mS cm−1 at 25°C (“The ionic conductivities at 25°C for PL, PLL-15, and PLL-25 are 0.5, 1.2, and 0.6 mS cm−1, respectively.”). Electrochemical stability window 5.09 V (“The electrochemically stable voltages of the PL, PLL-15, and PLL-25 are 4.78, 5.09, and 4.96 V, respectively.”). Critical current density 0.80 mA cm−2 (“The CCD values of PL, PLL-15, and PLL-25 are 0.65, 0.80, and 0.45 mA cm−2, respectively.”). Li||Li symmetric cell stable for ~800 h at 0.1 mA cm−2, 25°C (“the PLL-15 demonstrates stable operation for up to 800 h at 0.1 mA cm−2 at 25°C”). Li+ transference number 0.54 (“the Li+ transference number (tLi+) of the PLL-15 … is 0.54”). In LFP||Li full cells with PLL-15: rate capacities 155.7/148.2/134.0/109.0/81.9 mAh g−1 at 0.2/0.5/1/2/3 C and capacity retention 95.4% after 240 cycles at 1 C, 25°C. These confirm PLL-15’s role and performance as a Li+ solid-state electrolyte.

PLL-25 is evaluated as a composite solid electrolyte in solid-state Li batteries. Ionic conductivity at 25 °C is 0.6 mScm^{-1} (Figure 2c); activation energy Ea = 0.32 eV (Figure 2d). Electrochemical stability window is 4.96 V (Figure 2e). Critical current density (Li||Li) is 0.45 mAcm^{-2} (Figure 2g); Li||Li cells with PLL-25 show unstable voltage and rapid failure at 0.1 mAcm^{-2} (Figure 2h). In LFP||Li full cells at 25 °C, R_ohm = 83 Ω and R_p = 486 Ω (Figure 3a). Rate capacities for LFP||PLL-25||Li: 1C = 116.9 mAhg^{-1}, 2C = 89.0 mAhg^{-1}, 3C = 59.8 mAhg^{-1} (Figure 3b). Long-term cycling at 1C: steady-state capacity 110.2 mAhg^{-1} and capacity retention 75.6% (Figure 3e).

E-FPAESI core waveguides at 1.55 μm showed propagation losses less than 0.42 dB/cm (TE) and 0.40 dB/cm (TM) (Figure 5) and the Conclusions state the optical loss was measured to be less than 0.40 dB/cm, indicating suitability as a core material for optical waveguide devices.

No direct application performance was reported for FPAESI itself. Optical waveguide device fabrication and propagation loss (<0.42 dB/cm TE, <0.40 dB/cm TM at 1.55 μm) were measured only for E-FPAESI films: “To fabricate single-mode optical waveguides… E-FPAESI film was… The propagation losses… are less than 0.42 and 0.40 dB/cm… This result indicated that the E-FPAESI is a good candidate as a core material.” FPAESI data provided are synthetic and thermal (e.g., Tg, TGA) rather than application performance.

HBPS-(PTFEMA-b-PPEGMA)27/LiTFSI solid polymer electrolyte shows “The highest room temperature ionic conductivity of 2.4×10^-5 S cm^-1 … when the molar ratio of [EO] to [Li] is 30.” It also exhibits an electrochemical window of 4.9 V vs. Li/Li+ at 25 °C and tLi+ = 0.26 at 60 °C, and enables a Li/SPE/LiFePO4 cell delivering 147 mAh g^-1 at 0.1 C and 60 °C.

FPE-6 is the optimized fluorinated polymer electrolyte for Li metal batteries. Reported room-temperature metrics include ionic conductivity 2.96×10^-4 S/cm, lithium-ion transference number 0.46, and electrochemical stability window 5.0 V. In symmetric Li/FPE-6/Li cells it shows the lowest interfacial resistance (426.9 Ω) among the series and stable cycling over 1000 h with nearly constant polarization; the Li stripping/plating overpotential reaches a minimum of 92 mV at 0.1 mA/cm^2. In full cells, LiFePO4/FPE-6/Li gives 154.2 mAh/g (0.1C, first cycle), retains 86.5% capacity with ~99.9% coulombic efficiency after 424 cycles (~1350 h) at 0.5C, and shows rate capacities of 149.3→103.9 mAh/g from 0.2C→3C with 99.13% recovery at 0.2C. LiFePO4/FPE-6/Li@C achieves ~100% CE with 80.0% and 74.9% capacity retention after 1787 and 2589 cycles at 1C over ~2900 h.

PE-6 is the reference non-fluorinated polymer electrolyte used as the electrolyte in Li/PE-6/Li symmetric cells and LiFePO4/PE-6/Li full cells. Quantitative data given for PE-6 include a lithium-ion transference number of 0.28 (Fig. S18). The Li/PE-6/Li cell exhibited unstable stripping/plating and increasing polarization voltage above ~475 h, and LiFePO4/PE-6/Li showed inferior rate/cycling versus FPE-6, but no explicit numeric capacities/retentions for PE-6 are reported in the main text.

FPDE served as the electrolyte in Li/FPDE/LFP full cells, giving an initial discharge specific capacity of 150.6 mAh/g at 0.2C (96.7% initial Coulombic efficiency) and retaining 121.1 mAh/g after 600 cycles (80.4% retention). Additional FPDE metrics directly reported include ionic conductivity 0.97 mS/cm at room temperature, Li+ transference number 0.58, electrochemical stability window up to 4.8 V, stable Li/FPDE/Li symmetric cycling for 6000 h, critical current density 1 mA/cm^2, and nonflammability (SET 0 s g^-1; did not burn after >10 s flame exposure).

PVDF (Dense) refers to the solution-cast dense PVDF membrane used as a gel polymer electrolyte (GPE) matrix soaked with 1 M LiClO4/PC. Results & Discussion: “The ion conductivities of the GPEs for membranes with dense, finger-like, and honeycomb-like morphology structures were calculated to be 0.1 mS cm^-1, 1.7 mS cm^-1 and 0.9 mS cm^-1, respectively.” Table I lists PVDF (Dense) with porosity 0% and electrolyte uptake 27%, confirming the dense sample identity and test context.

PVDF (LIPS) refers to the PVDF membrane prepared by liquid-induced phase inversion (finger-like morphology) used as a GPE after soaking in 1 M LiClO4/PC. Evidence: (i) Ionic conductivity for the GPEs: “The ion conductivities of the GPEs for membranes with dense, finger-like, and honeycomb-like morphology structures were calculated to be 0.1 mS cm−1, 1.7 mS cm−1 and 0.9 mS cm−1, respectively.” (ii) Electrochemical stability: “GPE with a honeycomb-like cellular structure exhibited anodic stability up to 5.0 V versus Li+/Li, while the GPE with finger-like morphology anodic stability was as high as 4.4 V.” (iii) Cell discharge capacity (LiMn2O4/GPE/Li) at 1C for the finger-like (LIPS) sample: “The cell assembled with this GPE [VIPS] maintained a considerable discharge capacity of 75 mA h g−1 at 1C, higher than the GPE with a finger-like morphology structure (26 mA h g−1).” (iv) Supporting morphology/uptake for the LIPS sample: Table I lists PVDF (LIPS) porosity 53% and electrolyte uptake 490%.

PVDF (VIPS) refers to the PVDF gel polymer electrolyte prepared via the vapor-induced phase inversion method with a honeycomb-like cellular morphology. Reported for this specific GPE: (1) Ionic conductivity: “The ion conductivities of the GPEs for membranes with dense, finger-like, and honeycomb-like morphology structures were calculated to be 0.1 mS cm−1, 1.7 mS cm−1 and 0.9 mS cm−1, respectively.” (2) Electrochemical stability window: “GPE with a honeycomb-like cellular structure exhibited anodic stability up to 5.0 V versus Li+/Li.” (3) Rate performance in Li/LiMn2O4 cells: “the PVDF GPE with cellular morphology (VIPS) showed stable reversible capacities (158 mA h g−1, 140 mA h g−1, 120 mA h g−1, and 75 mA h g−1)” at 0.1C, 0.2C, 0.5C, and 1C, respectively. (4) Additional properties from Table I for PVDF (VIPS): porosity 34% and electrolyte uptake 231%.

For the PVDF/PVDF-g-PSSA (50/50) membrane, Table I reports porosity 37% and electrolyte uptake 371%. Figure 5A (Nyquist of SS/GPE/SS at 25 °C, 1 M LiClO4/PC) shows ionic conductivity of 2.8 mS cm^-1 for the 50/50 composition. Other electrochemical metrics (e.g., t_Li+, stability window, cell capacities) were presented for 70/30, not explicitly for 50/50, so are not assigned here.

Pure PFPE (cross-linked PFPE control with LiTFSI) is evaluated as a solid polymer electrolyte for Li+ transport. The paper reports a conductivity of 1.55 × 10^-11 S/cm at 60 °C for pure PFPE, contrasted with 2.26 × 10^-5 S/cm for the blend (Conclusions: “conductivity as compared to pure PFPE from 1.55 × 10^-11 to 2.26 × 10^-5 S/cm at 60 °C”). Additional context for the PFPE control includes Li+ transport metrics and electrochemical testing: ^7Li T1 ≈ 3 s for pure PFPE (Figure 4a) indicating low Li+ mobility, and Li|Li symmetric cells with PFPE r=0.05 did not cycle, shorting immediately due to poor interfaces (Figure 6a and text). These support the assignment of the application as a Li+ solid polymer electrolyte and the extracted conductivity value for pure PFPE.

FPCE is used as the solid polymer electrolyte in Li metal batteries and full cells. Reported performance metrics directly tied to FPCE include: ionic conductivity 0.33 mS cm^-1 at -10 °C and 1.13 mS cm^-1 at 30 °C (Figures 3a, S17); Li-ion transference number t_Li+ = 0.61 (Figure 3b). Additional FPCE-based cell results: Li|FPCE|Cu Coulombic efficiency remained 98.25% after 300 cycles (Figure 3e); Li|FPCE|Li symmetric cell operated >1100 h with smooth voltage (Figure 3f); LFP|FPCE|Li delivered stable cycling for 5000 cycles at 10 C with an average capacity decay rate of 0.00448% per cycle (Figure 4d); NCM811|FPCE|Li retained 98.62% capacity after 100 cycles at 1 C between 2.8–4.5 V (Figure 4f). Pouch cells (NCM811|FPCE|Gr, 1 Ah) showed 0.78 Ah initial and 0.62 Ah after 600 cycles at RT (Figure 5b); high-temp discharge capacities of 166.8 mAh g^-1 at 80 °C and 159.8 mAh g^-1 at 60 °C, returning to 143.9 mAh g^-1 at RT (Figure 5c); retained 0.64 Ah (73.8%) after 400 cycles at 60 °C (Figure 5d); low-temp discharge capacity 104.5 mAh g^-1 at -10 °C (Figure 5e).

P(VEC-co-TFEMA) as a cross-linked solid polymer electrolyte (FPCSPE) for Li metal batteries: FPCSPE 3-40 exhibited ionic conductivity σLi+ = 5.02×10^-5 Scm^-1 at room temperature (Fig. 3e; Conclusion). The Li-ion transference number tLi+ ≈ 0.44 at room temperature (Fig. 3f). Electrochemical stability window extends to ~5.7 V vs Li/Li+ (Fig. 4a,b). Li||FPCSPE 3-40||Li symmetric cell showed ~0.05 V overpotential at 0.1 mA cm^-2 and stable cycling for ~300 h without short circuit (Fig. 4c). In NCM811||FPCSPE 3-40||Li cells at 25°C: specific capacities 218/208/173/129 mAh g^-1 at 0.1/0.2/0.5/1 C, and ~70% capacity retention after 300 cycles at 0.1 C with ~99.6% coulombic efficiency (Fig. 5d,e). Pouch cell delivered ~214 mAh g^-1 (3rd cycle) with >93% retention after 20 cycles (Fig. 6).

As an all-solid-state polymer electrolyte, P-co-F-SN-NaTFSI shows: (1) ionic conductivity: 'The ionic conductivity of P-co-F-SN-NaTFSI was calculated to be 2.23 × 10−4 S cm−1' (Fig. 3a); (2) Na+ transference number: 'the introduction of SN elevates the t_Na+ for P-co-F-SN-NaTFSI to 0.7.' It enables Na//Na symmetric cells to cycle stably for 4690 h with ~230 mV polarization and has ESW around 5.55 V (Fig. 3f), confirming its role as a solid electrolyte.

The material P-co-F (1:2)-SN-NaTFSI is explicitly prepared as a solid electrolyte film: “During the preparation of solid electrolyte film, the molar ratios of PEGMEA to TFEMA (namely, 1:2 and 2:1) were adjusted, which are designated as P-co-F (1:2)-SN-NaTFSI…” (Section 2.2). Its application as an all-solid-state sodium polymer electrolyte is evaluated via EIS in coin cells (Section 2.4). In Results, it is specifically stated that at a PEGMEA:TFEMA ratio of 1:2, the insulated TFEMA increased impedance, inhibiting Na+ movement: “Specifically, when the ratio of PEGMEA to TFEMA was 1:2, the insulated TFEMA increased the impedance, inhibiting the movement of sodium ions” (Fig. 3a discussion). However, quantitative metrics (ionic conductivity, ESW, Na//Na cycling hours, full-cell capacities) are reported for P-co-F-SN-NaTFSI generally or for the optimal 1:1 ratio, not for the 1:2 sample; thus no numerical values can be assigned to this specific material.

The sample P-co-F (2:1)-SN-NaTFSI is explicitly defined as one of the electrolyte formulations: “the molar ratios of PEGMEA to TFEMA (namely, 1:2 and 2:1) were adjusted, which are designated as P-co-F (1:2)-SN-NaTFSI and P-co-F (2:1)-SN-NaTFSI, respectively.” (Section 2.2). Electrochemical measurements and battery tests are for P-co-F-SN-NaTFSI electrolytes in Na//Na and NVP/…/Na cells (Sections 2.4, 3.2–3.4), but the text identifies 1:1 as the optimal ratio and reports performance values (e.g., σ = 2.23×10^-4 S cm^-1) for the general/optimal (1:1) case rather than the 2:1 sample: “Thus, the optimal ratio of PEGMEA to TFEMA was determined to be 1:1… The ionic conductivity of P-co-F-SN-NaTFSI was calculated to be 2.23×10^-4 S cm^-1.” (3.2). No specific numeric performance is provided for the 2:1 formulation; therefore, only its application as a Na+ solid polymer electrolyte can be extracted.

PEGMEA-NaTFSI was prepared as a control electrolyte film ("As a control experiment, PEGMEA-NaTFSI, PEGMEA-SN-NaTFSI, and P-co-F-NaTFSI films were prepared..."). Its electrochemical stability window (ESW) was reported as 4.53 V ("The ESW of the P-co-F-NaTFSI electrolyte surpasses 5.62 V, in contrast to the 4.53 V observed for PEGMEA-NaTFSI"). The sodium-ion transference number for P-co-F-NaTFSI is 0.41, noted as twofold higher than that of PEGMEA-NaTFSI (no exact t_Na+ value given for PEGMEA-NaTFSI). The nucleation overpotential during Na plating/stripping was 110 mV for PEGMEA-NaTFSI ("P-co-F-SN-NaTFSI displays a lower nucleation overpotential (75 mV) compared to PEGMEA-NaTFSI (110 mV)").

PEGMEA-SN-NaTFSI was prepared as a solid electrolyte film ("PEGMEA-SN-NaTFSI films were prepared using the same method."). It was used in Na//Na symmetric cells where "a short circuit occurred after 536 h of charging/discharging cycling for Na/PEGMEA-SN-NaTFSI/Na battery" and in full cells where "NVP/PEGMEA-SN-NaTFSI/Na delivers a high initial discharge capacity of 101.3 mAh/g ... and ultimately undergoes a direct short circuit at the 67th cycle." These results show its role as a Na-ion battery electrolyte. No explicit ionic conductivity, transference number, or ESW values are reported for PEGMEA-SN-NaTFSI in the provided text, so numeric performance fields are left null.

P-co-F-NaTFSI is reported as a control all-solid-state polymer electrolyte for Na+ conduction. Reported properties specific to P-co-F-NaTFSI include: ionic conductivity of 2.63×10^-5 S cm^-1 at room temperature (Figure S8a); Na+ transference number t_Na+ = 0.41 (Figure S8b-c); electrochemical stability window (ESW) surpassing 5.62 V (Figure S10). These demonstrate its application as a Na+ solid electrolyte, but no cell cycling data were provided for this specific composition without SN.

PPP (3 wt% in 1 M LiFSI/DOL) is used as an electrolyte additive for lithium metal batteries. Evidence directly tied to PPP: Li|DOL-PPP|Li shows t+ = 0.61 under 10 mV DC polarization (vs 0.42 without PPP); the electrochemical stability window extends to 4.2 V; symmetric Li|DOL-PPP|Li cells remain stable for 1800 h at 0.2 mA/cm2 and for 500 h at 0.5 mA/cm2; LFP|DOL-PPP|Li maintains 136.6 mAh/g after 150 cycles at 0.5C with 0.039% capacity decay per cycle (94.1% retention); EIS shows lower resistance (47–53 Ω) than DOL (126–318 Ω), consistent with increased ionic conductivity attributed to PPP.

DOL-PPP refers to DOL electrolyte with 3 wt% PPP additive (1 M LiFSI in DOL). For LFP | DOL-PPP | Li cells at 0.5C, after 150 cycles the specific capacity is 136.6 mAh g^-1 with 94.1% capacity retention and 0.039% capacity decay per cycle (Fig. 6a). Additional performance indicators for DOL-PPP include higher Li+ transference number t+ = 0.61 versus 0.42 (Fig. 3b,c), extended electrochemical stability window to 4.2 V (Fig. S13), stable Li|Li cycling for 1800 h at 0.2 mA cm^-2 (Fig. 3g) and 500 h at 0.5 mA cm^-2 (Fig. 3h), and lower cell resistance change (47–53 Ω) compared to DOL (126–318 Ω) (Fig. 6d,e).

FBCP_20 was used to prepare a solid polymer electrolyte film (FBCPE_20) by mixing with LiTFSI at [EO]/[Li] = 20:1 and casting (Section 2.4), and was included among “the three FBCPEs” evaluated for ionic conductivity in SS/FBCPEs/SS cells (Section 2.6; Fig. 6a: “The temperature-dependent ionic conductivities of the three FBCPEs… are plotted…”). However, only FBCPE_30’s conductivity value was reported numerically; no specific numeric performance values were given for FBCPE_20. Battery cycling data shown were for LiFePO4/FBCPE_30/Li (Fig. 7), not for FBCPE_20.

Electrolyte based on FBCP_30 (denoted FBCPE_30 with LiTFSI, [EO]/[Li]=20:1) shows: “FBCPE_30 shows a higher ionic conductivity (2.68 × 10^-4 S cm-1) at 70 °C than FBCPE_20 and FBCPE_40.” and “the t_Li+ was calculated to be 0.41.” Additional context: “FBCPE_30 shows decomposition at 4.9 V …” and in Li/FBCPE_30/Li: stable overpotential ~0.04 V with no short circuit over 600 h at 0.1 mA cm^-2, 70 °C.

FBCP_40 is used to prepare the solid polymer electrolyte FBCPE_40 by mixing with LiTFSI at [EO]/[Li] = 20:1 and casting a ~30 μm film (Section 2.4). FBCPE_40 is included among the three electrolytes studied for lithium battery applications, with morphology (AFM/TEM) shown for FBCPE_40 (Fig. 5h,i) and ionic conductivity vs temperature presented in Arrhenius plots for all three FBCPEs (Fig. 6a). However, specific numerical performance values (e.g., t_Li+, ESW, cycling in Li or LiFePO4/NCM cells) are only reported for FBCPE_30, not for FBCPE_40.

P-FEM@Li was applied as an artificial SEI for Li metal batteries. In Li||NCM811 pouch cells, 'achieved a specific energy of 428 Wh kg−1 and ... maintained a capacity retention of 73% after 175 cycles ... average CE 99.80%.' Symmetric cells with P-FEM@Li 'could stably cycle for more than 4400 h with a low polarization voltage at 1 mA cm−2 and 1 mAh cm−2.' Li||LFP full cells: initial 146 mAh g−1; after 600 cycles, 127.7 mAh g−1 with 87.2% retention at 1C.

In Li||Cu cells with P-FEM@Cu, cyclic CE tests at 1 mA cm^-2 and 1 mAh cm^-2 showed almost no attenuation within 270 cycles (Figure 3d). The average CE was 99.78% after 100 cycles (Figure 3e). Electrolyte: 1 M LiTFSI in DME:DOL (1:1 v/v) with 2 wt% LiNO3.

FP-GPE is used as a gel polymer electrolyte for Li metal batteries. Direct performance for FP-GPE: highest ionic conductivity among compared GPEs, 4.45 mScm^-1 at 25°C (Figure 1b, S7). High-voltage stability: no remarkable oxidation under 4.9 V vs Li/Li+ (Figure 1a). Li||Li symmetric cells: critical current density 4.5 mA cm^-2 and stable overpotential for 700 h (Figure S13). Cell-level results enabled by FP-GPE: NCM622|FP-GPE|Li delivers 183.4/180.8/172.9/165.1/154.7/134.3 mAh g^-1 at 0.1/0.2/0.5/1/2/5 C and retains 172.6 mAh g^-1 upon returning to 0.5 C; long cycling at 1 C with 80.1% retention after 1200 cycles and 71.5% after 1500 cycles (Figure 1f, Table 1). Broad cathode compatibility: NCM811 181.8 mAh g^-1, 86.0% after 1000 cycles; LCO 152.7 mAh g^-1, 79.7% after 1500 cycles; LFP 93.6% after 4000 cycles at 2 C (Table 1). High-voltage operation: LNCMO|FP-GPE|Li at 4.6 V gives 231.2 mAh g^-1 and 84.5% retention after 100 cycles (Figure 3a). Practical cells: high-loading NCM622 full cell (20.84 mg cm^-2) areal capacity 3.17 mAh cm^-2 with 85.2% retention after 100 cycles at 0.3 C (Figure 3b); pouch cells 1.2 Ah (NCM811) and 95.7% retention after 70 cycles (NCM622) (Figure 3c–e); 18650 NCM811||Li delivers 1.0 Ah and 158.5 Wh kg^-1 with 95.0% retention after 30 cycles (Figure 3f). Temperature robustness: at −25°C ionic conductivity 1.12 mScm^-1; NCM622|FP-GPE|Li gives 115.2 mAh g^-1 initially and 78.4% retention after 200 cycles; at 60°C 187.0 mAh g^-1 at 0.1 C with 87.5% retention after 150 cycles (Figure 4a–b, S27).

Direct mentions of the specific sample: “The solid electrolyte obtained was labeled as P-0.5, P-0.6, P-0.7, P-0.8, and P-0.8-FEC.” and “FT-IR spectra of PVDF-HFP, P-0.5, P-0.6, P-0.7, P-0.8, and P-0.8-FEC … were analyzed.” These confirm P-0.5 is a solid-state electrolyte sample. No quantitative performance (conductivity, stability window, cycling, etc.) is reported for P-0.5; metrics are given only for P-0.8 or P-0.8-FEC. Therefore, all performance fields are left null.

P-0.6 is prepared as a solid-state electrolyte sample (Experimental: Solid State Electrolyte Preparation; Figure 1e lists P-0.6 in FT-IR). However, all quantitative performance data in the text are given for P-0.8 or P-0.8-FEC (e.g., ionic conductivity 0.313 mS cm^-1 for P-0.8; Arrhenius/LSV/cycling for P-0.8-FEC and P-0.8/P-L). No explicit ionic conductivity value, electrochemical stability window, cycling, or other application metrics are reported for P-0.6 in the text; Figure 3a likely includes P-0.6 but no readout is provided in the text.

P-0.7 is explicitly one of the DES-modified solid electrolytes prepared and characterized: “The solid electrolyte obtained was labeled as P-0.5, P-0.6, P-0.7, P-0.8, and P-0.8-FEC.” (Experimental Section). It appears in FT-IR characterization among the electrolyte set: “FT-IR spectra of PVDF-HFP, P-0.5, P-0.6, P-0.7, P-0.8, and P-0.8-FEC…” (Figure 1e caption). The paper’s application is Li-ion conduction in solid-state Li-metal batteries, but numerical performance data in Results and Discussion are reported for P-0.8 and/or P-0.8-FEC (e.g., ionic conductivity, activation energy, LSV, cycling), with no specific quantitative metrics given for P-0.7. Therefore only the application scenario (solid-state electrolyte for Li+ transport) is provided; performance fields are left null.

P-0.8 is a DES-modified solid-state electrolyte; at room temperature, the ionic conductivity of P-0.8 reached 0.313 mS cm^-1 (Figure 3a). The text explicitly reports this value for P-0.8, indicating its application as a Li+ conducting solid polymer electrolyte.

Li/P-0.8-FEC/LFP full cells: after 500 cycles at 25 °C (1 C), the capacity was 139.9 mAh g−1 with 97.8% capacity retention (Figure 5a,b). Additional performance for P-0.8-FEC includes electrochemical stability up to 4.6 V (Figure 3d), stable Li|Li cycling for 500 h at 0.1 mA cm−2 (Figure 3e), and a Li+ transference number of 0.435.

In Li/P-L/Li symmetric cells at 0.1 mA cm^-2, the P-L short-circuited after only 30 h (Figure 3e). Room-temperature ionic conductivity was reported only for P-0.8 (0.313 mS cm^-1), stated to be approximately twice P-L, but no explicit numeric value for P-L was given, so conductivity was not extracted.

PFMC-SPE is used as a solid polymer electrolyte in Li-metal batteries. Evidence: "the cell showed 119.6 mAhg^-1 even after 750 cycles at 1 C" (Li/PFMC-SPE/LFP). Additional PFMC-SPE performance: "The ionic conductivity of PFMC-SPE at 25 °C can be calculated to be 1.0 mS / cm" and electrochemical stability "dramatically increases as the voltage reaches over 5 V".

与 FMC-SPE 直接相关的数据：
- 离子电导率："the ionic conductivity of the FMC-SPE is 1.1 mS / cm at 25 °C"（Fig. 3a 描述段）。
- 锂离子迁移数："tLi+ of FMC-SPE (tLi+ = 0.21)"（Fig. 3b/相关文本）。
- 其他表现（未入字段但支撑场景）：LSV 起氧化电位 "oxidation occurred at 4.25 V (FMC-SPE)"；电化学浮充时 "FMC-SPE revealed leakage currents exceeding 100 μA at 4.3 V"；临界电流密度 CCD 为 "0.5 mA cm−2"。在对称电池 0.1 mA/cm^2 下，"after 50 h, the overpotential increases ... from 50 mV to 5 V"。

Electrochemical properties comparison explicitly reports: "The ionic conductivity of PFMC-SPE at 25 °C can be calculated to be 1.0 mS / cm, which is much higher than that of the NfMC-SPE (0.5 mS / cm at 25 °C)." Additional NFMC-SPE performance evidence: Li-ion transference number tLi+ = 0.19 (Figs. S7, S8); oxidation occurred at 4.7 V (LSV); CCD = 0.35 mA cm^-2; Li symmetric cell short-circuited after 32 h at 0.1 mA cm^-2.

FPASEI is used as an artificial SEI on Li metal anodes. Its ionic conductivity is 0.87×10^-3 S cm^-1 and electronic resistivity ~2.17×10^4 Ω·cm (Fig. 2d,e), and it increases t_Li+ from 0.31 to 0.77 (Fig. 2h,i). Symmetric FPASEI-Li||FPASEI-Li cells cycle stably >500 h at 1 mA cm^-2, 1 mAh cm^-2 (Fig. 3a) and up to 900 h at 0.5 mA cm^-2 (Fig. S9). In full cells, FPASEI-Li||NCM811 retains 80% capacity after 265 cycles (Fig. 5d,e); at 70 °C it retains 80% after 120 cycles with CE ~99% (Fig. 5f,g). FPASEI-Cu half-cells show CE 97.88% vs 85.04% for bare-Cu (Fig. S8). These results are directly tied to the FPASEI layer.

HCE (LiFSI/DME liquid electrolyte, LiFSI:DME molar ratio 1.9) is used as a high-concentration electrolyte for lithium metal batteries.
