Reported HCE-specific performance: Li/Cu Coulombic efficiency ~99.3% (Figure 3a,b text); Li/Li symmetric short-circuits after ~740 h at 2.0 mA cm^-2 / 1.0 mAh cm^-2 (Figure 3j); lower onset oxidation potential than HCGPE in LSV (Figure 1h); Li-ion transference number tLi+ = 0.48 (Figure S15); ionic transport activation energy 0.18 eV (Arrhenius comparison). In high-voltage cells, HCE shows faster capacity decay after ~200 cycles under the same conditions compared with HCGPE-PE (Figure 4a,d; S32d; S33).

HCGPE is used as a gel polymer electrolyte for lithium metal batteries. Reported intrinsic electrolyte metrics for HCGPE include: ionic conductivity 4.8 mS cm^-1 at 30°C (Figures S13–S14); activation energy 0.16 eV (Arrhenius analysis); Li-ion transference number t_Li+ = 0.56 (Figure S15); higher oxidation stability vs HCE with an oxidation peak at ≈3.9 V associated with poly(TFEMA) oxidation forming a stable interphase (Figure 1h); lower exchange current density for Li plating/stripping, 0.518 mA cm^-2 (Figure S22). In Li/Li symmetric cells using HCGPE with an ultrathin PE separator (HCGPE-PE), stable cycling over 950 h at 2.0 mA cm^-2 / 1.0 mAh cm^-2 is reported (Figure 3j), illustrating application as a battery electrolyte.

In all-solid-state cells, the POE would be employed as a Li+-conducting electrolyte and polymer binder on cathode films. As shown in Figure 6d, the C5-POE-F-Li starts to be gradually oxidized at over 5.0 V; while the C5-POE-Li counterpart, without the trifluoroacetate terminating unit, show a sharp oxidating peak starting at 4.8 V (LSV at 45 °C, 0.1 mV s−1).

In all these samples, the C5-POE-F-Li in the ratio of 2.5:1 delivers the highest Li+ conductivity. At 45°C and 65°C, the Li+ conductivities of C5-POE-F-Li reach 3.6×10^-5 and 1.7×10^-4 Scm^-1, respectively.

C5-POE-F (used as a solid polymer electrolyte with LiTFSI, typically POE-F:LiTFSI = 2.5:1 by weight) was evaluated in Li metal cells and Li/NMC811 solid-state cells. Electrochemical stability: in Li/PPEGMA/C5-POE-F-Li/Fe, LSV shows C5-POE-F-Li starts gradual oxidation over 5.0 V, with no obvious oxidation peak until 5.0 V in CV (Figure 6c–d). Ionic conductivity: for C5-POE-F-Li (2.5:1), Li+ conductivity is 3.6e-5 S/cm at 45°C and 1.7e-4 S/cm at 65°C; membranes tested at 45°C to remain solid. Li/Li symmetric cell: stable Li plating/stripping over 320 h at 0.35 mA/cm^2 with ~±400 mV overpotential and no significant impedance rise (Figure 7a–b). Solid-state Li/C5-POE-F-Li/NMC811 (45°C, 0.1 mA/cm^2, 3.6–4.35 V): initial discharge 175 mAh/g, rising to 190.5 mAh/g by cycle 6; 157.7 and 115.6 mAh/g at cycles 100 and 200 (82.8% and 60.7% retention of 190.5 mAh/g). Coulombic efficiency reaches 99.5% at cycle 3 and ~99.8% thereafter over 200 cycles (Figure 9a–b).

The study prepared SPE membranes with varying SN loadings explicitly including PBA-PVDF-0.2SN (Section 2.1). Figure 3a presents Arrhenius plots of ionic conductivity for various SN weights, but no explicit numeric value is reported for 0.2SN at any temperature. Quantitative electrolyte and battery performance values are provided only for other samples (e.g., 0.4SN with 4.89 mS cm^-1 and cell tests), and 0.4SN was chosen for further investigations. Therefore, for PBA-PVDF-0.2SN, only the application context as a Li+ solid-state battery electrolyte can be stated, while specific performance metrics are not extractable from the provided text.

The sample PBA-PVDF-0.3SN is explicitly defined among the prepared solid polymer electrolytes: "the SPE membranes containing varying weight of SN were named PBA-PVDF-0.2SN, PBA-PVDF-0.3SN, PBA-PVDF-0.4SN and PBA-PVDF-0.5SN" (Section 2.1). These electrolytes are used as solid-state battery electrolytes in Li||LFP cells (Section 2.2) and evaluated for ionic transport: "EIS measurements were conducted" and Fig. 3a shows the Arrhenius plots "with various weights of SN" (Section 3.3). However, only PBA-PVDF-0.4SN is reported with a numeric ionic conductivity (4.89 mS cm^-1); no specific numeric value is provided for PBA-PVDF-0.3SN in the text, so the conductivity value is left null while retaining the appropriate unit.

PBA-PVDF-0.4SN shows the highest ionic conductivity among the series: "Adding 0.4 g SN to the PBA-PVDF membrane (PBA-PVDF-0.4SN) resulted in the maximum ion conductivity of 4.89 mS cm^-1." The text states this 0.4SN composition was chosen for further investigations. With this electrolyte (modified with IL), the paper also reports t_Li+ = 0.739 and solid-state LFP||SPE||Li cell performance of 157.0 mAh g^-1 at 0.5C (25 °C) with 87.3% capacity retention after 800 cycles.

PBA-PVDF-0.5SN (0.5 g SN) was prepared as one of the SPE variants, but specific performance metrics (ionic conductivity, transference number, cell cycling data) were quantitatively reported for PBA-PVDF-0.4SN or for unspecified “PBA-PVDF-SN (modified with IL)” rather than 0.5SN. Fig. 3a includes 0.5SN in Arrhenius plots, yet no numerical values are provided in text; only a qualitative note that further increasing SN beyond 0.4 g is detrimental to ion transport. Therefore, no numeric application metrics can be assigned exclusively to PBA-PVDF-0.5SN.

Electrochemical measurement states: "PBA-PVDF without SN exhibits a lower ion conductivity (0.9 mS cm^-1)." It also reports for this matrix that "Without the addition of SN and IL, the PBA-PVDF electrolyte only has a migration number of 0.403," indicating Li+ transport property specific to PBA-PVDF.

Sample definition: “a sample named PBA-SN without the use of PVDF” (Section 2.1). Performance mention (no PVDF): “the PBA-0.4SN electrolyte exhibited poor ion conductivity at room temperature… with a value of 0.8 mS cm^-1 (Figure S4). This can be attributed to the uneven dispersion of the SN ion conductor within the PBA system… without a 3D PVDF framework.” (Section 3.3). Thermal comparison context including PBA-SN: “compared to pure PVDF nanofiber, pure PBA, PBA-SN electrolyte and PVDF-SN electrolyte, PBA-PVDF-SN exhibits a higher maximum decomposition temperature.” (Fig. 2c description). No full-cell or Li||Li cycling data were reported for PBA-SN itself; such data are provided for PBA-PVDF-SN variants.

PVDF-SN is explicitly prepared as an electrolyte ('an electrolyte denoted PVDF-SN without the addition of PBA', Section 2.1). However, no specific electrochemical or battery performance data are reported for PVDF-SN; it appears only in TGA comparison (Fig. 2c) without quantitative performance metrics.

The text defines SFE only as a precursor solution: "First, precursor solution (named SFE) was obtained... after fully stirring... precursor was injected... and heated at 60°C for 4 h to obtain MSFE." All electrochemical characterizations and performance results are reported for MSFE or EDLE (e.g., ionic conductivity, Li/Li cycling, LCO/Li cell performance). No application performance data are provided for SFE itself.

Electrolyte application in Li metal and LCO/Li batteries is demonstrated. Key MSFE performance metrics reported: “Lithium ionic conductivity of MSFE electrolyte reached 1.77×10−3 S cm−1 at room temperature” (Section 3.2). Additional evidence of suitability for batteries: “linear scanning voltammetry (LSV) results showed that MSFE possessed a wide ESW up to 5.6 V” (Fig. 2d); “Li/MSFE/Li cells exhibited excellent stability… over 600 h under current density of 0.5 mA cm−2 and cut off capacity of 1 mAh cm−2” (Fig. 2e); “Li/MSFE/Cu cells exhibited an average Coulombic efficiency of 96.16% over 300 cycles, with current density of 0.2 mA cm−2 and cut-off capacity of 0.2 mAh cm−2” (Fig. 2f); and an Ah‑level LCO/MSFE/Li pouch cell maintained ~1.4 Ah over 15 cycles (Fig. 3g). Only the ionic conductivity is captured under capacity; other fields are left null due to schema fit.

EDLE is the commercial electrolyte (1 M LiPF6 in DMC:EC = 7:3 vol%) used as a comparator in Li metal and LCO/Li cells. Reported EDLE-specific performance includes: Li+ transference number t_Li+ = 0.32 (Fig. 2b,c). Electrochemical stability window ~4.7 V vs Li/SS (LSV, Fig. 2d). In Li/EDLE/Li symmetric cells (0.5 mA cm^-2, 1 mAh cm^-2), polarization rapidly increased ~200 h and a micro-short circuit occurred around 480 h (Fig. 2e). For LCO/EDLE/Li cells, capacity retention was 93.7% after 100 cycles at 0.2C and 81.2% after 150 cycles at 1C (text, Fig. S12 and S14a).

FGPE is a fluorinated, cross-linked polyether electrolyte for Li metal batteries. Reported performance includes: ionic conductivity 3.98 mS cm^-1 at 25 °C (Figure 1d); Li+ transference number t_Li+ = 0.59 (Figure S3). Additional FGPE-specific metrics (not encoded in fields): oxidation potential up to 5.1 V (Figure 1c); Li|FGPE|Li symmetric cell stable 2000 h at 0.5 mA cm^-2 with ~35 mV overpotential; critical current density 4 mA cm^-2; Li|FGPE|NCM622 achieves 1000 cycles with 78.0% capacity retention at 4.3 V and 210.2 mAh g^-1 with 74.2% retention after 100 cycles at 4.7 V.

P(HFBA1:1-co-MEA)/LiTFSI is used as a solid polymer electrolyte in Li metal batteries. Its room-temperature ionic conductivity reached 4.83e-5 S cm^-1 (Figure 2a). The Li+ transference number is 0.36 (Figure 2b,c). The electrochemical window exceeded 4.9 V (Figure 2f). In symmetric Li|electrolyte|Li cells it cycled stably for >1000 h at 0.05 mA cm^-2, with polarization ~75 mV initially and ~100 mV after 1050 h (Figure 4a). Full-cell data with this electrolyte: Li|...|LiNi0.5Co0.3Mn0.2O2 delivered 135.5, 121.3, and 93.6 mAh g^-1 at 0.1, 0.2, and 0.5C, respectively (Figure 5b); Li|...|LiFePO4 showed an initial discharge capacity of 159.4 mAh g^-1 and ~99% Coulombic efficiency over 200 cycles (Figure 5e).

PMEA/LiTFSI is used as a solid polymer electrolyte in Li metal batteries. Reported metrics for PMEA/LiTFSI include: (i) lithium-ion transference number t_Li+ = 0.17 ("the t_Li+ of P(HFBA1:1-co-MEA) is 0.36, which is 1.1 times higher than that of PMEA/LiTFSI (0.17, as shown in Figure S1)"); (ii) electrochemical window 4.2 V ("Unlike that of the PMEA/LiTFSI electrolyte (4.2 V)"); (iii) in symmetric Li|PMEA/LiTFSI|Li at 0.05 mA cm^-2, initial polarization 160 mV and failure after 109 h ("the Li+ plating and stripping voltage increased... initial polarization voltage of 160 mV, and the battery failed after just 109 h"). These directly pertain to PMEA/LiTFSI performance as an electrolyte.

Directly reported for PEO–PFP solid polymer electrolyte containing PFP: "Notably, the 80PEO20PFP polymer electrolyte exhibits a high lithium-ion transference number of 0.39 and ionic conductivity of 3.7 × 10^−4 S cm^−1 at 60 °C." Also, the oxidative stability of the PFP-containing electrolyte is higher: "the oxidation potential of 80PEO20PFP electrolyte has been increased to 4.4 V (vs. Li+/Li)." Battery tests with PFP-containing electrolyte further support applicability: at 60 °C, Li|80PEO20PFP|LiFePO4 maintained 150 mAh g−1 at 1C; at 28 °C with SN, capacity remained 128.24 mAh g−1 (88% retention) after 100 cycles.
The t_Li+ of the 80PEO20PEP electrolyte was reported to be approximately 0.18 (Fig. S5), higher than that of the PEO electrolyte.

F-PCEE is used as a solid polymer electrolyte in Li metal batteries. At −10 °C, Li||F-PCEE||NCM811 delivers discharge capacities of 187, 169, 123, 83.5, and 59.7 mAh g−1 at 0.033, 0.05, 0.1, 0.15, and 0.2 C, respectively, with recovery to 116 mAh g−1 when returning to 0.1 C (Figure 4a,b). Cycling at −10 °C shows 85.3% capacity retention after 150 cycles at 0.1 C with 99.2% average CE (Figure 4d). At 25 °C, it retains 85.7% capacity (157 mAh g−1) after 150 cycles at 0.5 C with 99.5% CE (Figure 3b,c). Symmetric Li||F-PCEE||Li sustains >1500 h cycling at −10 °C and 1.0 mA cm−2 (Figure S15; Conclusions). Oxidation stability is evidenced by <2 μA leakage current at 4.5 V and LSV oxidation onset ≈4.75 V (Figure 3a; Figure S12). Ionic conductivity of F-PCEE is 0.23 mS cm−1 at −10 °C (Figure 2d).

BA-PCEE was tested as the solid electrolyte in Li||BA-PCEE||NCM811 full cells at −10 °C. The paper states: "Specifically, BA-PCEE exhibiting a discharge capacity of ≈160 mAh g−1 at 0.033 C experienced a significant capacity drop to 70 mAh g−1 at 0.1 C." It also notes BA-PCEE showed good cycling at room temperature but rapidly decreased at 0 °C.

Li||Li symmetric cells employing RASEI@Li (P(HFBA-2%BAED)) showed cycle stability over 500 h at 1 mA cm^-2 and 1 mAh cm^-2 in carbonate electrolyte (Fig. 3a). Also reported: 1300 h stable cycling at 0.5 mA cm^-2 and 1 mAh cm^-2 with overpotential <60 mV (Fig. S10). Full/pouch cells: NCM811/Li with RASEI@Li retained ~80% after 300 cycles at 0.5C (Fig. 4a); ~90% after 190 cycles at ~20 mg cm^-2 loading (0.3C) while bare Li shorted after 45 cycles (Fig. 4b,c); LFP/Li with RASEI@Li retained ~85% after 400 cycles (Fig. S15); pouch cell retained >85% after 200 cycles under 1/3C charge and 1C discharge (Fig. 5a,b).

Ionic conductivity of EFA-G at 30 °C: “The σ values of EFA-G, EA-G, EF-G, and E-G were 9.87×10^-4 ... Scm^-1 at 30 °C” (Figure 4a text). Li+ transference number: “According to the Bruce-Vincent-Evans equation, the tLi+ value of EFA-G was determined to be 0.33” (Figure 4b,c). Other reported metrics for EFA-G (e.g., oxidation stability up to 5.0 V vs Li+/Li; symmetric Li/Li cycling ~1050 h at 0.1 mA/cm^2) are not mapped to the available schema fields.

PEO-cPTFBC-LiDFOB is used as a solid polymer electrolyte in Li metal batteries. Direct evidence: "It is noted that the Li+ conductivity of PEO-cPTFBC-LiDFOB electrolyte reaches 2.2×10^-5 Scm^-1 at 50 °C" (Fig. 2c). Additional application-related performance: "the PEO-cPTFBC-LiDFOB electrolyte is stable up to 4.7 V" vs. Li+/Li (Fig. 2d); in Li/Li symmetric cells, "the overpotential remains nearly stable in 300 h (0.090 V)" at 0.2 mA cm^-2; in full cells, "LiCoO2/Li cell based on PEO-cPTFBC-LiDFOB electrolyte delivers a discharge capacity of 118.4 mAh·g^-1 corresponding to 73.8% capacity retention after 50 cycles" and for LiFePO4/Li, initial discharge 157.3 mAh·g^-1 at 0.1 C and 60 °C, rising to 165.4 mAh·g^-1 at the 14th cycle and 158.5 mAh·g^-1 after 150 cycles.

PEO-LiDFOB is a PEO-based solid polymer electrolyte (80 wt% PEO, 20 wt% LiDFOB) used in Li metal batteries. Electrochemical performance specific to PEO-LiDFOB: Li+ conductivity 1.9e-5 S/cm at 50°C (Fig. 2c). Li+ transference number tLi+ = 0.21 (Fig. S8). Oxidative stability: anodic current starts to rise at 4.5 V vs Li+/Li (Fig. 2d). Li/Li symmetric cell galvanostatic polarization at 60°C: overpotential 0.019 V at 0.05 mA/cm2 and 0.039 V at 0.1 mA/cm2; at 0.2 mA/cm2 overpotential 0.081 V and a sudden drop at 245 h attributed to short-circuit (Fig. 3a). CV: lithium plating starts at −0.08 V (Fig. 3c). In LiCoO2/Li cells at 0.1 C and 60°C, initial discharge capacity ~160 mAh/g with Coulombic efficiency 92.8%; capacity drops to 16.4 mAh/g after 25 cycles (Fig. 5c and Fig. S11).

No application performance is reported for bF-B. The paper assigns performance metrics to the CSE films (e.g., bF-P) rather than bF-B: “Four CSE films were then fabricated … named as bF-P, bB-P, PF-P, and PB-P.” The only explicit mention of bF-B is in the methods for mechanical testing: “DMA (TA Q800) was conducted to perform the storage modulus and loss modulus of bF-B under variable frequencies (0.01–100 Hz) ….” Electrochemical and battery results (e.g., ionic conductivity 5.64×10^-4 S cm^-1 at 25 °C, t_Li+ = 0.58, discharge capacities, CCD, windows) are reported for bF-P and other CSEs, not for bF-B.

PEO-LiTFSI is used as a solid polymer electrolyte component enabling Li+ conduction in the study. The text explicitly states a "PEO-LiTFSI film was prepared as a control sample" and discusses it within the context of constructing composite solid electrolytes for ASSLMBs, but provides no standalone electrochemical performance metrics (ionic conductivity, t_Li+, electrochemical window, or cell cycling) for the PEO-LiTFSI film. Reported performance values (e.g., 5.64×10^-4 S cm^-1 at 25 °C, t_Li+ values, CCD, cycling) are for composite CSEs (bF-P, bB-P, PF-P, PB-P), not for PEO-LiTFSI alone. Only thermal property (Tm = 66.36 °C) and structural observations are given for the PEO-LiTFSI film, which are not application performance metrics.

HFGP-SE is a solid electrolyte for Li metal batteries. Results report: "At room temperature, HFGP-SE shows an ionic conductivity of 0.84 mS cm^-1" and a Li-ion transference number t_Li+ of 0.87. Li||Li symmetric cells with HFGP-SE cycled stably for >1200 h at 0.5 mA cm^-2 and >2500 h at 0.1 mA cm^-2. Li||HFGP-SE||LFP retained 70.5% capacity after 2200 cycles at 1C. Li(50 μm)||HFGP-SE||NCM811 delivered 190 mA h g^-1 at 0.3 mA cm^-2 with 94% retention after 110 cycles and CE >99%. Pouch cell retained 91% after 60 cycles. Leakage current at 4.5 V was 5 μA (vs 71 μA with PVHF-SPE), indicating superior oxidation stability.

SNSPE-40 is used as the solid polymer electrolyte in Li|SNSPE-40|Ni90 cells. At 26 °C and 4.5 V cut-off (0.5 C), the cell delivers an initial discharge capacity of 204.1 mAh g^-1 and shows 57.4% capacity retention after 300 cycles: “Fig. 5d shows the cycle performance of Li|SNSPE-40|Ni90 at 0.5 C and 26 °C with a cut-off voltage of 4.5 V. The Li|SNSPE-40|Ni90 cell delivers a high initial discharge capacity of 204.1 mAh g^-1 and exhibits stable cycling with a capacity retention of 57.4% after 300 cycles.” Additional related performance (not encoded in fields): ionic conductivity 1.33 mS cm^-1 at 25 °C; electrochemical window ~5.4 V; Li|SNSPE-40|Li symmetric cell stable plating/stripping for 2000 h at 0.1 mA cm^-2; LCO full cells show 162.0 mAh g^-1 initial at 1 C with 54.9% retention after 250 cycles.

FB-SPE is used as a solid polymer electrolyte in ASSBs. Full-cell Li/FB-SPE/LiFePO4 shows an initial specific capacity of 106 mAh/g at 0.1C and 70 °C, with 96% capacity retention after 50 cycles (Figure 5A,C). Supporting electrolyte metrics directly linked to FB-SPE include ionic conductivity 1.45×10^-4 Scm^-1 at 70 °C for the optimized composition (Figure 2C), electrochemical stability up to ≈5.0 V vs Li (Figure 2D), Li+ transference number tLi+ = 0.25 (Figure S9), symmetric Li||Li stability for >1500 h at 0.1–0.2 mA cm^-2 (Figure 3A–C), and Li||Cu CE stabilizing at ≈87% with ~30 mV overpotential (Figure 4A,B).

PFPE-SE is used as a solid-state electrolyte in Li metal batteries. Key performance directly tied to PFPE-SE: (1) Ionic conductivity: 1.31×10^-4 S/cm at 70 °C (Figure 1e; Conclusion). (2) Li+ transference number: 0.72 at 70 °C (Figure 2a and caption). (3) Electrochemical stability window: up to 4.7 V (Figure 2g). (4) Li|PFPE-SE|Li symmetric cell stable cycling: over 550 h at 0.1 mA cm^-2 (Figure 3c; Conclusion). (5) Li||LFP full-cell performance at 70 °C: at 0.2 C, after 220 cycles capacity 111.4 mAh/g with 75.9% retention (Figure 4a,b); at 0.5 C, initial 156.0 mAh/g and 116.4 mAh/g after 230 cycles with 74.3% retention (Figure 4c,d). (6) Rate capability (Li||LFP, 70 °C): 163.0 mAh/g at 0.1 C, 147.3 mAh/g at 0.6 C, and 114.4 mAh/g at 1 C; capacity recovers to 165.1 mAh/g when returning to 0.1 C (Figure 4e,f).

Poly(TEGDVE-OFDIB)-LiTFSI is used as a solid polymer electrolyte (SPE) in all-solid-state Li-metal batteries: symmetric Li|SPE|Li and full cells Li|SPE|LFP, Li|SPE|NMC622/811 (Sections 2.3, 3; Figs. 3, 5, 6). Its Li+ ionic conductivity from EIS is explicitly reported: “σLi values of 7.6×10−5 S cm−1 at 25 °C, … 1.3×10−4 at 40 °C, 1.8×10−4 at 60 °C, and 3.7×10−4 at 80 °C” (Fig. 2h). Electrochemical stability window reaches 4.98 V vs Li+/Li (Fig. 3d). These data directly pertain to Li+ transport in the SPE formulated as poly(TEGDVE-OFDIB)-LiTFSI.

Poly(TEGDVE)-LiTFSI was synthesized as a control SPE (without OFDIB). EIS measurements show a significantly lower t_Li of 0.20 and a higher t_TFSI of 0.80 at 40 °C (Fig. S6a). The poly(TEGDVE)-LiTFSI SPE exhibits Li+ conductivities of 5.8×10^-6, 8.1×10^-6, and 1.4×10^-5 S cm^-1 at 25 °C, 40 °C, and 60 °C respectively (Fig. S6b). These data indicate its use as a solid polymer electrolyte for Li+ conduction in all-solid-state lithium batteries.

PEO-LiTFSI was benchmarked in Li|PEO-LiTFSI|LFP full cells at 40 °C: initial discharge capacity 137.2 mAh g^-1 with ICE 88.2%, capacity retention 67.9% after 200 cycles; ΔV = 0.48 V at 50% SOC; ionic conductivity 2.26×10^-5 S cm^-1 at 40 °C (Fig. S12 referenced in text). The extracted capacity value (137.2 mAh g^-1) directly supports the battery application metric for the PEO-LiTFSI SPE.

FC-H_{0.1}-T_1 used as a polymer electrolyte: ionic conductivity 6.3×10^-4 S cm^-1 at 25 °C (Figure S16); Li+ transference number t_Li+ = 0.8 (Figure 2b; Figures S18,S19); electrochemical stability up to 4.4 V (Figure S20); Li//Li symmetric cell overpotentials 15/24/63/108 mV at 0.1/0.2/0.5/1 mA cm^-2 and stable cycling for 1000 h at 0.5 mA cm^-2 without short circuit (Figure 2c; Figure S22). LFP/FC-H_{0.1}-T_1/Li full cell: 109.4 mAh g^-1 at 6C; capacity recovers when rate returns to 0.2C; 98.8% retention of initial 147.2 mAh g^-1 after 1000 cycles at 0.5C; 95.5% retention with 135.4 mAh g^-1 after 500 cycles at 1C (Figure 2d; S25,S27). NCM811/FC-H_{0.1}-T_1/Li: initial 174.0 mAh g^-1 with >80% retention after 340 cycles and average CE 99.7%; 130.5 mAh g^-1 after 350 cycles at 1C (Figure 2f; S30).

No direct application performance is reported for FC-H_{0.1}-T_2. This sample appears only in degradation/recycling studies and a qualitative mechanical note: “FC-H_{0.1}·T_2 experiences a severe drop of ultimate strength.” (Section 2.1) and “Methanolysis of FC-H_{0.1}-T_2… only TBD is capable of completely degrading the PTMC side chains… disappearance … within 35 min indicates the complete decomposition of PTMC fragments” (Section 2.3). All battery electrolyte and capacitor/coating demonstrations are for FC-H_{0.1}-T_1 or FC-H_{0.1}(R), not for FC-H_{0.1}-T_2.

For FC-H_{0.1} used as a dielectric material: “the FC-H_{0.1} exhibits significantly lower remnant polarization (P_r) than PVDF-CTFE (0.17 vs 0.45 μC cm^{-2}) … The maximum discharged electric energy densities (UeS) of both pristine and recycled FC-H_{0.1} … are … 14.73 and 15.14 vs 0.78 J cm^{-3}” (Figure 5e,f). The text lists two UeS values (14.73 and 15.14 J cm^{-3}) for pristine and recycled FC-H_{0.1} but does not assign which is which, so capacity is left null.

Direct evidence for 0.03TB-TFPO/9DOL: (1) "When DOL and TFPO were mixed in 9/1 and 8/2 volumes ... the ionic conductivities of their copolymer electrolytes were 1.7 and 1.2 mS cm^-1 (Fig. 2a)"; (2) "the fluorinated copolyether electrolyte 0.03TB-TFPO/9DOL was encapsulated in a lithium-lithium (Li-Li) symmetric battery ... Li-ion transfer number was calculated to be 0.68" (Fig. S10, Table S2); (3) "the oxidation stability of the fluorinated copolyether electrolyte 0.03TB-TFPO/9DOL exceeded 4.5 V" (Fig. 2c); (4) "the 0.03TB-TFPO/9DOL did not ignite ... and the electrolyte remained intact" (flammability, Fig. 2d); (5) Li-Li symmetric cell showed stable voltage profile during 2000 h at 0.1 mA cm^-2 (Fig. 3c); (6) Li–LFP: "initial specific capacity of 154 mAh g^-1 and ... 142 mAh g^-1 after 320 cycles (92%)" at 0.2C (Fig. 4d), and "90% capacity retention after 800 cycles" at 1C (Fig. 4f).

PFT21 is used as a Li+ conducting polymer electrolyte with LiTFSI. Table 1 reports σ (10^-5 S·cm^-1, 30 °C) = 0.45 for PFT21 (measured by EIS on glass fiber-filled polymer electrolytes). Lithium-ion transference number tLi+ for PFT21 is 0.75 (galvanostatic polarization). ESW (V vs Li+/Li) for PFT21 is 5.7; without glass fiber, ESW decreases to 5.5 V.

PFT32 is evaluated as a polymer electrolyte (with 5 wt.% LiTFSI, glass fiber-filled). Table 1 lists for PFT32: σ (10^-5 S·cm^-1, 30 °C) = 0.82 (i.e., 8.2×10^-6 S/cm), tLi+ = 0.66, ESW = 5.6 V vs Li+/Li. Additional note: removing the glass fiber slightly lowers tLi+ for PFT32 from 0.65 to 0.57 (Figure 7), but Table 1 values are used as primary R&D data.

PFT11 is used as a polymer electrolyte (PFT + LiTFSI) for Li-ion conduction. Table 1 lists for PFT11: σ (10^-5 S·cm^-1, 30 °C) = 1.0 (measured by EIS on glass fiber-filled polymer electrolytes) and tLi+ = 0.57 (determined by galvanostatic polarization). ESW for PFT11 is 5.6 V vs Li+/Li by LSV (glass fiber-filled), and without glass fiber ESW decreases to 5.3 V. Relevant excerpts: “Ionic conductivity was measured on glass fiber-filled polymer electrolytes… (σ = d/(R·S)).” “Table 1… PFT11… σ (10^-5 S·cm^-1, 30 °C) 1.0 … tLi+ 0.57 … ESW (V vs Li+/Li) 5.6.” “the ESW of PFT11 decreases from 5.6 to 5.3 V” when removing the glass fiber separator.

Table 1 lists performance for each copolyether. For PFT23: σ (10^-5 S·cm^-1, 30 °C) = 1.3 (i.e., 1.3 × 10^-5 S·cm^-1 at 30 °C); tLi+ = 0.54; ESW = 5.5 V. These values were measured for PFT-based electrolytes with LiTFSI using EIS/LSV per the Experimental section.

Table 1 (Measurement Statistics of Polymer Electrolytes) lists values for PFT12: σ (10−5 S·cm−1, 30 °C) = 0.44 (i.e., 4.4×10−6 S·cm−1) and tLi+ = 0.42; ESW = 5.5 V. Section 3.2 text is consistent with σ at 30 °C for composition 1/2 (PFT12) being ~4.5×10−6 S·cm−1.

For ELE (EC/EMC-based liquid electrolyte): the paper reports "LRLO/ELE/Li batteries show a lower capacity retention of 54.2% with an initial discharge capacity of 191.4 mA h g^-1" (0.5C, 2–4.8 V). Additional ELE-specific performance details (not mapped to schema fields): "the ESW of ELE only reached 4.2 V" (Figure 3b); "lithium-ion transfer number of ELE (0.25)" (Figure 3d); and for Li–Cu cells, "a low average Coulombic efficiency of 32.5% over 300 cycles" (Figure 3f).

FLE is defined as the FEC/FEMC-based liquid electrolyte. Reported metrics for FLE include: (1) lithium-ion transference number t_Li+ = 0.33 (“It is noteworthy that the lithium-ion transfer number of FLE and FTLE reached 0.33 and 0.36 (Figure S9, Supporting Information), respectively.”). (2) Li/Li symmetric cells short-circuited at 550 h under 0.5 mA cm^-2 at 30 °C (“Li-Li symmetric cells using FLE and FTLE as electrolytes also suffered from a short circuit at 550 and 850 h (Figure S10, Supporting Information), respectively,” with the test described earlier at 30 °C and 0.5 mA cm^-2). (3) Li/Cu cells coulombic efficiency of 96.9% over 300 cycles at 0.5 mA cm^-2 with 0.5 mAh cm^-2 (“Li-Cu cells using FLE and FTLE as electrolytes both demonstrate a high Coulombic efficiency of 96.9% and 97.6%, respectively, during the long-term cycling test…”, and Fig. 3f specifies 0.5 mA cm^-2, 0.5 mAh cm^-2).

For FTLE (FEC/FEMC/TFTFE-based liquid electrolyte): (1) Electrochemical stability window (ESW) is 5.5 V (vs. 4.7 V for FLE) (Figure S8). (2) Li-ion transference number t_Li+ = 0.36 (Figure S9). (3) In Li/Li symmetric cells at 30 °C and 0.5 mA cm^-2, short circuit occurs at 850 h (Figure S10). (4) In Li/Cu cells at 0.5 mA cm^-2 with 0.5 mAh cm^-2, Coulombic efficiency averages 97.6% over 300 cycles (Figure 3f). (5) Activation energy from Arrhenius analysis: FTLE 1.37 kJ mol^-1 (lower than FLE 1.73 kJ mol^-1), indicating reduced viscosity and higher ionic conductivity than FLE (text in 2.2).

FTPE is used as a quasi-solid polymer electrolyte for lithium metal batteries. Reported performance directly tied to FTPE includes: (1) ionic conductivity of FTPE reached 6.3 × 10^-4 S cm^-1 at 30 °C (Results 2.2, Fig. 3a text); (2) lithium-ion transfer number of 0.48 (Fig. 3e text); (3) ESW up to 5.6 V (Fig. 3b text); (4) Li/Li symmetric cells with FTPE cycled stably for 1600 h at 0.5 mA cm^-2 and 30 °C without short circuit (Fig. 3c text); (5) Li–Cu cells average Coulombic efficiency 97.8% over 300 cycles at 0.5 mA cm^-2 with 0.5 mAh cm^-2 (Fig. 3f text). These support the electrolyte application; only ionic conductivity is recorded in the capacity field, with other metrics provided here as context.

Symmetric cell test conditions: 'constant current charge-discharge for the symmetrical Zn||Zn cells at the current density of 0.5 mA/cm^2 and areal capacity of 0.5 mA·h/cm^2.' Performance for the material PAA@Zn: 'The Zn||Zn cell coated with PAA exhibits an enhanced life span of 323 h far higher than the bare Zn||Zn battery (Figure 4A).' For full cells, only relative rate retention is given: 'the capacities of PAA@Zn||MnO2 ... from 1 to 10 C fast drop to 54% of initial capacity,' without absolute capacity values for PAA@Zn, so the structured metric reported is the symmetric-cell cycle life.

Symmetric and full cells were explicitly assembled with PAA-TFPA@Zn anodes: “The pouch cells of Zn||Zn, PAA@Zn||PAA@Zn, and PAA-TFPA@Zn||PAA-TFPA@Zn were assembled… PAM gel (1 mol/L ZnSO4) was used as both the electrolyte and the separator.” During operation, PAA‑TFPA evolves in situ to a hybrid SEI (PAA‑Zn/ZnF2@Zn) that delivers the reported performance. Symmetric cell: “the cyclic life of the PAA‑Zn/ZnF2@Zn||PAA‑Zn/ZnF2@Zn cell is improved to 3060 h” at 0.5 mA/cm2 and 0.5 mA·h/cm2. Full cell (PAA‑Zn/ZnF2@Zn||MnO2): “the specific capacity … is about 248 mA·h/g at the current density of 1 C… slightly decreases to 142 mA·h/g at 10 C (~59% retention).” Long‑cycle stability: “the reversible capacity … is 103 mA·h/g after 3000 cycles with 79% of the initial capacity,” and after bending 100 times it “can steadily work another 3000 cycles with 85% capacity retention.”

Direct evidence for M-(PTC)LE GPE: "In Fig. 4d, M-(PTC)LE GPE is calculated to possess the high ionic conductivity of 1.7 × 10^-3 S cm^-1" (room temperature, 25 °C). Additional related performance (not recorded as capacity here): electrochemical window 3.8 V (Figure S28); Li|M-(PTC)LE|Li overpotential ~23 mV at 0.1 mA cm^-2; Li|M-(PTC)LE|LFP discharge capacity 152.8 mAh g^-1 with 98.1% Coulombic efficiency at the 240th cycle (1 C).

“(PTC)LE, (PH)LE, PLE, (PTC)L, (PH)L and PL GPEs were acquired by the same method.” The cells used these GPEs: “The Li symmetric cells were assembled by sandwiching the gel polymer electrolyte between two Li metals.” and “Li//LFP battery was charged to 4.0 V and discharged to 2.5 V.” Additionally, properties were assessed for (PTC)LE: “Contact angles with liquid electrolyte of M-(PTC)LE, P(VDF-TrFE-CTFE), (PTC)L and (PTC)LE…” and “Dielectric constants of PLE, (PH)LE and (PTC)LE vs. frequency at 25 °C.” These passages indicate (PTC)LE is used as a gel polymer electrolyte for Li+ transport in quasi-solid-state lithium metal batteries.

The sample (PTC)L = P(VDF-TrFE-CTFE)/LiTFSI is explicitly prepared (Section 2.3) and used to make a GPE by soaking in liquid electrolyte (Section 2.4). It is evaluated for wettability/absorption via contact angle alongside M-(PTC)LE, P(VDF-TrFE-CTFE), and (PTC)LE (Fig. 4a), but no numeric values are reported for (PTC)L. Ionic conductivity values discussed in text are only provided for M-(PTC)LE, M-PLE, and M-(PH)LE (Fig. 4d–e), and electrochemical/battery performance metrics are reported for M- variants, not (PTC)L. Therefore, no quantitative performance specific to (PTC)L can be extracted.

The paper prepares PVDF-HFP/LiTFSI ((PH)L) polymer membrane (Section 2.3) and then states: "(PTC)LE, (PH)LE, PLE, (PTC)L, (PH)L and PL GPEs were acquired by the same method" (Section 2.4). Cells were assembled for lithium metal battery testing: "The Li symmetric cells were assembled by sandwiching the gel polymer electrolyte between two Li metals" and Li//LFP coin cells (Section 2.5). Performance values reported in the text are given for M-(PTC)LE, M-(PH)LE, and M-PLE; no specific numeric electrochemical metrics are provided for (PH)L, so capacity/selectivity/breakthrough/regeneration are left null.

The study explicitly prepares PVDF/LiTFSI (PL) membranes and derives PL GPEs: “PVDF-HFP/LiTFSI((PH)L) and PVDF/LiTFSI (PL), PVDF-HFP/LiTFSI/EC ((PH)LE) and PVDF/LiTFSI/EC (PLE)… were prepared…” and “(PTC)LE, (PH)LE, PLE, (PTC)L, (PH)L and PL GPEs were acquired by the same method.” It also mentions “The impedance plots of all kinds of GPEs” but provides explicit numerical performance only for M-(PTC)LE, M-PLE, and M-(PH)LE, not for PL. Therefore, PL is confirmed as a gel polymer electrolyte for Li+ conduction, but no quantitative performance specific to PL is reported in the provided text.

Evidence specific to (PH)LE (PVDF-HFP/LiTFSI/EC): “PVDF-HFP/LiTFSI/EC ((PH)LE) … were prepared by replacing P(VDF-TrFE-CTFE) with PVDF-HFP …” (Section 2.3). “(PTC)LE, (PH)LE, PLE, (PTC)L, (PH)L and PL GPEs were acquired by the same method.” (Section 2.4), indicating (PH)LE was used as a gel polymer electrolyte. Figure 5 caption notes: “Dielectric constants of PLE, (PH)LE and (PTC)LE vs. frequency at 25 °C (Fig. 5c)”, but no numerical values are provided in text. Quantitative ionic conductivity values reported in the text are for M-(PTC)LE, M-PLE, and M-(PH)LE GPEs (e.g., “M-(pH)LE GPE with 5.9 × 10^{-4} S cm^{-1}”), not for (PH)LE, so no numeric performance can be extracted for (PH)LE.

PLE is explicitly prepared and tested as a gel polymer electrolyte sample: "PVDF-HFP/LiTFSI/EC ((PH)LE) and PVDF/LiTFSI/EC (PLE), MIL-125 (Ti)-PVDF-HFP/LiTFSI/EC (M-(PH) LE) and MIL-125 (Ti)-PVDF/LiTFSI/EC (M-PLE) were prepared..." and "(PTC)LE, (PH)LE, PLE, (PTC)L, (PH)L and PL GPEs were acquired by the same method." Performance figures include PLE in dielectric-constant comparisons: "Dielectric constants of PLE, (PH)LE and (PTC)LE vs. frequency at 25 °C." However, numerical ionic conductivity and electrochemical performance values reported in the text are for M-variants (e.g., "In Fig. 4d, M-(PTC)LE GPE ... 1.7 × 10^-3 S cm^-1, followed by M-PLE GPE with 1.0 × 10^-3 S cm^-1"), and no explicit numeric values for PLE (non-M) are provided in the text. Therefore, only the application scenario is recorded for PLE, with no extractable numeric performance.

Ionic conductivity for M-(PH)LE GPE at 25 °C is given as 5.9×10^-4 S cm^-1 (Fig. 4d,e: 'M-(PTC)LE ... 1.7×10^-3 S cm^-1, ... M-PLE ... 1.0×10^-3 S cm^-1, and finally M-(pH)LE GPE with 5.9×10^-4 S cm^-1'). Additional related data for M-(PH)LE: electrochemical window 3.6 V (Fig. S28) and Li//LFP discharge capacity 147.3 mAh/g at 1 C at the 240th cycle (Conclusion), but ionic conductivity directly characterizes the electrolyte.

For M-PLE (MIL-125 (Ti)-PVDF/LiTFSI/EC) gel polymer electrolyte: the ionic conductivity is reported as 1.0 × 10^-3 S cm^-1 at 25 °C (Fig. 4d,e: “M-PLE GPE with 1.0 × 10^-3 S cm^-1”). Additional related performance for the same material includes: electrochemical window 3.3 V (Figure S28); Li|M-PLE GPE|Li symmetric cell overpotential ~60 mV at 0.1 mA cm^-2 (Fig. 6a) and 50, 82, 116 mV at 0.1, 0.2, 0.5 mA cm^-2 (Fig. 6e); Li|M-PLE GPE|LFP delivers 140.8 mAh/g at 1 C at the 240th cycle (Conclusion).

PVH-IM is used as a solid polymer electrolyte for Li metal batteries. Li|PVH-IM|Li symmetric cells maintain stable cycling for 5550 h at 0.2 mA/cm^2, delivering a cumulative Li stripping/plating capacity of 1110 mAh/cm^2. PVH-IM also shows high ionic conductivity of 1.23e-3 S/cm at 25°C, CCD of 1.7 mA/cm^2, and enables LFP|PVH-IM|Li full cells with 95.7% capacity retention after 130 cycles at 0.1C and 80.0% after 280 cycles at 0.5C.

B2-CPE is used as the electrolyte in LiFePO4|B2-CPE|Li cells. The paper states: "the ionic conductivity of B2 membrane is calculated to be 9.4 mS cm−1 as shown from the impedance data represented in Fig. 6b." It also reports: "The Li+ transference number was computed using Eq. (5) and found to be 0.23 for the B2 composite polymer electrolyte." Additional cell performance with B2-CPE: "The initial discharge capacity is observed to be approximately 144.3 mAh g−1 ... capacity retention value reached up to about 98.4% after 50 cycles" at 0.2 C.

S1 is PVdF/P(VdF-HFP)/SiO2 = 95/0/5. Table 1 lists ionic conductivity σ = 4.01 mS cm-1 for S1 at room temperature in LiFePO4|Li cells using 1 M LiPF6 (EC/DMC = 50/50). Other S1 metrics reported include UE = 145%, L = 17%, S = 0%, η = 4.67 MPa, but no discharge cycling or Li+ transference number was measured for S1; those were for S5/S6.

From Table 1: S2 (PVdF/P(VdF-HFP)/SiO2 = 85/10/5) shows ionic conductivity σ = 4.8 mS cm-1 for LiFePO4|Li at room temperature (membrane radius 0.925 cm). Other S2 parameters listed include t = 113 μm, Rb = 87 Ω, porosity = 73.7%, electrolyte uptake = 191%, leakage = 16.3%, shrinkage = 2%.

Table 1 lists S3 (PVdF/P(VdF-HFP)/SiO2 = 75/20/5) with σ (ionic conductivity) = 6.4 mS cm-1 for the LiFePO4|Li system at room temperature; ionic conductivity measurements were performed in CR-2032 coin cells using 1 M LiPF6 (EC/DMC = 50/50) via EIS.

Table 1 reports S4 (PVdF/P(VdF-HFP)/SiO2 = 65/30/5) with ionic conductivity σ = 8.6 mS cm-1 for LiFePO4|Li at room temperature (membrane radius 0.925 cm). Additional S4 metrics listed include porosity 78.6%, electrolyte uptake 320%, and leakage 14.3%. No specific cycling/discharge data are provided for S4 (only S5/S6 are evaluated for charge–discharge).

Table 1 and Fig. 7a report S5-CPE ionic conductivity σ = 9.9 mS cm^-1 at room temperature in LiFePO4|S5-CPE|Li cells. Fig. 7b–c show LiFePO4|S5-CPE|Li delivers 157 mAh g^-1 at 0.2C initially and retains ~92% (144.5 mAh g^-1) after 500 cycles. Fig. 9a–b give Li+ transference number t+ = 0.28 (Li/S5-CPE/Li symmetric cell), and Fig. 9d shows an electrochemical stability window ~4.5 V.

Table 1 lists S6 (PVdF/P(VdF-HFP)/SiO2 = 45/50/5) with ionic conductivity σ = 9.1 mS cm-1 for LiFePO4|Li at room temperature (membrane radius 0.925 cm). Figure 8a shows LiFePO4|S6-CPE|Li initial discharge capacities of 148.3, 141.5, 129.5, and 108.6 mAh g-1 at 0.2, 0.5, 1, and 2C, respectively.

Table 1 lists S7 (PVdF/P(VdF-HFP)/SiO2 = 35/60/5) with ionic conductivity σ = 5.5 mS cm−1 for the LiFePO4|Li system at room temperature. The Experimental and Results sections state that ionic conductivity of all membrane samples was measured in LiFePO4|Li coin cells using 1 M LiPF6 (EC/DMC), indicating S7 functions as a composite polymer electrolyte for Li+ conduction.

S8 is the P(VdF-HFP)/SiO2 membrane (PVdF/P(VdF-HFP)/SiO2 = 0/95/5). Used as a composite polymer electrolyte/separator in LiFePO4|CPE|Li coin cells with 1 M LiPF6 (EC/DMC=50/50) at room temperature. Table 1 reports for S8: ionic conductivity σ = 4.3 mS cm-1 (Rb = 93 Ω, thickness = 108 μm). Additional S8 properties listed in Table 1: porosity 69 %, electrolyte uptake 182 %, leakage 9.6 %, thermal shrinkage 20 %.

Ionic conductivity of PDOL@ZnO/PVDF-HFP SE: "At 30 °C, the ionic conductivity of PDOL@ZnO/PVDF-HFP SE is as high as 0.22 mS cm^-1" (Fig. 3e). Additional performance with this SE: oxidation voltage 4.8 V vs Li+/Li (Fig. 3f); Li+ transference number tLi+ = 0.59 (Fig. 3h); Li/SE/Li symmetric cycling >1400 h at 0.5 mA cm^-2 with 0.5 mAh cm^-2 per cycle, and >900 h at 1 mA cm^-2 with 1 mAh cm^-2 per cycle; CCD = 1.5 mA cm^-2 (Fig. 4a–c). Full LFP/Li cells using this SE: initial 158 mAh g^-1 at 0.2 C; 144 mAh g^-1 after 600 cycles with 91.3% retention; pouch cell 149 mAh g^-1 with 71.2% retention over 200 cycles (Fig. 6). No regeneration conditions reported.

NSPE (SiO2) is evaluated as a Li+-ion conducting solid polymer electrolyte/separator. Table 2 gives σdc = 0.51×10−5 S cm−1 (i.e., 5.1×10−6 S/cm) and t_ion = 0.9894 (t_e = 0.0106). Table 1 reports Vs = 3.09 V and VESW = 6.18 V; the text states an ESW around 6 V is high enough for solid-state LIB electrolyte/separator use, with measurements in SS/NSPE/SS cells at room temperature.

NSPE (Al2O3) is a PEO-PMMA-LiClO4 solid polymer electrolyte with 3 wt% Al2O3 nanofiller, evaluated as an electrolyte/separator for solid-state LIBs. Table 1 reports Vs=3.00 V and VESW=6.00 V for NSPE (Al2O3), indicating adequate electrochemical stability. Table 2 gives t_ion=0.9836 (electronic transference number 0.0164) and σdc=0.38×10^-5 S cm^-1 (=3.8×10^-6 S/cm) at room temperature. The paper concludes these films are suitable as electrolyte/separator for solid-state rechargeable LIBs due to high ion transference number and ~6 V stability window.

NSPE (ZnO) is a Li+-ion conducting solid polymer electrolyte film evaluated in SS/NSPE/SS cells. It shows a conduction stability voltage Vs = 3.03 V and an electrochemical stability window VESW = 6.06 V (Table 1), with symmetric CVs from −3 to 3 V indicating reversibility over this window. Chronoamperometry yields t_ion = 0.9857 (t_e = 0.0133), I0 = 13.2 ×10^-6 A and ISS = 18.8 ×10^-8 A (Table 2), confirming predominantly ionic conduction suitable for solid-state electrochemical cells. EIS gives σdc = 1.67 ×10^-5 S/cm and τs = 0.65 ×10^-6 s (Table 2). The paper states these films (including NSPE (ZnO)) have ~6 V ESW and ion transference numbers close to unity, supporting their use as electrolyte/separator for solid-state rechargeable LIBs.

NSPE (SnO2) denotes the (PEO-PMMA)-LiClO4 solid polymer electrolyte with 3 wt% SnO2 nanofiller. Table 2 reports for NSPE (SnO2): σdc = 1.00 × 10^-5 S cm^-1 (room temperature), t_ion = 0.9881 (electronic transference number 0.0119), I0 = 15.8 ×10^-6 A, ISS = 18.8 ×10^-8 A, and τs = 0.87 ×10^-6 s. Table 1 gives electrochemical stability data: Vs = 3.05 V and VESW = 6.10 V. The paper concludes these films have an ESW around 6 V and ion transference numbers close to unity, confirming suitability as electrolyte/separator for solid-state rechargeable LIBs.

PCUF is a quasi‑solid‑state polymer electrolyte membrane for sodium‑metal batteries. Reported PCUF metrics directly tied to electrolyte function include: Na+ ionic conductivity at room temperature σNa+ = 2.590×10^-4 S cm^-1 and Na+ transference number t_Na+ = 0.910; electrochemical stability window 4.97 V; Na|PCUF|Na symmetric cell polarization ~58.6 mV with stable cycling >2000 h at 1 mA/cm^2. Cell-level performance with PCUF: NVP|PCUF|Na delivers 87.4 mAh/g at 0.5 C with 99.75% CE over 4000 cycles; at 1 C maintains 82.5 mAh/g after 2004 cycles; at 85 °C retains 92.5 mAh/g after 480 cycles with 99.6% CE. Pouch cell with PCUF: initial 82.0 mAh/g, 89.3% retention after 100 cycles; self‑discharge retention 98.5% after 72 h at 30 °C; ARC T2 = 125.1 °C, thermal runaway onset time 659 min; nail penetration and flame tests show no fire/explosion and continued operation. No applicable selectivity, breakthrough (t50), or regeneration data for PCUF as an electrolyte.

PCU is the PVDF-HFP/Ce-BDC-F0/NaClO4 polymer membrane used as a quasi-solid-state electrolyte. At room temperature, the sodium-ion conductivities for PH, PCU, and PCUF were 0.432×10^-4, 1.036×10^-4, and 2.590×10^-4 S cm^-1, respectively, so PCU = 1.036×10^-4 S cm^-1. Additional PCU-specific performance supporting its role as an electrolyte includes t_Na+ = 0.670 and an electrochemical window of 4.76 V. In NVP|PCU|Na cells, a specific capacity of 58.5 mAh g^-1 at 0.5C and 40.5 mAh g^-1 after 2004 cycles at 1C were reported. The PCU membrane decomposition temperature was 480.9 °C.

QSPE-S (PEGDA-based quasi-solid polymer electrolyte without TFMA) was tested in Li-CO2 batteries. Full discharge capacity for QSPE-S is 1.73 mAh cm^-2 (Figure S6): "the discharge capacity in QSPE-S, and QSPE is 1.73 mAh cm^-2 and 2.91 mAh cm^-2, respectively." Additional electrochemical properties specific to QSPE-S include: Li+ transference number t_Li+ = 0.34 (Fig. 2c, 2d, S3) and an oxidation current appearing at 5.19 V in LSV (Fig. 2b): "the initial decomposition potential of QSPE-S is lower than QSPE, and a significant oxidation current appeared at 5.19 V."

The Li–CO2 batteries were evaluated using cellulose carbon aerogel-supported Ru nanoparticles (Ru@CCA) as cathode catalysts. “The electrochemical properties of Li-CO2 batteries with LE and QSPE are evaluated using cellulose carbon aerogel-supported Ru nanoparticles (Ru@CCA) as cathode catalysts (Figure S5).” For full discharge at 50 μA cm−2 (to 2.0 V), “the discharge capacity in LE and QSPE is 5.37 mAh cm−2 and 2.91 mAh cm−2, respectively.” Ru@CCA’s reversibility is noted: “Figure S7 shows that the surface of the Ru@CCA cathode can be fully exposed after ten cycles, indicating that the Ru@CCA cathode ensures good reversibility for both LE and QSPE-based Li-CO2 batteries.” Additionally, with Ru@CCA, cycling at 50 μA cm−2 and DOD 100 μAh cm−2 gave “200 cycles (843 h)” for LE and “295 cycles (1250 h)” for QSPE, with overpotentials of “1.22 V” (LE) and “1.05 V” (QSPE).

Poly(pentanediol adipate) corresponds to the C6–C5 polyester backbone in the paper. For the LiTFSI-containing SPE made from this polymer with trifluoroacetate end groups (C6-C5-3F-Li), the room-temperature Li+ conductivity reached 0.59 × 10^-4 S cm^-1 when Mw was reduced to 6k (“when the Mw reduces to 6k, the room-temperature Li+ conductivities of C6-C5-3F-Li … reach 0.59 × 10^-4 … S cm^-1”). As an electrolyte, it enabled Li//Li symmetric cells with stable overpotentials for >1500 h and Li/C6-C5-3F-Li/LMFP (30 °C) cells with 144 mA h g^-1 at 0.2C and 95% capacity retention after 300 cycles; oxidative stability >4.5 V was also reported for C6-C5-3F-Li. These metrics are specific to the C6–C5 system (poly(pentanediol adipate)) in the study.

P(BA-co-HFBA-co-mPEG) was used as a solid polymer electrolyte (with LiTFSI). At 30 °C and optimized LiTFSI mass ratio 0.15, the Li+ conductivity was 1.3×10^-4 S cm^-1. The Li+ transference number for the P(BA-co-HFBA-co-mPEG) film SPE was 0.50. Additional application evidence: the film remained electrochemically stable up to 5.4 V and enabled Li/P(BA-co-HFBA-co-mPEG)/LiFePO4 cells with 98% capacity retention after 100 cycles at 0.05 C at room temperature.

F-BtTp@PP (fluorinated porous covalent polymer nanofilm on PP) served as a functional separator in Li-metal batteries. Li|Cu cells at 0.5 mA/cm^2 and 1 mAh/cm^2 showed CE = 98.4% after 140 cycles. Li|Li symmetric cells cycled ~3000 h at 10 mA/cm^2, 1 mAh/cm^2, and reached 5600 h at 20 mA/cm^2, 10 mAh/cm^2 with polarization 52.5 mV (also summarized as 52.2 mV). In Li|LiFePO4 full cells, first-cycle discharge capacity was 151.52 mAh/g at 0.1C and capacity retention was 88.76% after 400 cycles at 1C. In Li|S full cells at 0.5C, F-BtTp@PP delivered higher specific capacity and lower decay than bare PP.

F-BtTp-Nfilm is used to fabricate a fluorinated sandwich separator (F-BtTp@PP) for Li metal batteries. The Li+ transference number increased from 0.38 to 0.87 with F-BtTp-Nfilm (Fig. S9; text: “tLi+ increased preeminently from 0.38 to 0.87”). In Li|Cu cells at 0.5 mA cm^-2 with 1 mAh cm^-2, CE with F-BtTp@PP remains 98.4% after 140 cycles (Fig. 3a). In Li|Li symmetric cells, the modified battery can run smoothly for 5600 h at 20 mA cm^-2 and 10 mAh cm^-2 (Fig. 3d; conclusion reiterates 5600 h).

P-PMIA is defined as the double-layer PMIA membrane with 0 wt% TiO2 used as a gel polymer electrolyte (GPE) separator: “the obtained double-layer PMIA membranes with different TiO2 contents (0, 2 wt%, 4 wt%, 6 wt%, 8 wt% …) were referred to P-PMIA, 2T-PMIA, 4T-PMIA, 6T-PMIA and 8T-PMIA, respectively.” The work tested “the battery assembly and the electrochemical performances test of the lithium-metal cells using the obtained multilevel GPE membranes.” In LCO/lithium cells: “the LCO/T-PMIA/lithium cell exhibited the highest initial discharge capacity when compared with the Celgard, PMIA and P-PMIA separators,” implying P-PMIA was a tested separator but without numeric values reported for P-PMIA. In lithium–sulfur cells: “the initial discharge capacity acquired a significant increase when the battery assembled with P-PMIA separator instead of Celgard and PMIA separators,” again without explicit numerical capacity for P-PMIA. Since no quantitative performance values specific to P-PMIA are provided in text/tables/figure captions, capacity and other metrics are left null.

The work fabricates a gel polymer electrolyte (GPE) separator based on PMIA with TiO2; the obtained double-layer PMIA membranes with different TiO2 contents (0, 2 wt%, 4 wt%, 6 wt%, 8 wt%) were referred to ... 2T-PMIA, 4T-PMIA, 6T-PMIA and 8T-PMIA. The separators are used in lithium‑metal batteries: ‘battery assembly and the electrochemical performances test of the lithium-metal cells using the obtained multilevel GPE membranes’. General statements apply to all T‑PMIA variants (thus to 2T‑PMIA): ‘all the T-PMIA membranes displayed much smaller average pore sizes compared with the P-PMIA sample’ and ‘all T-PMIA membranes exhibited the reduced contact angles to liquid electrolyte’. The paper positions T‑PMIA as lithium battery separators: ‘the upgraded designing of the versatile multi-scale T-PMIA separator delivered the prominent dendrite-blocking and polysulfide-inhibiting effects, which would be truly viewed as the ideal separators for advanced lithium-metal cells’. The main text does not provide numeric electrochemical performance values specifically for the 2T‑PMIA sample; therefore metrics are left null.

The paper defines 8T-PMIA as an electrospun PMIA separator with 8 wt% TiO2 ("... referred to ... 8T-PMIA, respectively."). It evaluates separators in lithium-metal batteries (LCO/Li and Li–S) using T-PMIA membranes, but battery performance numbers are given for 6T-PMIA or unspecified T-PMIA, not explicitly for 8T-PMIA. Examples: LCO/Li tests are for "the multi-scale 6T-PMIA-based GPE"; Li–S high-T retention: "the 6T-PMIA-based cell possessed the much higher capacity retention rate of 73.9%"; symmetric Li–Li cell stability (400 h) and LSV decomposition voltage (5.3 V) are stated for "T-PMIA" without specifying 8T. Although a property (porosity) is reported for 8T-PMIA ("8T-PMIA membranes could reach the maximum value of 89.33%"), no explicit cell performance metrics (capacity, rate, long-cycle values) are provided for 8T-PMIA specifically. Therefore, application scenario is battery separator, but performance fields are left null per the "宁缺毋滥" rule.

The pristine PMIA electrospun membrane (denoted as PMIA) was used as a contrast separator in lithium‑metal cells. The paper assembled and tested Li–Li symmetric cells and LCO/Li and Li–S batteries with Celgard, PMIA, P‑PMIA, and T‑PMIA separators (e.g., Fig. 6(c–e) voltage profiles of Li–Li cells with Celgard, PMIA, P‑PMIA and T‑PMIA at 2.0 mA cm^-2 and 2.0 mAh cm^-2; Fig. 6(f–h) LCO/Li performance with various separators; Fig. 6(i–j) Li–S cells). However, the excerpt provides specific numeric performance only for T‑PMIA; no explicit numeric metrics are given for PMIA alone, so capacity/selectivity/breakthrough are left null.
The provided excerpts contain acknowledgements, references, and general notes without any sample-specific application or performance data linked to material P1.

No application/performance data specific to sample P2 appears in the provided text. The excerpt mainly includes acknowledgements, references, and brief experimental notes (e.g., removal/recycling of BTP; monomer reactivity; NMR signal overlap) without any measurable application metrics or scenarios linked to P2.
The provided text contains acknowledgements, keywords, and references only; no application/performance data specific to sample P3 is present.

No direct information about sample P4 or its application/performance is present in the provided text. The excerpt contains acknowledgements, references, and brief general notes ([18]–[22]) without any sample codes, performance metrics, scenarios, or regeneration details tied to P4.

The provided content includes acknowledgements, references, and brief synthetic/characterization notes ([18]–[21]) without any application or performance data tied to sample P5. No catalysis, separation, ion-exchange, or other application metrics are reported for P5.

PCG1 SPE (C=O:Li=1:1) is explicitly referenced for lithium battery electrolyte use: 'Lithium-ion transference number results for PCG1 (molar ratio C=O:Li=1:1) SPE were shown in Figure S3' and 'The cycling performance of PCG0 SPE, PCG1 SPE, PCG5 SPE, and PCG10 SPE at 1 C (25 °C, 2.5–4.0 V) is presented in Figure 6g.' However, no numeric performance values for PCG1 (e.g., conductivity, tLi+, electrochemical window, capacity) are given in the text; thus fields are left null.

PCG5 SPE is used as the solid polymer electrolyte in Li|PCG5 SPE|Li and LiFePO4|PCG5 SPE|Li cells. The paper reports: "Notably, the Li|PCG5 SPE|Li system exhibited excellent cycling stability ... maintaining an overpotential of 50 mV and cycling stably over 1800 h" and for full cells: "The capacity of the LiFePO4|PCG5 SPE|Li remains stable after 1000 cycles ... The capacity ... was at 122.0 mAh g−1 after 1000 cycles (a capacity retention rate exceeding 91.3% ...)." These confirm the application (battery electrolyte for Li+) and provide the extracted capacity value.

PCG10 SPE is a solid polymer electrolyte variant for lithium metal batteries: “samples were named respectively PCG1 SPE, PCG5 SPE, PCG10 SPE” and “These attributes position PCG SPE as a promising candidate for lithium metal batteries.” For PCG10-specific performance, the paper states: “Notably, the PCG10 (molar ratio C = O:Li = 10:1) SPE exhibits the highest ionic conductivity,” while also noting its activation energy is larger than PCG5 due to stronger complexation. No numerical ionic conductivity value for PCG10 is provided in the text or figure captions here, so quantitative fields are left null.

Explicit data for PCG0 SPE: (1) Ionic conductivity: "the room temperature ionic conductivity of PCG0 SPE is only 1.8×10^-4 S cm^-1." (2) Lithium-ion transference number: "Compared to PCG0 SPE (t_Li+ = 0.34)..." Additional performance context: Li|PCG0 SPE|Li showed "a high overpotential of 600 mV after 270 h" and LiFePO4|PCG0 SPE|Li had capacity "merely 10.1 mAh g^-1 after 400 cycles."

PEMVH-A is the composite gel polymer electrolyte (PEO/PMMA/P(VDF-HFP)) filled with nano-sized γ-Al2O3 (defined in Experimental 2.1). For Li-based cells: Table 2(a) reports for PEMVH-A in 1 M LiPF6/EC-DMC at 25 °C: liquid absorbability 96%, Rb = 4.40 Ω, ionic conductivity 0.91 mS cm-1, and Li+ transport number tLi+ = 0.75. Symmetric Li/GPE/Li cycling (Fig. 4a) at 0.25 mA cm-2 shows a maximum overpotential of ~35 mV, stable even after 1200 h. Full-cell Li/GPE/LiCoO2 (Fig. 4b,d) at 0.1 mA cm-2 delivers initial discharge capacity 152.9 mAh g-1 and 145.1 mAh g-1 after 100 cycles (95% retention); at 1.0 mA cm-2, discharge capacity is 141.2 mAh g-1.

PEMVH-T is a TiO2-filled composite gel polymer electrolyte evaluated for Li batteries. Table 2(a) reports for PEMVH-T (immersed in 1 M LiPF6/EC-DMC): Rb = 3.15 Ω, ionic conductivity at 25 °C = 1.27 mS cm-1, and Li+ transport number tLi+ = 0.79. In symmetric Li/GPE/Li cycling at 0.25 mA cm-2, the PEMVH-T cell showed early instability (~80 h, max ~60 mV) then stabilized at ~20 mV in subsequent cycles. In Li/GPE/LiCoO2 full cells at 0.1 mA cm-2, initial discharge capacity = 155.1 mAh g-1 and 150.1 mAh g-1 after 100 cycles (97% retention); at 1.0 mA cm-2, discharge capacity = 141.4 mAh g-1.

PEMVH-S is a SiO2-filled gel polymer electrolyte used in Li metal batteries. Table 2(a) reports ionic conductivity at 25 °C of 2.02 mS cm-1 (Rb = 1.98 Ω; Li+ transport number tLi+ = 0.84; liquid absorbability 113%). LSV shows PEMVH-S//LiPF6/EC-DMC is electrochemically stable up to 5.3 V vs Li/Li+. In Li symmetric cells (0.25 mA cm-2, 1 h plating/stripping), the overpotential is ~10 mV and remains stable even after 1200 h. In Li/GPE/LiCoO2 full cells (0.1 mA cm-2), initial discharge capacity is 155.5 mAh g-1 and after 100 cycles 153.6 mAh g-1 (99% retention), indicating suitability as a Li battery electrolyte.

R&D text states: "The membrane porosity increases with the weight percentage of PMMA in the matrix and reaches maximum 56.8% when PMMA content is 60%, ... PMMA-60 can entrap as much as 234 wt% of liquid." The liquid is the ionic liquid [BMIM]BF4 used for electrolyte uptake.

MIL-60 is a gel polymer electrolyte used in Li/MIL-60/LiFePO4 cells. It shows the maximum ionic conductivity about 1.4 × 10^−3 S·cm^−1 at 30 °C ("MIL-60 shows the maximum ionic conductivity about 1.4 × 10^−3 S·cm^−1 at 30 °C"). It also has the lowest Li+ conduction activation energy (5.3 kJ·mol^−1). Electrochemical stability window is up to 4.5 V vs. Li+/Li. In full-cell tests (Li/MIL-60/LiFePO4), first-discharge capacities are 157, 142, and 137 mAh·g^−1 at 0.1C, 0.2C, and 0.5C (30 °C), with capacity retention of 96% (0.1C) and 95% (0.2C) at the 50th cycle, and interfacial resistance stabilizing around 900 Ω after 144 h. These results directly pertain to MIL-60.

GPE-1 is PVDF-HFP: PMMA (9:1) with EC:DEC plasticizer and NaCF3SO3 salt (Table I). EIS gives Rb = 50 Ω for GPE-1, and Table III reports its room-temperature ionic conductivity as 1.0 × 10^-3 S cm^-1, with t_ion = 0.98 and t_Na+ = 0.36. Battery cycling performance was demonstrated only for the optimized GPE-2; no cell performance data were provided for GPE-1.

Results & Discussion report: "Among the prepared GPEs, GPE-2 (PVDF-HFP: PMMA - EC: DEC - NaClO4) exhibited the highest ionic conductivity at room temperature, 1.2 × 10^-3 S cm^-1, and the lowest activation energy of 0.25 eV." LSV: "confirmed that the GPEs are stable up to 4.8 V." Suitability for SIBs demonstrated: "fabricating a sodium-ion coin cell... The optimum performance was obtained with hard carbon (HC) anode, demonstrating a stable charge/discharge capacity of 190 mAh g^-1 after 100 cycles."

GPE-3 is defined as PVDF-HFP: PMMA (9:1) + EC: DEC with NaBF4 (Table I). Table III reports for GPE-3: conductivity σ = 7.4 × 10−4 S cm−1, t_ion = 0.99, t_Na+ = 0.27, Ea = 0.30 eV, Xc = 34.28 ± 0.74%. The EIS section states Rb for GPE-3 = 63 Ω, and overall LSV notes that the GPEs are stable up to 4.8 V, but coin-cell performance was only demonstrated for GPE-2.

GPE-4 (PVDF-HFP: PMMA (9:1) + EC:DEC, NaPF6) is one of the gel polymer electrolytes prepared for sodium-ion batteries. Table III reports for GPE-4: ionic conductivity σ = "3.9 × 10-4" S cm-1 (i.e., 3.9e-4 S/cm), total ionic transference number t_ion = 0.95, Na+ transference number tNa+ = 0.25, and activation energy Ea = 0.33 eV. EIS section also lists the bulk resistance for GPE-4 as 165 Ω. No coin-cell cycling data were provided for GPE-4; battery tests used the optimized GPE-2.

iHSE is used as the in-situ hybrid solid electrolyte in LFP/iHSE/Li solid-state cells. Performance reported specifically for LFP/iHSE/Li: "At a discharge rate of 0.1C, the battery attained a discharge-specific capacity of 159 mAh·g−1"; rate tests show recovery to 155 mAh·g−1 at 0.1C after high-rate cycling; cycling at room temperature shows capacity retention of 95.4% and 99.4% at 0.1C and 0.5C respectively; long-term cycling at 1C for 1000 cycles gives a capacity retention of 67.3% for LFP/iHSE/Li. These results directly pertain to the iHSE-based full cell (LFP/iHSE/Li).

Ionic conductivity: “We could calculate ionic conductivity by eqn (3), the gel polymer electrolyte film has an ionic conductivity of 4.2×10^-3 Scm^-1 (GPE without in situ SiO2) …” (Fig. 6). Context of use in Li batteries: LSV and Li/PE/SS blocking cell measurements (Sections 2.4–2.5); cell test: “The gel polymer electrolyte film (GPE without in situ SiO2) delivers a charge capacity of 164.84 mAh g^-1 and discharge capacity of 162.15 mAh g^-1” in Li/GPE/LiFePO4 (Section 3.7). Electrochemical stability: “electrochemical stability of gel polymer electrolyte without in situ SiO2 is at 5.4 V” (Fig. 7).

For sample (b) with 1% in situ SiO2: In Li/GPE/LiFePO4 cell, the first discharge capacity is 164.98 mA h g^-1 and charge capacity is 166.80 mA h g^-1 (Section 3.7, Fig. 9). Related electrolyte performance for this sample includes ionic conductivity 6.8×10^-3 S cm^-1 (Section 3.4: “GPE with 1% in situ SiO2”), electrochemical stability 5.8 V (Section 3.5: “GPE with 1% in situ SiO2”), and electrolyte uptake 295% at 2 min, reaching 360% at 15 min (Section 3.4, Fig. 5).

Specific to sample (c) with 2% in situ SiO2: “We could calculate ionic conductivity by eqn (3), the gel polymer electrolyte film has an ionic conductivity of … 8.5×10^-3 Scm^-1 (GPE with 2% in situ SiO2) …” (Section 3.4, Fig. 6). The material is evaluated as a gel polymer electrolyte for Li-ion batteries (“The high stability of these polymer electrolytes indicate that them are very suitable for applications in lithium-ion battery.”) and shows electrochemical stability up to 5.9 V for the 2% sample (Section 3.5). In Li/GPE/LiFePO4 cells, the 2% sample delivers first discharge capacity 166.58 mA h g^-1 and strong cycling (Section 3.7).

Sample (d) with 3% in situ SiO2 (PVDF-HFP/TPU/PMMA GPE): electrolyte uptake 350% within 2 min and 415% after 15 min; ionic conductivity 5.5×10^-3 S/cm; electrochemical stability window 5.6 V; Li/PE/LiFePO4 first-cycle capacities: charge 166.50 mAh/g, discharge 164.38 mAh/g (2.0–4.5 V, 25°C).

PP is defined as pure PVdF-HFP (Table 1). No application performance (e.g., ionic conductivity data, cell testing) is reported for PP. Conductivity values and battery cell assembly are presented for PL-35 and PEMA-blended samples (Table 3; Fig. 5; Section 4: LiV3O8/PE-5/Li cell), not for PP. For PP, only morphology and porosity (45%) are discussed, which are not application performance metrics per the schema.

In Table 1, 'PE' is defined as 'Pure PEMA'. The reported performance data (ionic conductivity, porosity, thermal analysis, and cell testing) are for blended/plasticized films (e.g., PL-35, PE-5, PE-10, etc.). The lithium metal polymer cell is explicitly assembled with PE-5 (LiV3O8/PE-5/Li). No ionic conductivity or battery performance metrics are provided for pure PEMA (PE), so no application-specific values can be extracted for PE.

PS (85 wt% PVdF-HFP + 15 wt% LiClO4; Table 1) is a gel polymer electrolyte. Electrical studies report: "The conductivity of the polymer electrolyte increases from 6.45 × 10^-5 S/cm for PS to 3.15 × 10^-3 S/cm for PL-35 (Fig. 4(b))." AC impedance measured 40 Hz–1 MHz at room temperature (303 K). The study focuses on lithium metal polymer battery electrolytes.

PL-35 (50 wt% PVdF-HFP + 35 wt% EC + 15 wt% LiClO4) is reported as a plasticized polymer electrolyte. 3.4.1 and Fig. 4(b): conductivity increases from 6.45 × 10^-5 S/cm (PS) to 3.15 × 10^-3 S/cm for PL-35 at 303 K; Table 3 lists PL-35 conductivities (e.g., 3.15 × 10^-3 S/cm at 303 K, rising with temperature).

PE-5 is a gel polymer electrolyte (45 wt% PVdF-HFP + 5 wt% PEMA + 35 wt% EC + 15 wt% LiClO4). It showed the highest ionic conductivity: “The highest conductivity value of 2.14 × 10−2 S/cm was observed for PE-5 …” (Table 3; Fig. 5). PE-5 was also used as the electrolyte in LiV3O8/PE-5/Li coin cells (Section 2.3; Fig. 10), but the quantified electrolyte property directly reported for PE-5 is its ionic conductivity.

PE-10 is the polymer electrolyte with composition 40 wt% PVdF-HFP + 10 wt% PEMA + 35 wt% EC + 15 wt% LiClO4 (Table 1). Ionic conductivity determined by AC impedance at 303 K is 5.12 × 10^-4 S/cm (Table 3; also stated in Section 3.4.2.1). Measurements used stainless steel blocking electrodes over 40 Hz–1 MHz.

Table 3 reports ionic conductivity for PE-15: 1.33 × 10^-4 S/cm at 303 K (rising to 3.65 × 10^-4 S/cm at 353 K); Ea = 0.44 eV; porosity 73%. These values directly evidence Li+ conduction performance of PE-15.

PE-20 (30 wt% PVdF-HFP + 20 wt% PEMA + 35 wt% EC + 15 wt% LiClO4) is a gel polymer electrolyte evaluated for lithium-ion conduction. Table 3 reports its ionic conductivity as 3.91 × 10^-5 S/cm at 303 K, increasing with temperature to 6.75 × 10^-5 S/cm at 353 K; activation energy 0.47 eV; porosity 71%. Measurements used stainless steel blocking electrodes, 40 Hz–1 MHz, 303–353 K.

PE-25 is a PEMA-blended, plasticized PVdF-HFP gel polymer electrolyte (25 wt% PVdF-HFP + 25 wt% PEMA + 35 wt% EC + 15 wt% LiClO4; Table 1). Table 3 reports ionic conductivity values versus temperature; for PE-25 at 303 K the conductivity is 2.62 × 10^-5 S/cm (increasing to 5.21 × 10^-5 S/cm at 353 K). Measurements are AC impedance with blocking stainless-steel electrodes.

Table 3 reports ionic conductivity for PE-40 (10 wt% PVdF-HFP + 40 wt% PEMA + 35 wt% EC + 15 wt% LiClO4); at 303 K it is 1.51 × 10^-5 S/cm, measured by AC impedance with blocking stainless steel electrodes.

PPAL5-D is a composite polymer electrolyte for lithium metal batteries. Table 4 reports its room-temperature ionic conductivity: "PPAL5-D ... 25 °C: 3.47 (×10^-4 S cm^-1)" (i.e., 3.47×10^-4 S/cm). Additional PPAL5-D performance (context): t_Li+ = 0.61 (Fig. 5i); oxidative stability up to 5.2 V vs Li/Li+ (Fig. 5e); Li|PPAL5-D|Li shows CCD of 1.75 mA cm^-2 (Fig. 6d) and stable cycling >1200 h at 0.05 mA cm^-2 with ~20 mV overpotential after returning (Fig. 6e); LFP|PPAL5-D|Li delivers 160.9 mAh g^-1 at 0.5C after 300 cycles with 97.6% retention and >99.5% CE (Fig. 8b); at 1C after 400 cycles, 131.5 mAh g^-1 with 78.3% retention and >99.5% CE (Fig. 8d); NCM811|PPAL5-D|Li gives 170.2 mAh g^-1 at 0.5C after 100 cycles with ~99.3% CE (Fig. 9b).

Pure PVDF membrane was used as a porous support for polymer electrolytes formed by soaking in 1 M LiPF6 in DMC/EMC/EC (Section 2.3 and 2.5). Table 2 reports the electrolyte uptake of the pure PVDF membrane as 113% (HPE-g-MPEG/PVDF ratio 0). Ion conductivity was measured (Figs. 9–10), but no explicit numeric value for pure PVDF is provided in text/tables; only that the 3/10 blend reached 1.76e-3 S/cm at 20°C and was >4× that of pure PVDF (Conclusions).

The work prepares porous HPE-g-MPEG/PVDF membranes and then forms polymer electrolytes by soaking them in 1 M LiPF6 in DMC/EMC/EC (1/1/1, w/w/w). Table 2 reports electrolyte uptake for each composition; for HPE-g-MPEG/PVDF ratio 1/10 (w/w), electrolyte uptake is 149%. Ionic conductivity at 20 °C is discussed and plotted (Fig. 10), but no explicit numeric value is given for the 1/10 sample, so only electrolyte uptake is extracted for this material.

For HPE-g-MPEG/PVDF ratio 2/10 (w/w), Table 2 lists electrolyte uptake = 187% (porosity 52.1%). The electrochemical section states ion conductivity exceeded 10^-3 S/cm when the ratio reached 2/10 (w/w), measured at 20°C using 1 M LiPF6 in DMC/EMC/EC.

Polymer electrolyte for Li-ion batteries based on the HPE-g-MPEG/PVDF membrane. For the blend membrane with HPE-g-MPEG/PVDF ratio 3/10 (w/w), the ion conductivity was reported as 1.76×10^-3 S/cm at 20 °C (Conclusions). Electrochemical stability remained higher than 4.5 V vs Li+/Li for this highest HPE-g-MPEG content (Fig. 12, text). Supporting property: electrolyte uptake = 215% for the 3/10 sample (Table 2). Temperature dependence for the 3/10 sample shows conductivity >10^-3 S/cm even at 0 °C (Fig. 11, text).

PPK GPE (PEO/PVDF-HFP/Kevlar) is used as a gel polymer electrolyte for Li-based batteries. Reported performance directly for PPK GPE includes: ionic conductivity 2.815 mS cm−1 at 25 °C (“At 25 °C, the PPK GPE exhibits a high ionic conductivity of 2.815 mScm−1”). Li+ transference number t_Li+ = 0.571 (“The t_Li+ of PPK GPE (0.571) is higher…”). Electrochemical stability window 4.95 V (“Notably, the electrochemical stability window of the PPK GPE is 4.95 V”). Li||Li symmetric cell stability for 1200 h at 0.5 mA cm−2 and 0.5 mAh cm−2 (“the PPK GPE achieves high stability during long-term cycling of 1200 h at a current density of 0.5 mAcm−2 and a capacity of 0.5 mAhcm−2”). In LiFePO4||graphite full cells, CE ~99.92% and 80.5% capacity retention after 1150 cycles at 0.5 C; rate capacities 137.1, 123.1, and 92.6 mAh g−1 at 0.1, 0.5, and 1.0 C, respectively.

Table 1 lists CPE-Z (composite polymer electrolyte doped with Z particles) having ionic conductivity σ = 2.83 mS cm-1 and lithium ion transference number TLi+ = 0.28 at room temperature: "CPE-Z ... σ/mS cm-1: 2.83 ... TLi+: 0.28". Additional Z-related context: LSV shows the CPE doped with Z stabilizes at 5.2 V ("the CPE membrane doped with the Z, PZ and sPZ particles can stabilize at 5.2, 4.9 and 5.0 V, respectively"), and thermal analysis notes "the CPE-Z membrane presents the highest decomposition temperature about 430°C". For cell performance comparison with graphite: "the coin cells assembled with the CPE-PZ and CPE-Z can only release ... 198.3 mAh g−1 at 1.0C after 120 cycles".

PZ denotes PMMA–ZrO2 hybrid particles prepared from commercial ZrO2; CPE-PZ is the P(VDF-HFP) composite polymer electrolyte doped with PZ. For Li/CPE-PZ/Graphite coin cells, the authors report: “the coin cells assembled with the CPE-PZ and CPE-Z can only release 208.7 and 198.3 mAh g−1 at 1.0C after 120 cycles” (main text). Supporting electrolyte metrics for CPE-PZ include σ = 3.26 mS cm−1 and TLi+ = 0.38 at room temperature (Table 1), and an electrochemical window of 4.9 V (Fig. 4C), confirming its application as a lithium-ion battery electrolyte.

sPZ denotes the PMMA–ZrO2 hybrid particles used to dope P(VDF-HFP) CPEs; the resulting membrane is labeled CPE-sPZ. Table 1 reports for CPE-sPZ at room temperature: ionic conductivity 3.59 mS cm-1 and Li+ transference number 0.41. The text states: “the value increases … finally up to 3.59 mS cm-1 for the CPE-sPZ.” Highlights also note assembled coin cells show excellent rate and cycle performance, and the CPE membranes maintain shape after 160 °C for 1 h.
CPE-0 is the undoped P(VDF-HFP) polymer electrolyte. Table 1 (row CPE-0) reports ionic conductivity 1.95 mS cm-1 at room temperature and Li+ transference number 0.16.

Table 1 (room temperature) for CPE-Z: ionic conductivity σ = 2.83 mS cm^-1; tensile strength T = 24.67 MPa; uptake A = 126.3%; activation energy Ea = 6.04 kJ mol^-1; lithium-ion transference number T_Li+ = 0.28. Fig. 4(C): the CPE membrane doped with Z particles stabilizes at 5.2 V (electrochemical window). Table 2 (Li/CPE/LiCoO2 cell): Rs = 2.114 Ω, Rp = 509.4 Ω. Additionally, the text notes coin cells assembled with CPE-Z can release 198.3 mAh g^-1 at 1.0C after 120 cycles (Figure S3, S4).

Table 1 (CPE-PZ): A/% 132.8; T/MPa 23.89; σ/mS cm-1 3.26; Ea/kJ mol-1 5.62; TLi+ 0.38. LSV: 'the CPE membrane doped with the Z, PZ and sPZ particles can stabilize at 5.2, 4.9 and 5.0 V, respectively.'

CPE-sPZ is a P(VDF-HFP)-based composite polymer electrolyte used in Li-ion coin cells. Evidence: “the assembled Li/CPE/LiCoO2 and Li/CPE/Graphite coin cells…,” and specifically, “the cell delivers high specific capacity about 126.4 mAh g−1 at 2.0 C after 150 cycles that keeps about 85.2% capacity retention ratio of the cell at 0.1 C.” Additional electrolyte-relevant performance for CPE-sPZ includes ionic conductivity 3.59 mS cm−1 and TLi+ = 0.41 (Table 1), electrochemical window ~5.0 V (LSV), and thermal shape retention after 160 °C for 1 h (Fig. 6; Highlights).

Direct evidence for P(VDF-HFP)-Li10-50LATP as a lithium battery electrolyte: “The P(VDF-HFP)-Li10-50LATP HSE exhibits the highest Li+ conductivity (7.88 × 10^-4 S cm^-1 at 25 °C) and low ion migration activation energy (0.18 eV) than other HSEs.” Also, “The decomposition voltage of the P(VDF-HFP)-Li10-50LATP HSE is 5.16 V,” and it shows the highest lithium transference number (tLi+ = 0.61). Symmetric Li cells with this HSE are stable for 1000 h with low polarization (29 mV at 0.1 mA cm^-2), and full cells (NCM622||Li, LNMO||Li) exhibit stable cycling, further confirming its function as a solid-state battery electrolyte.

Explicitly for the PVDF-[POSS-(PMMA46)8] GPE: “From Fig. 8, the ionic conductivity of GPE with POSS-(PMMA46) as a modifier is 4.85 mS cm−1,” measured for the LiPF6-based gel polymer electrolyte soaked into the PVDF/POSS-(PMMA46)8 electrospun membrane. Additional context: this GPE was used in Li/GPE/LiFePO4 coin cells and showed an electrochemical stability window of 6.01 V and improved interfacial resistance (256.15 Ω), but these metrics are not captured in the schema fields.

BPLE-1 is used as a solid polymer-in-salt electrolyte for Li metal batteries. Its room-temperature ionic conductivity is 1.6 × 10^-3 S cm^-1, increasing to 5.8 × 10^-3 S cm^-1 with temperature (Figure 3g); higher than BPLE-0 (8.1 × 10^-4 S cm^-1). Electrochemical stability window (ESW) reaches 4.8 V (Figure 3h). Li+ transference number t+ = 0.66 (Figure 3j; Figures S22–S23). Critical current density (CCD) is 2.4 mA cm^-2 (Figure 4a). Li||BPLE-1||Li symmetric cells show stable cycling for 800 h at 0.3 mA cm^-2 without short circuits (Figure 4c), with overpotentials of 23/50/56/63 mV at 0.05–0.3 mA cm^-2 (Figure 4b). Full-cell performance with LFP cathode: rate capacities at 0.1–5C of 161/157/152/149/143/133 mAh g^-1 with ~100% CE (Figure 5b); cycling at 0.5C: 154 mAh g^-1 initial, 98.7% retention after 300 cycles, avg CE 99.8% (Figure 5e); at 1C: 149 mAh g^-1 initial, 98.0% retention after 500 cycles, CE 99.8% (Figure 5g); at 2C: 142 mAh g^-1 initial, 84.5% retention after 800 cycles, CE 99.5% (Figure 5h); at 5C: 138 mAh g^-1, 75.4% retention after 800 cycles (Figure S32). BPLE-1 also supports NCM811||Li cycling: 142 mAh g^-1 after 100 cycles at 0.2C (Figure S38), and an LFP||BPLE-1||Li pouch cell shows stable cycling at 0.2C (Figure S39).

PHGB is a polymer electrolyte applied in LFP||Li batteries and Li||Li symmetric cells. Performance highlights directly tied to PHGB: (i) Rate capability (LFP||Li, PHGB, 46 μm): "The reversible specific capacities of the cells with PHGB polymer electrolytes are 160.15, 155.16, 139.60, 119.99, and 97.26 mAh g−1 at 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1.0, and 2.0 C, respectively. When recovered to 0.1 C, the reversible specific capacity is still 152.31 mAh g−1." (ii) Cycling at 0.1 C: "the specific capacity of the battery with PHGB polymer electrolyte reaches 153.94 mAh g−1 in the first cycle… 147.32 mAh g−1 with a capacity retention of 95.7% after 100 cycles at 0.1 C." (iii) High-rate cycling: "stably cycled for 500 cycles at 1 C… 120.19 mAh g−1 (1st) and 111.82 mAh g−1 (500th), capacity retention 93%." (iv) Symmetric cell stability: at 0.5 mA cm−2, PHGB shows an overpotential of ~15 mV after 120 h, indicating dendrite suppression. No explicit t50-type lifetime metric or any thermal/chemical regeneration procedure and recovery ratio for PHGB were reported; therefore 'breakthrough' and 'regeneration' are set to null.

PVDF-HFP polymer electrolyte was tested in LFP||Li coin cells with 1 M LiPF6 in EC/DMC/EMC (1:1:1). First-cycle discharge specific capacity: 151.95 mAh g^-1; after 100 cycles at 0.1 C: 95.49 mAh g^-1 with 62.8% capacity retention (Fig. 6c). Plateau voltage difference: 190 mV (Fig. 6b). Rate capacities: 160.35, 138.63, 115.07, 78.29, and 30.51 mAh g^-1 at 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1.0, and 2.0 C, respectively (Fig. 6e). Interfacial impedance Rf: 905.7 Ω (Table S1). Thermal images show significant shrinkage at 200 °C (Fig. 3a).

In LFP||Li coin cells, the PVDF-HFP/GO (PHG) polymer electrolyte shows first-cycle discharge specific capacity 145.14 mAhg^{-1} and after 100 cycles at 0.1 C retains 124.84 mAhg^{-1} with 86% capacity retention (Fig. 6c). The platform voltage difference for PHG is 179 mV (Fig. 6b). Interfacial impedance R_f for PHG is 239 Ω (Table S1). Utilization at 2 C for PHG is 53.72% (Fig. 6f).

Direct property for LLZTO pellet: "the ionic conductivity of LLZTO pellets is 3.24×10^-4 S cm^-1 at room temperature (Fig. S2)." Other performance data (electrochemical window, Li+ transference number, cycling) refer to composite electrolytes and not LLZTO alone, so they are not extracted.

For 50[PEO8-LiTFSI]-40LLZTO-10PVDF + 9% OX: “The highest ionic conductivity has reached 2.0×10^-4 S cm^-1 at 55 °C with the presence of 10% PVDF” (Fig. 1c); “The electronic conductivity … is only 4.9×10^-10 S cm^-1 at 55 °C” (Fig. S6); “the t+ … is 0.45 at 55 °C” (Fig. 1d); “no peak or apparent current are measured … from 2.5 to 5.0 V” for 50[PEO8-LiTFSI]-40LLZTO-10PVDF and +9% OX (Fig. 2a); Li|50[PEO8-LiTFSI]-40LLZTO-10PVDF+9% OX|Li symmetric cell “was still stable after 360 h” at 0.3 mA cm^-2 and 55 °C (Fig. 3b).

Conventional Al2O3@NCM (c-Al2O3@NCM) cathode in c-Al2O3@NCM|50[PEO8-LiTFSI]-40LLZTO-10PVDF+9% OX|Li coin cells (55 °C, 2.5–4.3 V) shows initial discharge capacity 142.9 mAh g^-1 and Coulombic efficiency 68.9% at 0.2 C (Fig. 5a). Under rate testing to 1 C, the conventional-cathode cell delivers 58.6 mAh g^-1 (Fig. 5b). Interfacial resistance for the conventional-cathode cell increases from 212 Ω (pristine) to 441, 1074, and 1564 Ω after 1, 25, and 50 cycles (Fig. 5e).

For f-Al2O3@NCM|50[PEO8-LiTFSI]-40LLZTO-10PVDF+9% OX|Li at 55 °C and 2.5–4.3 V (0.2 C), the cell with composite cathode framework shows an initial discharge capacity of 150.6 mAh g^-1 and Coulombic efficiency of 84.9%. Under rate testing, it delivers 103.7 mAh g^-1 at 1 C and recovers to 152 mAh g^-1 when returning to 0.1 C. In cycling at 0.2 C and 55 °C, the f-Al2O3@NCM cell is reported to decay from 140.2 to 34.5 mAh g^-1 over the same cycling times (text reports this explicitly for f-).

Li/Li symmetric battery with 0LLZTO/PVDF-PEO-LiTFSI electrolyte short-circuited after 854 h at 0.3 mA cm^-2 and 50 °C: "the voltage of Li/Li symmetric battery with 0LLZTO/PVDF-PEO-LiTFSI electrolyte decreases rapidly ... after 854 h and eventually causes the occurrence of a short-circuit phenomenon." For full cells at 1C and 50 °C: "The discharge capacity of the Li/0LLZTO/PVDF-PEO-LiTFSI/LiFePO4 battery also gradually decreased during the cycle with the lower capacity retention rate of 54.6%."

The sample naming defines 5LLZTO/PVDF as PVDF nanofiber membrane with 5 wt% LLZTO (“5LLZTO/PVDF means that 5% LLZTO was added”). This membrane is used as the scaffold in the composite solid polymer electrolyte for Li metal batteries (LLZTO/PVDF-PEO-LiTFSI). Specific electrochemical metrics reported in the text are given for the 10% sample (e.g., ionic conductivity 9.30×10^-4 S cm^-1 at 50 °C; stability window 4.9 V) and not numerically for the 5% sample. For the 5% case, only qualitative/cycling outcome is stated: the Li/Li symmetric cell with 5LLZTO/PVDF-PEO-LiTFSI short-circuited after 1125 h at 0.3 mA cm^-2 and 50 °C, but no schema-compatible quantitative performance (e.g., conductivity value) is provided for 5LLZTO/PVDF. Therefore, scenario and target species are given, while numeric fields remain null.

No direct application performance is reported for the 10LLZTO/PVDF nanofiber membrane alone. All quantified electrochemical/mechanical results are for the composite electrolyte (10LLZTO/PVDF-PEO-LiTFSI), not for 10LLZTO/PVDF by itself. Examples: “the ionic conductivity of 10LLZTO/PVDF-PEO-LiTFSI electrolyte can reach 9.30 × 10^−4 S cm^−1 at 50 °C”; “the tLi+ of the Li/10LLZTO/PVDF-PEO-LiTFSI/Li symmetric battery … is 0.30”; “10LLZTO/PVDF-PEO-LiTFSI electrolyte did not observe a significant change in current value between 2.5 V and 4.9 V”; “Li/Li symmetric battery assembled with 10LLZTO/PVDF-PEO-LiTFSI electrolyte shows relatively excellent cycle stability … during the 1200 h cycle”; “the tensile strength … 10LLZTO/PVDF-PEO-LiTFSI electrolyte … 9.2 MPa.” For 10LLZTO/PVDF itself, the paper provides morphology/structure only (e.g., SEM/EDS/TEM in Fig. 2) without standalone application metrics.

Li/Li symmetric battery assembled with 15LLZTO/PVDF-PEO-LiTFSI electrolyte shows short-circuit after 959 h at 0.3 mA cm^-2 and 50 °C: "Similarly, Li/Li symmetric battery assembled with 5LLZTO/PVDF-PEO-LiTFSI and 15LLZTO/PVDF-PEO-LiTFSI electrolytes also shows relatively poor cycle stability and short-circuited after 1125 h and 959 h, respectively (Figs. S1a and S1b)."

M0 (PVDF/PMMA=100/0) is a porous membrane used as the host for gel polymer electrolytes in Li-ion batteries. The paper provides quantitative values primarily for the corresponding gel electrolytes (E-series), e.g., electrolyte uptake explicitly stated for E0 (35%) and ionic conductivity for E90 (3.097e-3 S/cm), and Li+ transference numbers for E0–E100 (Table 2). No unambiguous numerical performance specific to M0 alone is reported; thus metrics are left null.
