M10 is the porous PVDF/PMMA (90/10 wt%) membrane (Table 1: "90/10 -> M10/E10"). The application throughout the paper is as a host/separator to form gel polymer electrolytes for Li-ion batteries using LiTFSI in EMITFSI and tested in Li/Electrolyte/LiFePO4 cells. However, quantitative performance values are not given for M10 specifically. Electrolyte uptake Figure 1 covers M0–M100 but only reports numeric examples for M0 (35%) and for M90 (mislabelled as E90) 122.2%, not for M10. Ionic conductivity and Li+ transference numbers are reported for E-series (gel electrolytes), with detailed values for E90 and a table listing TLi+ for E10 (0.5957), but these are for E10 (the gel derived from M10), not the M10 membrane itself. Therefore, only the scenario (electrolyte for Li-ion battery; target species Li+) is recorded, and no M10-specific numeric performance is extracted.

No direct application/performance metrics are reported for sample M30. M30 appears in morphology (SEM), ATR-FTIR, and XRD figures, but ionic conductivity, Li-ion transference number, and battery testing are only given for the gel electrolytes (E-samples), with explicit values for E90 (e.g., 3.097×10^-3 S/cm) and TLi+ in Table 2 (including E30, not M30). The battery cell characterization used E90, not M30. Hence no extractable application data specific to M30.

Table 1 defines M50 as the 50/50 PVDF/PMMA porous membrane. The figure caption states 'Liquid electrolyte uptake for sample M0–M100', indicating uptake was measured for membranes including M50. The text only gives uptake numbers for E0 (35%) and E90 (122.2%) and an ionic conductivity value for E90; no numeric uptake or conductivity is provided specifically for M50. Transference numbers in Table 2 are for gel electrolytes (E-samples), not the membrane M50, so no E50/M50 values are assigned.

Table 1 maps M70 (PVDF/PMMA 30/70) to the corresponding gel electrolyte E70. Table 2 reports for E70: Li-ion transference number TLi+ = 0.5504 (Ri0 = 3.670×10^3 Ω, Ri = 2.047×10^4 Ω). No specific ionic conductivity, electrolyte uptake, or electrochemical window values are given for M70/E70; full-cell tests and window data were only reported for E90.

In the Conclusions: "SEM that only sample of M90 gives clearer and better pores if compare with the other membranes and it also gives the highest ionic conductivity at room temperature at 3.097 × 10−3 S cm−1 and this sample has the lowest activation energy. But, at the same time it improved the lithium-ion transference number, 0.7922." The work positions the PVDF/PMMA blends as electrolytes for lithium rechargeable batteries.

M100 is the pure PMMA porous membrane; its corresponding gel polymer electrolyte is E100 (Table 1). Application context is lithium battery electrolyte. For E100, Table 2 reports a Li-ion transference number TLi+ = 0.2962 with resistances before/after polarization of 2.171×10^3 Ω and 2.563×10^3 Ω. No explicit ionic conductivity, electrochemical stability window, or battery cycling data were provided for E100 (those detailed results were given for E90 only).

E0 is the gel polymer electrolyte from PVDF/PMMA 100/0 (Table 1). Figure 1 states: “E0 reach the saturation with the electrolyte uptake at 35%.” Table 2 lists for E0: TLi+ = 0.5472 (Ri0 = 0.520×10^0 Ω; Ri = 8.797×10^0 Ω). Battery testing and stability window data were reported for E90, not E0.

Table 2 reports for sample E10: Lithium-ions transference number (TLi+) = 0.5957; Resistance before dc polarization Ri0 = 6.923×10^0 Ω; Resistance after dc polarization Ri = 2.018×10^1 Ω. The Li-ion transference number was measured in a Li/gel polymer electrolyte/Li cell, indicating use as a lithium battery electrolyte.

E30 is a gel polymer electrolyte (PVDF/PMMA 70/30) per Table 1. Table 2 reports for E30: Lithium-ions transference number TLi+ = 0.5513; resistance before dc polarization Ri0 = 1.552×10^2 Ω; resistance after dc polarization Ri = 3.551×10^2 Ω. These measurements are from Li/gel polymer electrolyte/Li cells using chronoamperometry and AC impedance.

E50 is the gel polymer electrolyte from the 50/50 PVDF/PMMA membrane (Table 1). Table 2 reports its lithium-ion transference number TLi+ = 0.5297 with resistances before/after dc polarization of 4.464×10^2 and 5.269×10^2 Ω, respectively. No explicit ionic conductivity value or battery cycling data are provided for E50 (battery tests used E90).

Table 2 lists lithium-ion transference number (TLi+) for each gel polymer electrolyte. For E70: Ri0 = 3.670×10^3 Ω, Ri = 2.047×10^4 Ω, and TLi+ = 0.5504. No explicit ionic conductivity value for E70 is given in text/tables (only plotted in Figure 5), so it is left null.

For E90: "The ionic conductivity of the gel polymer electrolyte is 3.097 × 10^{-3} S cm^{-1} for sample E90." and Table 2 lists "Lithium-ions transference number, TLi+ ... E90 ... 0.7922." E90 was also used in a Li/E90/LiFePO4 cell and showed an electrochemical stability window "stable up to 4.3 V," and had electrolyte uptake "E90 reaches saturation with a maximum liquid electrolyte uptake ratio of 122.2%."

Table 2 reports the lithium-ion transference number for sample E100 as 0.2962 (with Ri0 = 2.171×10^3 Ω and Ri = 2.563×10^3 Ω). Other numeric performance metrics (ionic conductivity, electrochemical stability window, battery cycling) are explicitly given for E90, not E100.

Sample PIL-GPE@IL is used as the electrolyte in Li/PIL-GPE@IL/LiFePO4 cells. Reported discharge specific capacities for Li/PIL-GPE@IL/LiFePO4 are 152 mAh g^-1 (0.1C), 148 mAh g^-1 (0.2C), 144 mAh g^-1 (0.5C), 129 mAh g^-1 (1C), 72 mAh g^-1 (2C) (Section 3.6). During cycling at 0.5C, the cell delivers an initial discharge capacity of 143 mAh g^-1 and retains ~97% after 120 cycles with coulombic efficiency close to 99% (Fig. 8f; text in 3.6 and Conclusions). Supporting electrolyte metrics specific to PIL-GPE@IL include ionic conductivity 1.76×10^-3 S cm^-1 at 30 °C and 5.23×10^-3 S cm^-1 at 80 °C (Table 1; 3.4), electrochemical stability window up to 4.26 V vs. Li+/Li (Fig. 5b; 3.4), and Li+ transference number tLi+ = 0.32 (Table 2; 3.5).

SPE is defined as a polymer electrolyte composed of PVDF and LiTFSI (weight ratio 1:0.5). Table 1 reports ionic conductivities for SPE; at 30 °C the conductivity is 2.78 × 10^-4 S cm^-1 (also 3.84, 5.36, 7.09, 9.31, 11.89 ×10^-4 S cm^-1 at 40–80 °C). LSV shows an oxidation potential of 4.67 V vs Li+/Li for SPE. No battery cell data were reported for Li/SPE/LiFePO4.

Material identification: “Another polymer electrolyte composed of PVDF, [BMIM]TFSI and LiTFSI with weight ratio of 1:2:0.5 was also prepared and marked as GPE@IL.” (Section 2.4)
Electrolyte properties (GPE@IL): Table 1 gives ionic conductivities (×10^-4 S cm^-1): 30 °C 15.6 (i.e., 1.56×10^-3 S/cm), 40 °C 18.7, 50 °C 27.9, 60 °C 32.8, 70 °C 41.3, 80 °C 52.7; Ea = 23.4723 kJ mol^-1. LSV oxidation potential: “The oxidation potential of … GPE@IL … 4.40 V vs. Li+/Li.” (Fig. 5b). Li+ transference number: Table 2, tLi+ = 0.26.
Battery performance using GPE@IL: “Li/GPE@IL/LiFePO4 … specific discharge capacity of 151 mAh g^-1 at 0.1C, 145 mAh g^-1 at 0.2C, 126 mAh g^-1 at 0.5C, 104 mAh g^-1 at 1C and 67 mAh g^-1 at 2C.” (Section 3.6, Fig. 8a,b). Cycling at 0.5C: “The Li/GPE@IL/LiFePO4 cell delivers a initial discharge capacity of 136 mAh g^-1, then … 127 mAh g^-1 after 120 charge-discharge cycles, which is 93% of the initial discharge capacity.” (Fig. 8e).

PE-PM-PVH is reported as a gel polymer electrolyte for Li metal batteries. At 25 °C, its ionic conductivity is 0.81 mS cm−1 (Table 1; Fig. 4a). The Li+ transport number is 0.72 (Fig. 5; Table 1). LSV shows an oxidation stability up to 5.0 V vs Li/Li+ (Fig. 6). In Li/GPE/Li symmetric tests, Li plating/stripping exhibits ~60 mV initial hysteresis, decreasing to ~50 mV and remaining stable over 500 h (Fig. 9b). In Li/GPE/LiCoO2 full cells, the initial discharge capacity is 152.7 mAh g−1 with 98% retention after 100 cycles at 0.1 mA cm−2 and >99% coulombic efficiency after the first cycle (Fig. 9c).

FPEC (specifically FPEC80/50) is used as a solid polymer electrolyte for Li batteries and shows ionic conductivity of 1.08×10^-4 S/cm at 30°C; electrochemical window ~4.5 V vs Li+/Li; Li/FPEC/LFP full cell delivers 156.2, 154.8, 130.4, 78.1 mAh/g at 0.2C, 0.5C, 1C, 2C (50°C) with 93% capacity retention after 50 cycles at 0.2C; Li/FPEC/Li symmetric cell shows ~150 mV overpotential stably for 200 h at 0.05 mA/cm^2 (50°C).

Nafion 211 (N211) is used as the commercial PFSA proton exchange membrane benchmark in a vanadium redox flow battery (VRFB), focusing on proton transport and suppression of vanadium (VO2+) crossover: (i) “two orders of magnitude lower areal resistance compared to 25 μm-thick PFSA membranes (Nafion 211).” (Abstract); (ii) VO2+ permeation test images include N211 after 72 h (Fig. 5b); (iii) “The red-dashed lines are efficiencies of the commercial PFSA membrane (N211) at each current density.” (Fig. 6 caption); (iv) “the single cell with PCEW10-100 performed better in EE at 200 mA cm−2, achieving approximately 76% EE compared to the commercial Nafion 211 (EE ~ 73%).” Numeric EE for N211 is given, but the schema lacks an efficiency field; no explicit numeric permeance/resistance/selectivity values for N211 are provided in text/tables.

Table 1 lists ionic conductivity of PEO20-LiDFOB-40% IL (EMImTFSI) as 1.85 × 10^-4 S/cm at 30 °C; Conductivity section confirms “σ = 1.85×10^-4 S/cm at 30 °C”. Additional application evidence: Li/PEO-LiDFOB-40 wt% IL/LiFePO4 cell delivered 155 mAh g^-1 at 0.1 C initially and 134.2 mAh g^-1 after 50 cycles at 25 °C.

PH (PVDF-HFP fibrous membrane) is used as the electrolyte/separator in Li metal batteries. Its ionic conductivity at room temperature is 3.83×10^-3 S cm^-1 (Figure 2h). Additional PH-specific performance: Li|PH|Li symmetric cell shows sudden polarization fluctuation at ~510 h at 0.1 mA cm^-2 and fails at 120 h at 0.5 mA cm^-2 (Figure 3a,b). In Li|PH|Cu cells, initial CE is 88.6% and average CE is 89.0% over 100 cycles at 0.5 mA cm^-2 (Figure 3d). In LFP|PH|Li cells at 0.2 C, capacity retention decreases to 80.4% after 220 cycles (Figure 4d). The Li+ transference number of PH is 0.46 (Figure S4).

NVPH is used as a gel polymer electrolyte in Li metal batteries. For LFP|NVPH|Li, the cell "delivers specific discharge capacities of 157.1, 155.8, 150.6, 143.2, and 130.1 mA h g−1 at 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1, and 2 C, respectively". High-temperature performance: "After 250 cycles at 80 °C, the discharge specific capacity of the LFP|NVPH|Li cell at 1 C is 149.7 mA h g−1 with a capacity retention of 91.8%". Symmetric Li|NVPH|Li cells show stable plating/stripping "beyond 1000 h" at 0.1 mA cm−2 and "over 600 h" at 0.5 mA cm−2. Electrochemical window: NVPH (1:1, 5 wt%) shows an upper limit of ~5.2 V.

For PETEA-HFA-TCGG-PAN GPE (3:2): "GPE 3:2 at 30 °C exhibits a satisfactory σ (4.06×10−3 S cm−1)" and shows a high lithium-ion transference number: "GPE 3:2 and GPE 1:1 both show excellent tLi+ of 0.87 and 0.73, respectively". Additional context: the electrochemical stability window is 5.35 V at 30 °C; NCM811|GPE 3:2|Li delivers 135.80 mAh g−1 at 10C and retains 84% (2C) and 70% (3C) after 300 cycles; symmetric Li|GPE 3:2|Li overpotential ~9 mV remains stable before 1100 h.

Specific to PETEA-HFA-TCGG-PAN GPE (1:1): (i) Ionic conductivity at 30 °C: “GPE 1:1 (3.59×10^{-3} S cm^{-1})” (Fig. 5a–c). (ii) Electrochemical stability window at 30 °C: “decomposition potentials … GPE 1:1 … 4.8 V” (Fig. 5f). (iii) Li+ transference number: “GPE 1:1 … 0.73” (Fig. 5d,e). (iv) NCM811 cell initial discharge: “NCM811|GPE 1:1| … 204.67 mAh g^{-1}; … coulombic efficiencies … 89.19%” (Fig. 6b).

CPE(PVAc) is used as the electrolyte in LiFePO4|CPE(PVAc)|Li cells. Reported metrics include: “The assembled LiFePO4|CPE(PVAc)|Li coin cells exhibit excellent initial discharge specific capacity (135.4 mAh g^-1 at 2C and 25°C, and 157.5 mAh g^-1 at 1C and 55°C) …” and electrolyte properties “This selectively infiltrated gel polymer electrolyte has a lithium-ion transference number of up to 0.75 and a satisfactory ionic conductivity of about 1.47 mS cm^-1 at 30°C.”

LiFePO4/SHSPE3/Li at 60°C: discharge capacity reached 144.8 mA h g^-1 at 0.2C with coulombic efficiency >97%, and after 100 cycles remained 119.3 mA h g^-1 (82% retention). Rate performance: 163.9, 137.2, 121.6, 104.7 mA h g^-1 at 0.1C, 0.2C, 0.5C, 1C, respectively. Li/SHSPE3/Li symmetric cell showed ~0.22 V overpotential. Electrochemical stability window for SHSPE3 >4.7 V vs Li/Li+; t_Li+ = 0.37.

PVFH-PMC-PEGC (10 wt% optimized) exhibited ionic conductivity of 2.70 mS cm^-1 at 25 °C: “At this optimized content, the ionic conductivity of PVFH-PMC-PEGC reaches 2.70 mScm^-1” (Fig. 3d). Lithium-ion transference number: “the t+ of … PVFH-PMC-PEGC are … 0.77” (Fig. 3b, S14). Additional relevant performance (context, not used as fields): electrolyte uptake 245% and retention 94% after 30 min (Fig. 2e); symmetric Li|PVFH-PMC-PEGC|Li stable 500 h at 0.5 mA cm^-2 with ~13 mV overpotential; critical current density up to 4 mA cm^-2 (Fig. S19); Li|PVFH-PMC-PEGC|LFP rate capacities e.g., 163.1 mAh g^-1 at 0.1C and 96 mAh g^-1 after 800 cycles at 2C with 0.032%/cycle decay.

All electrochemical/application results are reported for the composite PVFH-PMC-PEGC gel polymer electrolyte (e.g., ionic conductivity 2.70 mS cm^-1, t+ = 0.77, Li|PVFH-PMC-PEGC|Li symmetric cells, and Li|PVFH-PMC-PEGC|LFP/NCM622/S full cells), not for PMC-PEGC alone. For PMC-PEGC alone, only material properties such as contact angle (5.1°) are provided, which are not standalone application metrics.

PVFH (baseline GPE) data: ionic conductivity 0.19 mS cm^-1 at 25 °C (Fig. 3d: “PVFH has an ionic conductivity of 0.19 mScm^-1 at 25 °C”). Lithium-ion transference number t+ = 0.43 (“the t+ of PVFH … are calculated to be 0.43”). Additional PVFH application context: Li|PVFH|Li shows ~80 mV overpotential at 0.5 mA cm^-2 and short circuit at ~180 h (Fig. S17a), and Li|PVFH|LFP delivers 39.9 mAh g^-1 at 3C and 69.9 mAh g^-1 after 200 cycles at 0.5C with 53.2% retention (Fig. 5b,c).

The material is used as a gel polymer electrolyte (GPE) in lithium metal batteries. Evidence: “The CA/PVDF-based GPE delivers the ionic conductivity of 1.82 mS cm−1 and Li+ transfer number of 0.506…”. Electrochemical stability window: “the electrochemical stability window of CA/PVDF-GPE is about 4.8 V.” Symmetric Li/Li performance: “a stable voltage profile is observed for the GPE with no detectable failure in 350 h… at a current density of 1 mA cm−2 with an area capacity of 1.0 mA h cm−2.” Full-cell data (LiFePO4/GPE/Li): initial discharge capacity 144.7 mA h g−1 at 1C; after 200 cycles 150.8 mA h g−1; rate performance up to 5C; at 5C initial 101.2 mA h g−1 with capacity retention of 94.6% after 400 cycles and coulombic efficiency 96.81%.

Direct evidence for CA/PVDF-GPE as electrolyte: "The CA/PVDF-based GPE delivers the ionic conductivity of 1.82 mScm^-1 and Li+ transfer number of 0.506, which achieve uniform Li+ ion electrodeposition and inhibit the formation of lithium dendrites." It operates stably in Li symmetric cells: "a stable voltage profile is observed for the GPE with no detectable failure in 350 h" at 1 mA cm^-2. Electrochemical stability window: "the electrochemical stability window of CA/PVDF-GPE is about 4.8 V". Performance in full cells confirms applicability: "LiFePO4/GPE/Li cell shows an initial discharge capacity of 144.7 mAh g^-1... After 200 cycles, ... 150.8 mAh g^-1" and at high rate: "initial reversible capacity of 101.2 mAh g^-1 at 5C with the capacity retention of 94.6% after 400 cycles."

ME91 is one of the ionogel polymer electrolyte membranes prepared by varying the MPEGA/ETPTA ratio: "According to the quality ratios of MPEGA/ETPTA, they were numbered as ME91, ME82, and ME73, respectively." It is evaluated among electrolytes for Li+ conduction: "Comparing the ionic conductivity of all the electrolyte sample, it is found that σ(ME91) > σ(ME82) > σ(ME73)."

ME82 is an ionogel polymer electrolyte used in LiFePO4/ME82/Li cells. Its room-temperature ionic conductivity is reported as 1.19 mS cm^-1 (“The room temperature ionic conductivity of ME82 is calculated by the formula to be 1.19 mS cm^-1”). Additional battery results with ME82 include LiFePO4/ME82/Li showing 151.0 mAh g^-1 at 0.5C and 25 °C (148.5 mAh g^-1 after 200 cycles) and 160.9 mAh g^-1 at 55 °C with 94% capacity retention after 200 cycles; Li/ME82/Li symmetric cells exhibit stable stripping/plating for ~1000 h at 0.1 mA cm^-2. The ESW of ME82 is ~5 V.

ME73 is one of the ionogel polymer electrolyte samples prepared by varying the MPEGA/ETPTA ratio: "According to the quality ratios of MPEGA/ETPTA, they were numbered as ME91, ME82, and ME73, respectively." Performance data in the paper focus on ME82; only a relative statement involves ME73: "Comparing the ionic conductivity of all the electrolyte sample, it is found that σ(ME91) > σ(ME82) > σ(ME73)." All cell tests are LiFePO4/ME82/Li, so no numerical performance for ME73 is reported.

ME00 is the PVDF-HFP ionogel electrolyte without MPEGA/ETPTA. The paper reports application performance (ionic conductivity, ESW, t_Li+, Li/ME82/Li stability, LiFePO4/ME82/Li cycling) only for ME82. For ME00, only morphology, mechanical strength (0.42 MPa; 47% elongation), and relative thermal stability vs ME82 are discussed; no battery/electrochemical performance metrics are given in text or tables. Figure 3b includes ionic conductivity markers but no numeric values are stated, so no quantitative extraction for ME00.

Li|CPE-n|Li tests reported lithium-ion transference numbers: "As shown in Fig. 5 (a-d), the calculated t_{Li+} of CPE-0, CPE-1, CPE-3, and CPE-5 are 0.39, 0.43, 0.48, and 0.45." CPE-n are used as electrolytes in LiFePO4|CPE-n|Li batteries (Section 2.4). No specific ionic conductivity or battery performance values were reported for CPE-1 beyond t_{Li+}.

CPE-3 is an in-situ gel polymer electrolyte for Li metal batteries. Performance with LiFePO4|CPE-3|Li at 25°C: initial discharge specific capacity 150.4 mAh/g at 1C with 88.7% retention after 500 cycles and ~99.80% coulombic efficiency (Fig. 6g). High-temperature (55°C): initial 160.3 mAh/g at 1C, 96.63% retention after 500 cycles, 80.2% after 1000 cycles, CE ~99.82–99.87% (Fig. 7f). Supporting electrolyte metrics for CPE-3 include ionic conductivity 1.70 mS/cm at room temperature, 1.88×10^-3 S/cm at 30°C and 3.99×10^-3 S/cm at 80°C; electrochemical stability window ~5.0 V; t_Li+ = 0.48; Li|CPE-3|Li symmetric cell stable ~1000 h at 0.1 mA/cm^2 with ~70 mV polarization (Sections 3.1–3.4, Figs. 2, 5–7).

CPE-5 is one of the CPE-n electrolytes (n = mass% LLZTO). For Li|CPE-n|Li cells at room temperature, the calculated t_Li+ of CPE-0, CPE-1, CPE-3, and CPE-5 are 0.39, 0.43, 0.48, and 0.45 (Fig. 5a–d; chronoamperometry + EIS; eq. (3)). No specific ionic conductivity, electrochemical stability window, or full-cell cycling data are reported for CPE-5; detailed performance values are mainly provided for CPE-3.

EVOH-graft-LiCPSI was blended with PVDF-HFP to form a gel SIPE used as both electrolyte and separator in Li metal and LiFePO4/Li cells. Electrochemical performance of this SIPE includes: ionic conductivity 5.7×10^-5 S/cm at 25 °C (also reported as 5.7×10^-5 S cm^-1 in the Conclusion), electrochemical stability window up to 4.8 V vs Li/Li+, and Li+ transference number t+ = 0.88. In a Li|SIPE|Li symmetric cell, the polarization was ~19 mV and remained stable for 300 h at 0.5 mA/cm^2, with smooth lithium surface after cycling. In a LiFePO4/Li half-cell using this SIPE, rate capabilities were 123, 115, 110, 106, 101, 96, 92 mAh/g at 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8, 1, 1.5, 2C, respectively; upon returning to 1C, capacity recovered to 102 mAh/g. At higher rates: 84, 77, 72 mAh/g at 3, 4, 5C; coulombic efficiency ~100%. Long-term cycling at 1C delivered ~100 mAh/g initially and 95 mAh/g after 500 cycles (95% retention). These results directly evidence the use of EVOH-graft-LiCPSI-derived SIPE as a lithium battery electrolyte/separator.

The gel SIPE was used as electrolyte and separator in a LiFePO4/Li half-cell. Battery performances: “The discharge capacity at 1C stabilizes at 100 mA h g^-1 during the first 250 cycles. After 500 cycles, the discharge capacity still remains at 95 mA h g^-1, suggesting a 95% retention of the initial discharge capacity.” It also shows near 100% coulombic efficiency at various C-rates, and stable Li|SIPE|Li plating/stripping with polarization ~19 mV for 300 h at 0.5 mA cm^-2. Electrochemical window up to 4.8 V vs Li/Li+, and lithium-ion transference number 0.88. Ionic conductivity is reported for the gel SIPE (text states 5.7 × 10^-5 mS cm^-1 at 25 °C; conclusion states 5.7 × 10^-5 S cm^-1 at 25 °C).

PMS-PVDF-55 is used as a gel polymer electrolyte (GPE) in Li/PMS-PVDF-55 GPE/LFP cells. Rate performance reports: "The discharge specific capacities were: ... 106.5 mAh g−1 (10 C)." (Fig. 4a-b). Highlights also state: "LMBs deliver 106.5 mAh/g at 10 C." This confirms PMS-PVDF functions as a Li+ conducting battery electrolyte delivering 106.5 mAh/g at 10 C.

The TPP@PAN/PVDF-HFP fiber membrane is used as the precursor for a gel polymer electrolyte (GPE) by soaking in 1 M LiPF6 in EC/DEC and tested in Li||Li, Li||Cu, and Li||LiFePO4 cells. Reported metrics directly for this material/system include: (1) Li||LiFePO4 batteries: remaining capacity 158 mAh g^-1 with capacity retention of 91.2% after 200 cycles at 0.5 C (Figures 5d,e). (2) Li||Cu cells: plating/stripping Coulombic efficiency of 99.1% at 0.5 mA cm^-2 (Figure 4h). Additional corroborating performance for the same material includes ionic conductivity 0.85 mS cm^-1 at room temperature (Figure S9), Li||Li symmetric cycling stability up to 1000 h at 0.2 mA cm^-2 (Figure 4b), and flame resistance in pouch cells up to 370 °C (Figure 6a).

TPP@PAN/PVDF-HFP is used as a gel polymer electrolyte (GPE) for lithium metal batteries by immersing the electrospun membrane in 1 M LiPF6-EC/DEC. Key performance for this specific GPE includes: ionic conductivity 0.85 mS cm^-1 at room temperature (Figure S9); Li+ transference number 0.877 from CA/EIS (Figure 4a, Eq. S1); Li||Li symmetric cells stable for 1000 h at 0.2 mA cm^-2–0.2 mAh cm^-2 (Figure 4b) and for 800 h at 1 mA cm^-2 (Figure S11); charge-transfer resistance after 1 cycle 85 Ω (Figure 4c); interfacial activation energy 26.6 kJ mol^-1 (Figure 4d); lithium nucleation overpotential 66.0 mV on Cu (Figure 4e); exchange current density 0.650 mA cm^-2 (Figure 4f); Li||Cu plating/stripping Coulombic efficiency 99.1% at 0.5 mA cm^-2 (Figure 4h). In Li||LiFePO4 cells, capacity retention is 91.2% after 200 cycles at 0.5 C (remaining 158 mAh g^-1) and 82% after 200 cycles at 1 C (Figure 5d–g). Safety metrics: pouch cells with this GPE show excellent fire resistance up to ~370 °C and self-healing after puncture with voltage recovery within 100 s (Figure 6a–d).

The NCM811|GTSSE|Li battery still presents a reasonable cycle ability discharge specific capacity of 107.1 mAh g−1 after an ultralong cycle life of 1000 cycles at 0.5C (tested at 50 °C). The Li|GTSSE|Li symmetric cell can stably plated/stripped for more than 2500 h at 0.1 mA cm−2.

PVDF/PP SSPEs are used as solid-state polymer electrolytes in Li-ion batteries. Key performance directly reported for this material includes: (i) ionic conductivity at room temperature: “the conductivity of PVDF/PP SSPEs at room temperature still achieves 1.53×10^-4 Scm^-1” (Fig. 3a,b text); (ii) electrochemical stability window: “PVDF/PP SSPEs can exhibit an apparent expanded electrochemical stability window up to 4.8 V” (Fig. 3c); (iii) Li+ transference number: “The calculated value of tLi+ (0.24)” (Fig. 3d); (iv) symmetric Li cells: “long-term cycle stability up to 400 h nearly without voltage fluctuation at 0.15 mA cm^-2, 25 °C” (Fig. 4a); (v) full cells with LFP: initial discharge capacity 134 mAh g^-1 at 0.3C and 131 mAh g^-1 after >180 cycles; capacity retention 97.8% after 180 cycles; rate capacities: 141/136/127/112/86/41 mAh g^-1 at 0.1/0.2/0.5/1/2/5C; recovery 137 mAh g^-1 when returning to 0.1C (97.2%) (Fig. 5a–c); (vi) NCM523|PVDF/PP SSPEs|Li operated 2.8–4.3 V at 0.3C and 25 °C for 200 cycles steadily. These results are all explicitly reported for the sandwich-like PVDF/PP SSPEs membranes.

Blank PVDF SSPEs were used as solid-state polymer electrolytes in Li metal batteries. Full-cell data: “the discharge capacity of the LFP| PVDF SSPEs|Li cells decays rapidly to 85 mAh g−1 from the initial capacity of 125 mAh g−1 … after only 64 charge-discharge cycles” at 0.3C between 2.8–3.6 V and 25 °C (Fig. 5b–c). Symmetric Li|PVDF SSPEs|Li cells: “suddenly drops to zero leading to cell failure after only 43 h cycling” at 0.15 mA cm−2 and 25 °C (Fig. 4a). Electrochemical stability window: “the PVDF SSPEs start to decompose at the voltage of 4.7 V” (Fig. 3c).

HBA-PCEE is used as a polymer electrolyte in Li metal batteries. Reported full-cell performance with LFP cathode: "CNBA-PCEE and HBA-PCEE showed initial discharge capacities of 109.60 mAhg^-1 and 70.47 mAhg^-1 at 0.5C, respectively" (Fig. 5a). Rate performance at 2C was also given for HBA-PCEE: 13.66 mAhg^-1 (Fig. 5b and 5c), but the JSON holds a single capacity value. Additional property specific to HBA-PCEE: oxidation potential 4.6 V (Fig. 3b), though not mapped to a schema field.

CNBA-PCEE is a polymer electrolyte tested in LFP||CNBA-PCEE||Li full cells. Reported performance: "CNBA-PCEE ... showed initial discharge capacities of 109.60 mAh g^-1 at 0.5C" and at high rate: "significantly outperforming LFP||HBA-PCEE and LFP||CNBAPCEE||Li cells (13.66 mAh g^-1 and 51.73 mAh g^-1, respectively)" at 2C. Additional electrochemical property specific to CNBA-PCEE: oxidation potential 4.9 V. No explicit t_Li+, ionic conductivity value, breakthrough time, or regeneration data reported for CNBA-PCEE in the main text.

FBA-PCEE was used as the solid polymer electrolyte in full cells. LFP||FBA-PCEE||Li delivered an initial discharge specific capacity of 137.3 mAh/g at 0.5C, with 85.38% retention after 200 cycles and 77.38% after 500 cycles (Fig. 5a). Additional performance metrics for FBA-PCEE include t_Li+ = 0.75, oxidation potential = 5.1 V, and ionic conductivity = 2.02 mS/cm at 30 °C at the optimized 5:5 SN:monomer ratio (Figs. S3–S6; Table S1). Polarization in Li||FBA-PCEE||Li remained low (≈35 mV at 200–210 h; ≈45 mV at 1000–1010 h) (Fig. 3c). NCM-622||FBA-PCEE||Li also showed 151.61 mAh/g at 0.1C with 91.84% retention after 50 cycles and 78.95% after 100 cycles (Fig. 5f).

PFIGE is used as an ionogel electrolyte (PFIGE@Li) in Li||NCM523 cells. The paper reports: “PFIGE@Li's superior cyclability, achieving 92.1% capacity retention over 300 cycles at 0.5C (2.8–4.3 V)” (Fig. 7a,b). Additional performance relevant to this application includes tLi+ = 0.46 (Fig. 5a,b), electrochemical stability window up to 4.8 V (Fig. S17), Li||Li symmetric cells stable for 300 h (Fig. 6a), retention 78.9% at 4.6 V (Fig. 7c,d), 61.4% after 100 cycles at 80 °C (Fig. 7f), and pouch-cell retention 78.7% over 50 cycles with improved thermal safety (Fig. 8a–d).

SIC_GPE-40 is evaluated as a lithium-conducting gel polymer electrolyte for lithium metal batteries: “the ionic conductivity is modest at RT (3.7×10^{-7} S cm^{-1} for SIC_GPE-40 … at 25 °C) and increases … reaching … 3×10^{-6} S cm^{-1} at 60 °C” (Fig. 2a). The lithium transference number (LTN) is reported “obtaining a value in both cases of 0.85 ± 0.1,” i.e., for SIC_GPE-40 and SIC_GPE-60 (Fig. 2b). Critical current density testing notes for SIC_GPE-40 that it “shows some polarization … reaching the safety limit of 5 V at 3C (0.60 mA cm^{-2})” (Fig. 2c). Thermal stability for SIC_GPE-40 is “higher (Td5 = 150 °C)” than SIC_GPE-60 (Fig. 1c). No full-cell discharge capacity, selectivity-type metric, breakthrough-style lifetime metric, or regeneration protocol/efficacy are provided specifically for SIC_GPE-40; therefore capacity, selectivity, breakthrough, and regeneration are left null.

SIC_GPE-60 is used as a single-ion conducting gel polymer electrolyte and separator in lithium metal battery coin cells. Direct performance data for SIC_GPE-60 include:
- Critical current density (CCD): stable up to 2 mA cm^-2 at 40 °C in Li|SIC_GPE-60|Li symmetric cells (Fig. 2d; text: “stable plateau at high current densities up to 2 mA cm^-2”).
- Ionic conductivity: 1.1×10^-5 S cm^-1 at 25 °C; 4×10^-5 S cm^-1 at 60 °C (Fig. 2a).
- Lithium transference number (LTN): 0.85 ± 0.1 at 40 °C (Fig. 2b; equation-based extraction).
- Electrochemical stability (anodic): main oxidation >5.0 V vs Li/Li+; slight current near ~4.0 V with <1 µA cm^-2 up to 4.6 V (Fig. 3b–c).
- Cathodic behavior: initial PC reduction near ~1 V; stable plating/stripping thereafter (Fig. 3a).
- Li|Li plating/stripping overpotential at 0.1 mA cm^-2: ~160 mV at 50 °C; ~300 mV at 25 °C (Fig. 3e).
- Interfacial stabilization time with Li: ~144 h by EIS (Fig. 3d).
- Li deposition on Cu at 2 mAh cm^-2 (40 °C) showed dense, uniform deposits (text; Fig. S9); highlights dendrite suppression.
- Full-cell performance with SIC_GPE-60: LFP (2.7–3.8 V) ~140 mAh g^-1 at C/20; ~120 mAh g^-1 at C/5; 95% capacity retention after 50 cycles; CE ~99.7%. V2O5 (2.0–3.8 V) >200 mAh g^-1 at C/5; >96% retention after 50 cycles. NMC622 (3.0–4.25 V) 97% retention after 50 cycles; CE >99.0% (Fig. 4). These results explicitly pertain to SIC_GPE-60 as the electrolyte.

SIC_GPE-80 is listed (Table 1) but was not pursued for electrochemical testing: "while no plasticizer oozing is observed in the SIC_GPE-40 and SIC_GPE-60 samples, it is highly evident in the SIC_GPE-80 composition, as shown in Fig. S3. Therefore, this composition was excluded from further investigation." Additionally, "there is no loss of plasticizer during the membrane preparation process, unlike in the case of SIC_GPE-80 (Fig. S4)." No performance metrics (conductivity, LTN, CCD, cycling) were reported for SIC_GPE-80.

For 40 wt% [BMIM][SCN]/PVDF used as SPE in Li/SPE/C-LiFePO4 half-cells, Table 4 reports ionic conductivity 0.013 × 10^-3 S/cm (25 °C) = 0.013 mS.cm-1 for the PVDF composite, and Fig. 9a shows corresponding discharge behavior (148 mAh/g at C/8). Electrochemical stability window up to 5.0 V for the 40 wt% [BMIM][SCN]/PVDF composite is shown in Fig. 8d.

Li/SPE/C-LiFePO4 half-cells were produced with [BMIM][SCN]/PVDF-HFP with 40 wt% IL content. The fifth charge–discharge at room temperature and C/8 showed: 'the one for the PVDF-HFP composites 124 mAh.g−1'. Supporting applicability to batteries: 'The highest room temperature ionic conductivity value is 0.15 mS.cm−1 for 40 wt% [BMIM][SCN]/PVDF-HFP' and 'there are no oxidation/reduction peaks between 0.0 and 5.0 V ... being very stable for their use in lithium-ion battery applications.'

PE 4 is used as the electrolyte in LFP|PE 4|Li cells. Table 3 reports for LFP|PE 4 (24.7 wt% PVdF, 2.3 wt% LiODFB, 51.1 wt% EtMeImNTf2, 21.9 wt% TMS): at C/10, discharge specific capacity after 50 cycles = 168 mAh g−1; at 1C, cycle 1 = 120 mAh g−1 and cycle 50 = 115 mAh g−1. The text also summarizes: “The LiFePO4 cathode with the membrane … exhibited a good reversible capacity of 138 mAh g−1 and 120 mAh g−1 at high current densities (the C/2 and 1C rates).” Additionally, Table 2 shows PE 4 ionic conductivity of 3.21 mS cm−1 at 25 °C and activation energy 19.58 kJ mol−1, and the Li+ transference number for PE 4 is ~0.033 (after 3 days).

Parent electrolyte performance: “the t_Li+ of the PDEGMA gel electrolyte (n = 1) (0.45) was lower than that of the parent electrolyte (0.56).” Measurements in Fig. 3 were conducted at 30 °C (“ionic conductivity and Li-ion transference numbers (tLi+) of the [LiTFA]/[SL] = 1/3 solution and the gel electrolytes … at 30 °C”). No numerical ionic conductivity for the parent solution is given in text.

Only spectroscopic/structural observations are reported for the PPMA solution (polymerized PMA dissolved in [LiTFSA]/[SL] without cross-linker): “In the case of a solution of polymerized PMA (PPMA), the TFSA peak appeared at a wavenumber comparable to that of the [LiTFSA]/[SL] = 1/3 solution; however, the peak shifted slightly toward a lower wavenumber when the PPMA content was further increased (n ≥ 1.5)… This indicates that the carbonyl oxygen of PPMA was weakly coordinated to Li+, and the CIPs and AGGs were slightly dissociated into ions.” Performance metrics (ionic conductivity, t_Li+, battery discharge) were measured only for gel electrolytes with crosslinked networks, not for the PPMA solution: “The ionic conductivity… of the gel electrolytes…,” “The PPMA gel electrolytes exhibited tLi+ values…,” and “Discharge curves of Li/LiCoO2 cells with gel electrolytes…”. Therefore, no direct application performance for the PPMA solution is available in the text.

In this article, 'PTFPMA solution' denotes the polymerized TFPMA dissolved in LiTFSA/SL without crosslinker, used for solvation-structure analysis (e.g., '...polymerized without a crosslinker to obtain a polymer solution.' and 'For the solution of polymerized TFPMA (PTFPMA), the TFSA peak position was independent... suggesting that the polymer side chains did not coordinate or affect the solvation structure of Li+'). All performance results (ionic conductivity, t_Li+, electrochemical window, Li/LiCoO2 discharge) were measured on crosslinked PTFPMA gel electrolytes, not on the PTFPMA solution. Hence, no application performance specific to 'PTFPMA solution' can be extracted.

The paper prepared polymer solutions by polymerizing methacrylate monomers without a crosslinker in [LiTFSA]/[SL] = 1/3 ("...polymerized without a crosslinker to obtain a polymer solution."). For the solution of polymerized DEGMA (PDEGMA), Raman spectra showed the TFSA peak shifted to lower wavenumber and increased free SL due to Li+ complexation by ether side chains ("for the solution of polymerized DEGMA (PDEGMA), the TFSA peak clearly shifted toward a lower wavenumber... indicating... enhanced dissociation of Li+-TFSA−... the fraction of free SL... was higher in the PDEGMA solution due to the complex formation of PDEGMA with Li+."). No quantitative Li+ transference number or battery performance was reported for the PDEGMA solution (values were given only for gel electrolytes), so numeric fields are left null.

For the PDEGMA gel electrolyte with composition [LiTFSA]/[SL]/[monomer unit] = 1/3/1, the paper states: "the t_Li+ of the PDEGMA gel electrolyte (n = 1) (0.45) was lower than that of the parent electrolyte (0.56)." (Fig. 3b, measured at 30 °C). No numerical ionic conductivity or regeneration data for PDEGMA (n=1) were provided in the text.
The PTFPMA gel electrolytes exhibited larger t_Li+ values and reached 0.70 for [LiTFSA]/[SL]/[TFPMA] = 1/3/2; the transference numbers in Fig. 3b were measured at 30 °C.

Al-MCM41 is used to form a composite quasi-solid-state electrolyte (Al-MCM41@HD) for solid-state Li metal batteries. Reported metrics: ionic conductivity 1.09×10^{-3} S cm^{-1} at 30 °C for Al-MCM41@HD (Figure 3c; text: “ionic conductivity reaches 1.09×10^{-3 Scm^{-1}} at 30 °C”), and Li+ transference number t_Li+ = 0.81 (Figure 3d; Table S2). Additional application-relevant evidence: wider electrochemical stability window ≈ 4.83 V (Figure 2i); Li|Al-MCM41@HD|Li shows stable plating/stripping for 4000 h at 0.2 mA cm^{-2}/0.2 mAh cm^{-2} with ~153 mV polarization (Figure 4c); flame-retardant behavior of Al-MCM41@HD (Figure 4e).

PI/PV hybrid separator (electrospun, thermally imidized) used as a lithium battery separator shows: Li+ ionic conductivity 3.3 mS cm−1 (EIS, Fig. 4d: “the PI/PV hybrid separator has high Li+ conductivity (3.3 mScm−1)…”); Li+ transference number t_Li+ = 0.70 (Fig. 4e,f: “The Li+ transference number of the PI/PV hybrid separator is 0.70…”). Additional relevant performance: electrochemical potential window 5.1 V (Fig. 5a); porosity 75% and electrolyte uptake 775% (Table 1); 0° contact angle (Fig. 4b); Li/Cu half-cells at 0.5 mA cm−2 and 2 mAh cm−2 show CE 96.9% (1st) and ~97% after 65 cycles (Fig. 5d); Cu/NMC after 140 °C storage for 1 h retains 1.4 mAh cm−2 discharge capacity at 10th cycle with 94.5% CE (Fig. 6a); MCMB/NMC delivers 2.3 mAh cm−2 first discharge and 86.1% retention after 200 cycles; rate to 1.4 mAh cm−2 at 2 mA cm−2 (Fig. 6c–f).

LiBFSIE is a single-ion-conducting polymer gel electrolyte used in Li metal/LFP batteries. Reported electrolyte performance includes ionic conductivity 2.0×10^-4 S/cm at 35 °C (Figure 3a; Conclusions), Li+ transference number t_Li+ = 0.93 (Figure 3c,d), and electrochemical stability with no obvious oxidation up to 4.8–5 V (Figure 3b; Conclusions). Cell-level results with LiBFSIE include symmetric Li plating/stripping stable for 300 cycles with rectangle-like profiles (Figure 5), Li/LFP reversible capacities: 137.9 mAh/g (0.1 C), 135.0 (0.2 C), 131.1 (0.4 C), 124.7 (0.7 C), 106.4 (1.5 C), recovery to 131.6 mAh/g when returned to 0.4 C (Figure 6a), up to 96.5 mAh/g at 2.5 C for thinner (~100 μm) electrolyte (Figure 6b), 125.4 mAh/g after 200 cycles at 0.5 C with average Coulombic efficiency 99.95% (Figure 6c,d).

P(OEM-DMS-FPM) was used as a solid polymer electrolyte (and binder) in Li metal polymer batteries. For P(OEM7-DMS2-FPM1), the ionic conductivity reached 1.1×10^-4 S cm^-1 at 60 °C (and 1.1×10^-6 S cm^-1 at 20 °C). The Li-ion transference number of the cell featuring P(OEM-DMS-FPM) as the electrolyte was 0.38. The oxidative decomposition threshold was ~4.5 V. In Li/PE/NCM cells, initial discharge capacities were 126 mAh g^-1 at 4.0 V and 120 mAh g^-1 at 4.1 V; capacity retention was 76% after 200 cycles at 4.1 V. In Li/PE/Li symmetric cells, stable cycling persisted for 580 h at 0.6 mA cm^-2 with <0.2 V polarization.

PEO-based solid polymer electrolyte (PEO/LiTFSI) shows ionic conductivity 2.06 × 10^-4 S cm^-1 at 60 °C (Fig. 2e). LSV indicates decomposition begins at 3.8 V vs Li/Li+ for PEO/LiTFSI (Fig. 2f). In Li|PEO/LiTFSI|Li symmetric cells, lifespan ≈130 h with average overpotential 128 mV before short-circuit (Fig. 3a). In LFP|PEO/LiTFSI|Li cells, discharge capacities are 148.9, 143.7, 136.8, 121.4 mAh g^-1 at 0.2, 0.3, 0.5, 1.0 C; at 0.5 C initial capacity 118.9 mAh g^-1 decays to near 0 mAh g^-1 by ~350 cycles (Figs. 6a,c). Li+ transference number t_Li+ = 0.14 at 60 °C (Fig. S12). Additionally, PEO-based electrolytes have low RT conductivity (<1×10^-5 S cm^-1) and narrow stability window (~3.8 V) as background (intro).

F-PEO is used as a solid polymer electrolyte (with LiTFSI) for all-solid-state Li metal batteries. Key performance directly reported in Results & Discussion: ionic conductivity of F-PEO/LiTFSI reaches 3.32 × 10^-4 S cm^-1 at 60 °C (Fig. 2e). Electrochemical stability window expanded to 4.3 V vs Li/Li+ (Fig. 2f: no decomposition below 4.3 V). Li+ transference number t_Li+ = 0.19 at 60 °C (Fig. S12). Li|F-PEO/LiTFSI|Li symmetric cell shows stable cycling up to 2000 h with average polarization ~56 mV at 0.1 mA cm^-2 (Fig. 3a). Critical current density (CCD) = 0.35 mA cm^-2 (Fig. S13). LFP|F-PEO/LiTFSI|Li full cells: at 0.5 C, initial 151.0 mAh g^-1, 74.3% capacity retention after 500 cycles (capacity 112.2 mAh g^-1, avg CE 99.7%) (Figs. 6b,c); at 1.0 C, initial 141.5 mAh g^-1 and 99.8 mAh g^-1 after 900 cycles (70.5% retention), CE > 99.5% (Fig. 7a). Pouch cell (single-layer) at 0.1 C: initial 159.8 mAh g^-1, retains 99.2% after 25 cycles (Fig. 7c).

PEO/LiTFSI is used as a solid polymer electrolyte. Results & Discussion report: ionic conductivity 2.06 × 10^-4 S cm^-1 at 60 °C for PEO/LiTFSI (Fig. 2e). Electrochemical stability: current begins to increase at 3.8 V vs Li/Li+ (Fig. 2f). Li|PEO/LiTFSI|Li symmetric cell: ~130 h lifespan at 0.1 mA cm^-2 with average overpotential 128 mV before short-circuit (Fig. 3a). Critical current density 0.25 mA cm^-2 (Fig. S13). Li+ transference number t_Li+ = 0.14 at 60 °C (Fig. S12). In LFP|PEO/LiTFSI|Li cells, discharge capacities: 148.9, 143.7, 136.8, 121.4 mAh/g at 0.2, 0.3, 0.5, 1.0 C, respectively; initial 118.9 mAh/g at 0.5 C, decaying to near 0 mAh/g by ~350 cycles (Figs. 6a,c).

F-PEO/LiTFSI is used as a solid polymer electrolyte in all-solid-state lithium metal batteries. Reported performance includes: ionic conductivity 3.32×10^-4 S cm^-1 at 60 °C (Fig. 2e); Li+ transference number t_Li+ = 0.19 at 60 °C (Fig. S12); electrochemical stability window up to 4.3 V vs Li/Li+ (Fig. 2f). Li|F-PEO/LiTFSI|Li symmetric cell at 0.1 mA cm^-2 and 60 °C cycled stably for 2000 h with average polarization 56 mV (initial 87 mV, stable 49 mV) (Fig. 3a). LFP|F-PEO/LiTFSI|Li coin cells: rate capacities 158.5 mAh g^-1 at 0.1 C; 157.1, 156.0, 153.0, 146.3 mAh g^-1 at 0.2, 0.3, 0.5, 1.0 C (Fig. 6a). Cycling at 0.5 C: initial 151.0 mAh g^-1, 112.2 mAh g^-1 after 500 cycles (74.3% retention), average CE 99.7% (Figs. 6b,c). At 1.0 C: initial 141.5 mAh g^-1, 99.8 mAh g^-1 after 900 cycles, CE > 99.5% (Fig. 7a). Single-layer pouch cell (4.3 cm × 5.6 cm electrode): initial 159.8 mAh g^-1, 99.2% capacity retention after 25 cycles, average CE 99.2% (Fig. 7c). Critical current density 0.35 mA cm^-2 in Li|Li cells (Fig. S13).

LS is a fluorinated polymer lithiophilic separator loaded with MOF801 for dendrite-free lithium metal batteries. Key application data with LS: Li||Li symmetric cells show stable operation for >1000 h with a small overpotential of 34 mV at 1 mA cm^-2 and 1 mAh cm^-2; even at 5 mA cm^-2 it maintains a moderate overpotential (180 mV). LS exhibits high Li+ transference number t_Li+ = 0.80, higher ionic conductivity (0.85 mS cm^-1), and larger exchange current density j0 = 1.963 mA cm^-2 compared to Celgard. Electrochemical stability: LSV shows LS current does not fluctuate significantly within 4.5 V. Full-cell performance: NCM811||Li with LS delivers 202, 195, 184, 174, 160 mAh/g at 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1, 2 C, respectively; at 0.5 C it starts at 179 mAh/g and retains 113 mAh/g after 400 cycles (62% retention, higher than Celgard’s 51%). LFP||Li with LS retains 92.9% after 500 cycles at 1 C. A NCM811||thin-Li pouch cell (≈24 mg cm^-2 loading) with LS shows superior discharge capacity and powers a light bulb even after bending/folding.

PZI-F is used as a polymer solid-state electrolyte for Li metal batteries. It shows ionic conductivity of 1.39×10^-4 S/cm at -30 °C and 9.05×10^-4 S/cm at room temperature (Fig. 3a; text: “it still has an ionic conductivity of 1.39 × 10^-4 S cm^-1 even at -30 °C”). The Li+ transference number t_Li+ = 0.83 (Fig. 3b). Electrochemical stability window reaches 5.3 V vs Li/Li+ (Fig. 3c). Symmetric Li||Li cells with PZI-F exhibit ~0.05 V polarization for >1500 h at 0.2 mA/cm^2 and 0.2 mAh/cm^2 (Fig. 4a), and at -10 °C cycle stably up to 4000 h with ~78.4 mV overpotential (Fig. 6a); stable operation also at -30 °C (Fig. 6d). LFP||Li delivers 154.53 mAh/g initially and 144.16 mAh/g after 150 cycles (93.2% retention) at 1C, RT (Fig. 4b). At -10 °C and 0.2C, it cycles 800 times with 105.25 mAh/g retained (Fig. 4d). NCM811||Li shows 161.3 mAh/g initially and 70% retention after 400 cycles at 0.3C, RT (Fig. 4c). A Li|PZI-F|LFP pouch cell powers LEDs from -23 to 99 °C and withstands mechanical abuse (Fig. 7b).

PZI is the comparison polymer electrolyte (without HFMA). Its room‑temperature ionic conductivity is 7.11×10^-4 S cm^-1 (Fig. 3a). The Li+ transference number t_Li+ for PZI is 0.73 (Fig. 3b). Linear sweep voltammetry shows an electrochemical window of 4.7 V for PZI (Fig. 3c). In Li||Li symmetric cells at 0.2 mA cm^-2 and 0.2 mAh cm^-2, PZI shows a stable polarization voltage of ~0.045 V but short‑circuits after 547 h (Fig. 4a). TEM after cycling with PZI shows a thick, inhomogeneous CEI (~12.3 nm) on LFP, indicating unstable interface (Fig. 5a).

Directly reported for the single-salt sample: “At room temperature, the ionic conductivities of ... PZI-LiTFSI are calculated to be 5.59×10^-4 S cm^-1” (EIS on SS||SS cells). The Li+ transference number is given as “PZI-LiTFSI (0.62)” (Fig. S9).

PZI-LiPF6 is a single-salt (LiPF6) version of the polymer electrolyte prepared as a comparison sample. Reported electrochemical metrics (not mapped to schema fields) include: room-temperature ionic conductivity of 5.24×10^-4 S cm^-1 and Li+ transference number t_Li+ = 0.63. Evidence: "At room temperature, the ionic conductivities of PZI, PZI-F, PZI-LiPF6 and PZI-LiTFSI are calculated to be 7.11×10^-4 S cm^-1 9.05×10^-4 S cm^-1 5.24×10^-4 S cm^-1 and 5.59×10^-4 S cm^-1, respectively." and "than those of PZI-LiPF6 (0.63), PZI-LiTFSI (0.62) (Fig. S9)."

For LFPP 1%-Li anode: Table 1 reports capacity retention 77.7% and CE 100.0% at 0.5C; at 1C after rate performance, CE 98.8% and CR 57.9%. Symmetric cell with LFPP 1%-Li shows 0.038 V overpotential for >1000 h at 0.5 mA cm^-2, 1 mAh cm^-2. Quotes: “the batteries using an LFPP 2% - Li anode and an LFPP 1% -Li anode exhibit … capacity retentions of 77.5% and 77.7% respectively” (Fig. 6e, Table 1). “The symmetric cell with LFPP 1% -Li exhibits the lowest and most stable over-potential at 0.038 V for over 1000 h.”

Table 1: "The cell using an LFPP 2% -Li anode exhibits the highest capacity retention of 75.8% and a high coulombic efficiency of 99.0% at 1C after 390 cycles." Also Table 1 (0.5C): LFPP 2%-Li shows CE 100.0% and CR 77.5%.

LFPP-4/1.3/0.3@PVDF is discussed as a single-ion polymer electrolyte for Li+ conduction: "Their ionic conductivities were measured by EIS with stainless steel as the blocking electrode at room temperature." Specifically for this sample: "the ionic conductivity of LFPP-4/1.3/0.3@PVDF with 5.4 mol% of PET4A decreases obviously... poor mechanical strength... resulting in low ionic conductivity." No quantitative performance values for this formulation are provided in the text.

Direct evidence for LFPP-4/1.6/0.6@PVDF: (1) Highest ionic conductivity among all samples: “the LFPP-4/1.6/0.6@PVDF SIPE with 9.7 mol% of PET4A shows the highest ionic conductivity up to 5.81 mS cm^-1 among all samples …” (Results 2.1; Fig. 1a; Table 1). (2) Table 1 explicitly lists for LFPP-4/1.6/0.6@PVDF: ESW = 5.2 V and σ at room temperature = 5.81 mS cm^-1. (3) Temperature dependence: “the LFPP-4/1.6/0.6@PVDF SIPE showed the highest ionic conductivities of up to 5.81 mS cm^-1 at 30 °C and 1.28×10^-2 S cm^-1 at 60 °C.” (Section 2.4; Fig. 5). Note: Battery cycle data reported in this paper were measured on LFPP-4/2/1@PVDF, not on LFPP-4/1.6/0.6@PVDF; hence no battery capacity/retention is assigned to this material.

For LFPP-4/2/1@PVDF: "the LFPP-4/2/1@PVDF SIPE is the best balanced choice ... showing an ionic conductivity of up to 2.32 mScm−1 at room temperature" (Section 2.4). Additional performance details for this sample: t_Li+ = 0.91 (Fig. 6a), ESW up to 5.2 V for SS|LFPP-4/2/1@PVDF|Li (Fig. 6b), Li|LFPP-4/2/1@PVDF SIPE|Li symmetric cell stable for ~1200 h with ~60 mV polarization (Fig. 6c), and Li|LFPP-4/2/1@PVDF SIPE|LFP at 0.2C: initial 138 mA h g−1 and ~140 mA h g−1 after 230 cycles (Fig. 1b / Section 2.7).

LFPP-4/3/2@PVDF is one of the LFPP@PVDF SIPEs prepared (Table 2: PET4A 22.2 mol%). The Results state: “As depicted in Fig. 1a, lithium-ion conductivities of all the LFPP@PVDF SIPEs with various contents of PET4A can reach as high as up to 10^-3 S cm^-1 at room temperature.” Specific numeric conductivity, tLi+, ESW, or cycling data are only reported for other ratios (e.g., LFPP-4/1.6/0.6 or LFPP-4/2/1), not for LFPP-4/3/2@PVDF.

The study explicitly used Celgard 2400 as the commercial PP separator: "a Celgard 2400 membrane (Celgard, China) with a thickness of 25 μm, was used as a LIB separator for comparison." Table 1 reports the ionic conductivity (σ) for PP as 0.66 mS cm-1 (Table 1: PP σ (mS cm-1) = 0.66). This value pertains to the Celgard 2400 comparator in the LIB separator application.

FPEEK-0.25 is used as an electrospun separator for lithium-ion batteries (LiFePO4||Li coin cells). Table 1 reports its ionic conductivity σ = 3.12 mS cm−1 (higher than PP’s 0.66 mS cm−1). Additional FPEEK-0.25-specific performance noted: EU = 559%, porosity = 88% (Table 1); contact angle = 5° (Fig. 5B); bulk resistance 1.71 Ω (EIS, Fig. 7a); no measurable decomposition below 5 V vs Li/Li+ (LSV, Fig. 7b). Cell metrics with FPEEK-0.25: initial discharge capacity 150.5 mAh g−1 at 0.5 C, 25 °C (Fig. 10); ≈93% capacity retention after 300 cycles at 25 °C and 0.5 C applicable to all electrospun separators including FPEEK-0.25 (Fig. 9); 85.3% retention after 300 cycles at 60 °C and 0.5 C (Fig. 11).

FPEEK-0.5 was used as an electrospun porous separator for lithium-ion batteries. Performance specific to FPEEK-0.5 includes: (i) Initial discharge capacity: "The initial discharge capacities were 143.9, 150.5, 146.9 and 135.0 mAh g^-1 for the electrospun porous PEEK, FPEEK-0.25, and FPEEK-0.5 separators and the commercial PP separator, respectively." (0.5 C, room temperature). (ii) Room-temperature cycling: "The LIBs with electrospun separators showed more stable discharge capacities during the 300 cycles and possessed a capacity retention ratio of approximately 93% after 300 cycles" (applies to all electrospun separators, thus to FPEEK-0.5). (iii) High-temperature cycling: "After 300 repeated charge-discharge cycles at high temperature (60 °C), the discharge capacity retention ratio of the cells assembled with the electrospun fibrous PEEK, FPEEK-0.25 and FPEEK-0.5 separators was 81.6%, 85.3% and 83.0%, respectively." Additional properties supporting application: Table 1 lists FPEEK-0.5 porosity 85%, electrolyte uptake 534%, and ionic conductivity 2.86 mS cm^-1; contact angle 8° and electrolyte retention after 5 days 69% were reported for FPEEK-0.5.

Electrospun PEEK was used as a separator in Li-ion coin cells (LiFePO4||Li). Table 1 and Fig. 7(a) report the ionic conductivity of the electrolyte-soaked PEEK separator as 2.71 mS cm-1 (bulk resistance 2.3 Ω). Additional PEEK-specific performance noted for context: porosity 92%, electrolyte uptake 520%, contact angle 9°, negligible thermal shrinkage at 150 °C for 1 h, thermal degradation not observed until ~450 °C; initial cell discharge capacity 143.9 mAh g-1 at 0.5 C with ~93% capacity retention after 300 cycles at 25 °C, and 81.6% retention after 300 cycles at 60 °C; electrochemical stability window with no measurable decomposition below 5 V vs Li/Li+.

F-cHBC (T-F-cHBC, P21/c phase) was tested as a Li-ion storage anode: “provided a reversible specific capacity of ≈160 mAh g−1 at a current density of 100 mA g−1” and maintained 100 mAh g−1 at 7000 mA g−1; at 1000 mA g−1 it delivered 200 mAh g−1 at the 400th cycle with 99.4% coulombic efficiency (Figure 3A–D). It also functioned as a Na-ion anode (≈125 mAh g−1 at 100 mA g−1; 65 mAh g−1 at 4000 mA g−1; Figure 5A–F), but the primary Li+ metrics above support the selected capacity.

For the Li-ion storage cell, the T-F-cHBC anode "provided a reversible specific capacity of ≈160 mA h g−1 at a current density of 100 mA g−1" (Figure 3A). It also "maintained a capacity of 100 mA h g−1 at a high current density of 7000 mA g−1 and delivered a reasonable recovery capacity of 160 mA h g−1 at the 42nd cycle (400 mA g−1)" (Figure 3C). At a fixed current density of 1000 mA g−1, the T-F-cHBC anode showed "a stable coulombic efficiency of 99.4% and specific capacity of 200 mA h g−1" at the 400th cycle (Figure 3D). Additionally, T-F-cHBC was functional in Na-ion storage cells (e.g., ≈125 mA h g−1 at 100 mA g−1; 65 mA h g−1 at 4000 mA g−1; Figure 5), but the primary Li-ion metrics above are directly tied to T-F-cHBC.

The paper prepares and tests PEO-based solid polymer electrolytes (SPEs) at EO/Li = 30:1 using several lithium (alkoxyfluorovinylether) sulfonate salts, including the monolithium salt 1. Ionic conductivities were measured by EIS as a function of temperature and presented as Arrhenius plots for the monolithium salts (Fig. 7), and VTF fit parameters for each salt (including salt 1) are listed in Table 2: for 1, T0 = -55 °C, A = 0.1 (K1/2 S/cm), B = 563 K, Δμ = 10.0 ± 2.5 kJ/mol. However, no explicit numeric conductivity values are tabulated for salt 1 (only curves are shown), so conductivity is not recorded here as a numeric field. Relevant excerpts: “SPE membranes were fabricated … EO/Li ratios of 30:1 … Ionic conductivities were measured … Arrhenius plots for SPEs made of … a monolithium (alkoxyfluorovinylether) sulfonate salt (1–3) …” and “Table 2 … for … salt (1–6) … 1: T0 -55, A 0.1, B 563, Δμ 10.0 ± 2.5 kJ/mol.”

The work prepares solid polymer electrolytes (SPEs) using PEO (EO/Li = 30:1) and evaluates ionic conductivity by EIS for all new salts, including the dilithium salt 4 (Arrhenius plots for dilithium salts 4–6 in Fig. 8; VTF parameters listed for salt 4 in Table 2). The study context is lithium battery electrolytes. Conductivities for the new salts are generally lower than LiTFSI/LiTf, with an exception noted for salt 4. No explicit conductivity value for salt 4 is tabulated, only curves and VTF fit parameters; hence a numeric conductivity is not extracted.

DF_{0.24}-20 is used as a solid polymer electrolyte in Li metal batteries. Symmetric Li||Li cells at 60°C (J = 0.1 mA/cm^2, Q = 0.1 mAh/cm^2) show nearly 6000 h ultra-stable cycling with long-period stable overpotential 90–180 mV for 300–5000 h, and elsewhere reported >5000 h with overpotential <100 mV. Oxidation stability for DF_{0.24}-20 is given as ~5.0 V (at 5 μA/cm^2 benchmark vs C/Al). Ionic conductivity: 0.11 mS/cm at 60°C; 1.9×10^-5 S/cm at 28°C. Full-cell results: Li|DF_{0.24}-20|LCO (charged to 4.45 V) retains 80% after 300 cycles at 0.5C. LFP high-loading (14 mg/cm^2) retains 75.3% after 100 cycles; low-loading LFP shows 90.8% retention (138.8 mAh/g) at 2C; LFP at 2.7 mg/cm^2 and 4.2 V retains 96% after 300 cycles. NCM 811 (to 4.5 V) shows initial CE 88.1%, average CE 99.05% over subsequent 99 cycles, and 67.8% capacity retention after 50 cycles. NCM 523 (to 4.2 V) retains 71.2% after 100 cycles.

Table 2 reports Aquivion-87 ionic conductivity at 25 °C: EC-DMA σ(25 °C) ×10^4 = 10.8 → 1.08×10^-3 S/cm; EC-PC σ(25 °C) ×10^4 = 0.9 → 9×10^-5 S/cm. Table 4 gives Li+ transference numbers: Aquivion-87-EC-DMA T_Li+ = 0.69; Aquivion-87-EC-PC T_Li+ = 0.88. Table 5 shows Li|Li overvoltage and interface resistance for Aquivion-87: EC-DMA ±262 mV, Rs = 400 Ω/cm2; EC-PC ±300 mV, Rs = 222 Ω/cm2. Results section states symmetric Li|Li with polymer electrolyte showed stable Li plating/stripping for 300 h at ±0.1 mA/cm2 with ±0.05 mAh/cm2 cutoff. LFP|Li cells with Aquivion-87 electrolytes: initial discharge capacity 142 mAh/g at 0.1C for EC-DMA and 141 mAh/g for EC-PC; Aquivion-87-EC-DMA delivers 136 mAh/g at 1C and ~99% capacity retention after 70 cycles.

Aquivion-98 used as Li+ form gel-polymer electrolyte in Li metal batteries. For Aquivion-98 solvated by EC-DMA, Table 2 reports σ(25 °C) ×10^4 = 9.8, i.e., 9.8×10^-4 S/cm. Table 4 gives T_Li+ = 0.69 for Aquivion-98-EC-DMA. Additional related performance for this material: in Li|Li cells, overvoltage ±119 mV and interface resistivity 206 Ω/cm2 (Table 5); in LFP|Li cells, initial discharge capacity 136 mAh/g at 0.1C and 99.9% capacity retention after 70 cycles (Results). For comparison, Aquivion-98-EC-PC shows σ(25 °C) = 0.2×10^-4 S/cm and T_Li+ = 0.93 (Tables 2 and 4).

PT-0 is the non-fluorinated polymer electrolyte (PEGMEA:TFEMA = 1:0) tested in lithium-metal battery configurations. Evidence specific to PT-0:
- Composition/identity: “PEGMEA:TFEMA molar ratios of 1:0, 1:0.5, 1:1, and 1:2 were prepared and designated as PT-0, PT-0.5, PT-1, and PT-2 respectively.”
- Electrolyte use in batteries: “CR2032 coin cells were employed to perform electrochemical characterization of the polymer electrolytes… Li|PE|Li … Li|PE|NCM811.”
- Oxidative stability (no capacity numbers given for PT-0): “In contrast, the non-fluorinated sample PT-0 underwent anodic oxidation starting at approximately 4.5 V, as shown in Fig. 3d.”
- Li+ transference number for PT-0: “The calculated tLi+ values… are significantly higher than that of the non-fluorinated sample PT-0 (0.42), as shown in Figs. 3f and S10.”
- Activation energy for PT-0: “The calculated Ea values were 0.24, 0.25, 0.30, and 0.37 eV for PT-0, PT-0.5, PT-1, and PT-2 respectively.”
- Battery performance statement (no explicit mAh g^-1 reported for PT-0): “the Li||NCM811 cell containing non-fluorinated sample PT-0 showed rapid capacity fading and poor Coulombic efficiency… Fig. S16.”
Since no explicit discharge capacity or cycle metrics are reported for PT-0 in the provided text, capacity/selectivity/breakthrough/regeneration are left null.

PT-0.5 is used as the polymer electrolyte in Li|PT-0.5|NCM811 cells. Reported metrics specific to PT-0.5 include: (i) coin-cell rate performance: "Li|PT-0.5|NCM811 cell demonstrated specific discharge capacities of 181.5, 169.9, 145.3, and 120 mAh g^-1 at 0.1C, 0.2C, 0.5C, and 1C"; (ii) long-term cycling: "retained a reversible specific capacity of 118.06 mAh g^-1 (81.47% retention) after 200 cycles at 0.5C" and at 1C retained 76.89% over 300 cycles; (iii) pouch cell: 144.96 mAh g^-1 with 87.9% retention over 100 cycles at 0.2C; (iv) electrolyte properties: ionic conductivity 0.329 mS cm^-1 (30°C), t_Li+ = 0.64, oxidative stability up to 5.2 V vs. Li/Li+ for PT-0.5; (v) Li|PT-0.5|Li symmetric cell: stable plating/stripping over >1000 h at 0.1 mA cm^-2 with ~39 mV overpotential and CCD of 1.5 mA cm^-2.

PT-1 (PEGMEA:TFEMA = 1:1) is a fluorinated copolymer electrolyte evaluated in Li metal battery configurations. PT-1-specific performance reported includes: t_Li+ = 0.58 (Li|PE|Li, chronoamperometry at 30°C) and activation energy Ea = 0.30 eV from Arrhenius analysis (30–80°C). The manuscript provides no explicit coin/pouch cell discharge capacities or stability window for PT-1 in the main text; only qualitative comparisons (higher overpotentials and poorer rate/cycling performance vs PT-0.5). Hence capacity/selectivity/breakthrough/regeneration are left null.

PT-2 is a fluorinated PEGMEA:TFEMA=1:2 copolymer electrolyte used in Li-metal batteries. Reported PT-2 metrics include: ionic conductivity 0.118 mScm^-1 at 30 °C ("The ionic conductivity of fluorinated copolymers increases from 0.118 to 0.329 mScm^-1 as the molar ratio ... changed from 1:2 (PT-2) to 1:0.5 (PT-0.5)"); oxidative stability window up to 5.4 V vs. Li/Li+ ("PT-2 showed further improved oxidative stability extending up to 5.4 V"); Li+ transference number t_Li+ = 0.51 ("The calculated t_Li+ values ... PT-2 (0.51)"); activation energy Ea = 0.37 eV ("The calculated Ea values were ... 0.37 eV for ... PT-2"). Battery cycling and discharge capacity data are given quantitatively for PT-0.5, while PT-2 is described qualitatively as having higher polarization and poorer rate performance; no explicit PT-2 capacity numbers are provided, so capacity/selectivity/breakthrough/regeneration are left null.

F&C-PE is used as a solid polymer electrolyte in Li metal batteries. Reported metrics specific to F&C-PE: Li+ conductivity 1.06×10^-4 S cm^-1 at 30 °C; Li+ transference number t_Li+ = 0.53; oxidative stability 4.5 V (LSV); activation energy for Li+ migration 0.31 eV; Li/F&C-PE/Li symmetric cell cycles stably for 2000 h at 0.1 mA cm^-2 with ~50 mV polarization; also stable 900 h at 0.2 mA cm^-2; minimal interfacial impedance change from day 1 to 40; Li/F&C-PE/NCM811 full cell shows 82.2% capacity retention after 300 cycles at 0.2C with 99.8% coulombic efficiency and better rate performance (0.1C–1C) than control; thermal stability from room temperature to 200 °C; mechanical strength up to 2 MPa and ~300% elongation. These data pertain directly to F&C-PE.

FGPE1:1 is reported as an electrolyte for lithium metal batteries with ionic conductivity σ = 1.21 × 10^-3 S cm^-1 at 25 °C and Li+ transference number t_Li+ = 0.68 (vs. LE 0.41). The work states it is a promising electrolyte for high-performance lithium metal batteries and demonstrates full-cell performance: stable cycling over 200 cycles at 0.2C (2.5–4.2 V) with 87% capacity retention, and 126.17 mAh g^-1 at 1C; coulombic efficiency 92.78% over 10 cycles vs. LE's 62.77%.

No application data for FGPE1:2 appears in the provided text. All quantitative results (e.g., ionic conductivity, t_Li+, cycling, CEI/SEI, flammability, pouch-cell demo) are explicitly reported for FGPE1:1 or LE, with no mention of FGPE1:2.
The excerpt reports application/performance data for FGPE1:1 and LE only (e.g., t_Li+ = 0.68, σ = 1.21 mS/cm, LFP||FGPE1:1||Li cycling); no data specific to FGPE4:3 is provided.

In the provided excerpts, performance data and discussions are specific to FGPE1:1 (e.g., t_Li+, ionic conductivity, SEI/CEI analyses, cycling results). FGPE3:4 is not explicitly mentioned and no quantitative or qualitative application results are tied to FGPE3:4 here. Therefore, no application metrics can be extracted for FGPE3:4 from these fragments.

LLZTO nanoparticles act as the ion-conducting ceramic filler to incorporate with PVHM, resulting in the PVHML composite SSEs. The PVHML has a significant σ of 0.94 mS cm−1 at 25 °C. Additionally, PVHML-based symmetric Li cells exhibit a high critical current density of 0.70 mA cm−2 and remain stable over 2800 h at 0.1 mA cm−2, indicating solid-state electrolyte applicability where LLZTO is a component.
