PE–x% LCE@PEG (x = 40, 50, 60) gel polymer electrolytes were prepared and used in Li/Li symmetric cells and LiFePO4//Li cells. The ionic conductivity shows 1.21 × 10^-4, 1.73 × 10^-4, and 2.11 × 10^-4 S cm^-1 for PE–x% LCE@PEG (x = 40, 50, and 60) at 60 °C, respectively. The tLi+ values for PE–x% LCE@PEG (x = 40, 50, and 60) gel polymer electrolytes are 0.415, 0.425, and 0.5, respectively. The critical current densities of PE–x% LCE@PEG (x = 40, 50, and 60) are 1.1, 1.1, and 0.9 mA cm^-2, respectively. LiFePO4//Li cells were assembled employing PE–x% LCE@PEG (x = 40, 50, and 60) and LCE.

For PE-50% LCE@PEG used as gel polymer electrolyte in LiFePO4//Li cells: "the PE-50% LCE@PEG based cell can stably cycle for 100 cycles at 0.5 C under 80 °C, exhibiting a reversible discharging specific capacity of 158.5 mAh g^-1 at the 100th cycle with a capacity retention of 98.87%." At 60 °C, rate capability shows 164.8, 165, 160, 157, 152, 142, 63.6 mAh g^-1 at 0.1–5 C for PE-50% LCE@PEG. Additional electrolyte metrics for this sample: ionic conductivity 1.73×10^-4 S cm^-1 at 60 °C; Li+ transference number 0.425; critical current density 1.1 mA cm^-2; Li//Li symmetric cell cycling stability 1200 h at 0.1 mA cm^-2 and 0.1 mAh cm^-2 under 60 °C.

Direct text reports: '... showing 1.21 × 10^-4, 1.73 × 10^-4, and 2.11 × 10^-4 S cm^-1 for PE-x% LCE@PEG (x = 40, 50, and 60) at 60 °C, respectively.' and 'the t_Li+ values for PE-x% LCE@PEG (x = 40, 50, and 60) are 0.415, 0.425, and 0.5, respectively.' These explicitly provide ionic conductivity (2.11×10^-4 S cm^-1 at 60 °C) and Li+ transference number (0.5) for PE-60% LCE@PEG. Additional context (not encoded in fields): oxidation onset ~4.3 V vs Li/Li+ for all x and CCD for x=60 is 0.9 mA cm^-2.

Ionic conduction performance for GPE-0 is explicitly reported: "The ionic conductivity at room temperature of the GPE-0 ... was calculated to be 1.87 × 10^{-3} S cm^{-1}". Additional application-relevant performance for GPE-0 in SIBs: electrochemical stability window "GPE-4 had a wider electrochemical window of 5.16 V than GPE-0 (5.06 V)"; Na-ion transference number "GPE-4 showed a higher t_Na+ of 0.63 than GPE-0 (0.33)"; cell capacity at 0.5C "The first discharge specific capacity was recorded to be 88.8 mAh·g^{-1} for the GPE-0... After 100 cycles, the discharge capacity was recorded to be 85.0 mAh·g^{-1} with capacity retention ratio of 95.7%"; interfacial resistance in cells "before cycling showed the resistance of 422 Ω ... After cycling 100 times, the resistance increased to 1030 Ω for the ... GPE-0 ... battery."

GPE-2 is a PVDF-HFP/PMMA/TPU gel polymer electrolyte containing 2 wt% Na3Zr2Si2PO12. Its room-temperature ionic conductivity was reported as 2.40 × 10^-3 S cm^-1 (Fig. 4(b)). In Na3V2(PO4)3/C|GPEs|Na coin cells at 0.5C, the first discharge specific capacity for GPE-2 was 92.1 mAh g^-1, and after 100 cycles 90.9 mAh g^-1 with 98.7% retention (Fig. 6(a)).

GPE-4 denotes the PVDF-HFP/PMMA/TPU gel polymer electrolyte with 4 wt% Na3Zr2Si2PO12 filler. Room-temperature ionic conductivity for GPE-4 is 2.83 × 10^-3 S cm^-1 (Fig. 4b: “GPE-4 … 2.83 × 10^-3 S cm^-1”). The Na-ion transference number increases to t_Na+ = 0.63 for GPE-4 (Fig. 5a,b: “GPE-4 showed a higher t_Na+ of 0.63 than GPE-0 (0.33)”). Electrochemical window for GPE-4 is 5.16 V (Fig. 4d: “GPE-4 had a wider electrochemical window of 5.16 V”). In Na3V2(PO4)3/C|GPE-4|Na cells at 0.5C: first discharge specific capacity 92.7 mAh g^-1 and after 100 cycles 92.0 mAh g^-1 (99.2% retention) (Fig. 6a); areal capacity 0.177 mAh/cm^2 (text: “The areal capacity of GPE-4 sample was calculated to be 0.177 mAh/cm^2”). Rate performance for GPE-4 cell: 99.8, 98.9, 98.6, 97.6, 95.5, 83.9 mAh g^-1 at 0.1C–5C (Fig. 6e). Symmetric Na|GPE-4|Na shows smaller, stable polarization at 0.5 mA cm^-2 (Fig. 5c).

GPE-6 is a PVDF-HFP/PMMA/TPU gel polymer electrolyte containing 6 wt% Na3Zr2Si2PO12, activated with 1 M NaClO4 in EC/DMC (1:1). As an electrolyte for Na-ion batteries, its room-temperature ionic conductivity is reported as 2.15×10^-3 S cm^-1. In Na3V2(PO4)3/C|GPEs|Na coin cells at 0.5C, the GPE-6 sample shows a first discharge capacity of 90.9 mAh g^-1 and 90.1 mAh g^-1 after 100 cycles (99.1% retention). Porosity for GPE-6 is 12.34% (context for ion transport).

GPE-8 was evaluated as electrolyte in Na3V2(PO4)3/C|GPEs|Na sodium batteries at 0.5C: first discharge specific capacity 90.6 mAh/g; after 100 cycles 89.8 mAh/g with 99.1% retention. Its room-temperature ionic conductivity was 1.89e-3 S/cm.

PVDF (Solef 5130) used as binder for C-LiFePO4 cathodes. At C-rate after 50 cycles, discharge capacity is 129 mAh/g with capacity retention 101% (Fig. 6d). At C/10 and C-rate, PVDF delivers 146 and 125 mAh/g, respectively (Fig. 5b–c). Coulombic efficiency for PVDF is 93% (initial) and 99% (mean) (Fig. 6c). Supporting PVDF-specific properties: contact angle 22.2 ± 4.3°, DC-electrical conductivity 0.57 ± 0.11 S/cm, swelling 18.0 ± 0.5% (Table 3), overall impedance ~437 Ω (Fig. 4b).

PVDF-HFP (Solef 21216) was used as a binder in C-LiFePO4 cathodes. Reported discharge capacities for PVDF-HFP were 139 mAh/g at C/10 and 97 mAh/g at C-rate; after 50 cycles at C-rate the discharge capacity was 75.5 mAh/g with 84% retention. The initial and mean coulombic efficiency for PVDF-HFP were 93% and 97%, respectively.

PVDF-TrFE is evaluated as a binder for C-LiFePO4 cathodes. For PVDF-TrFE, the discharge capacities are 130, 133, 124 and 80 mAh·g−1 at C/10, C/5, C/2 and C-rates, respectively (Fig. 5a). After 50 cycles at C-rate, the discharge capacity value is 94.3 mAh·g−1 with capacity retention of 89% (Fig. 6d). The initial and mean coulombic efficiency for PVDF-TrFE are 98% and 99%.

Ionic conductivity for NPH-GPE (NPH-130%) at 25 °C: "The NPH-130% shows a conductivity of 3.16 × 10^-4 S cm^-1 at 70 °C and a remarkable high ionic conductivity value of 1.21 × 10^-4 S cm^-1 at 25 °C" (Results 3.2, Fig. 2a). Lithium transference number: "the value is 0.57 for NPH-GPE" measured at room temperature via Bruce–Vincent method (Fig. 2e).

NPH-130% (NPH-GPE with 130% electrolyte uptake) is used as the electrolyte in Li metal and LFP//Li coin cells. Its ionic conductivity is explicitly reported as 3.16×10^-4 S cm^-1 at 70 °C and 1.21×10^-4 S cm^-1 at 25 °C; NPH-130% is chosen for further evaluation (Fig. 2(a,b)). Symmetric Li/NPH-GPE/Li shows stable operation for 500 h at 25 °C with ~0.11 V overpotential at 0.1 mA cm^-2, and ~0.04 V at 70 °C (Fig. 3(b,c)). LFP/NPH-130%/Li delivers 150.67 mAh g^-1 after 200 cycles at 0.5 C and retains 70% after 500 cycles at 70 °C with ~99% coulombic efficiency (Fig. 4(a,b)); at 25 °C it shows 150 mAh g^-1 with 98.65% retention after 100 cycles at 0.2 C (Fig. 4(c)). Rate performance at 70 °C: 155, 120, 95, 86 mAh g^-1 at 0.5, 2, 7, 10 C, respectively, recovering to 155 mAh g^-1 at 0.1 C (Fig. 5(d,e)).

Text states: "The PH-SPE electrolyte with salt concentration of 80 wt.% shows the highest conductivity, 4.786 × 10^{-5} S cm^{-1} at 25 °C (Fig. S7)." This directly provides the ion conduction performance for PH-SPE.

Material PH-GPE is defined as PVDF-HFP based gel polymer electrolyte obtained by immersing PH-SPE in 1 mol L^-1 LiPF6 in EC/DMC (1:1 v/v) for 6 h (Section 2.2). Performance tied to PH-GPE: (i) At 70 °C, initial discharge capacity for LFP/PH-GPE/Li is 147 mAh g^-1 (Fig. 4a text: "The initial discharge capacities of batteries assembled with ... PH-GPE are ... 147 mAh g^-1"). (ii) At 25 °C, after 100 cycles, LFP/PH-GPE/Li shows a discharge capacity of 134.8 mAh g^-1 (Fig. 4c text: "the discharge capacities of LFP/PH-GPE/Li and LFP/NMP-LE/Li are only 134.8 and 59.45 mAh g^-1"). Additional interface data (not mapped to fields): in Li//PH-GPE//Li symmetric cells at 25 °C and 0.1 mA cm^-2, overpotential >0.2 V and short circuiting occurs after 300 h (Fig. 3b text).

PPZ-0% corresponds to the PVDF-HFP/PMMA GPE (PPZ-X% with X = 0%). Table 2 lists ionic conductivity for PVDF-HFP/PMMA as 1.31 mS cm-1 at 25 °C (activation energy 0.172 eV). The lithium-ion transference number for PVDF-HFP/PMMA is 0.35 (Figure 5c–e). Its electrochemical stability window is 4.46 V (Figure 5f). In symmetric Li||Li tests, the cell with PVDF-HFP/PMMA short-circuits when the current density is increased to 0.4 mA cm-2 (Figure 6d). In LiFePO4|GPE|Li cells at 0.5 C, the initial discharge capacity with PVDF-HFP/PMMA is 150.4 mAh g-1 (Figure 7a).

Table 2 reports ionic conductivities for PPZ-2%: 1.11 mS cm-1 (25 °C), 1.28 (35 °C), 1.32 (45 °C), 1.84 (55 °C), 2.25 (65 °C), 2.42 (75 °C); activation energy Ea = 0.175 eV. These data are for a PVDF-HFP/PMMA–ZrO2 (2 wt%) gel polymer electrolyte used in Li battery context; no battery-level metrics specific to PPZ-2% were provided.

PPZ-4% is a PVDF-HFP/PMMA–ZrO2 gel polymer electrolyte (GPE) used in Li|GPE|Li and LiFePO4|GPE|Li cells. Table 2 reports ionic conductivity for PPZ-4%: 1.22 mS cm-1 (25 °C), 1.72 mS cm-1 (35 °C), 2.02 mS cm-1 (45 °C), 2.30 mS cm-1 (55 °C), 2.56 mS cm-1 (65 °C), 2.84 mS cm-1 (75 °C), with Ea = 0.165 eV. Battery cycling and LSV window values are provided for PPZ-6% (not PPZ-4%), so no discharge capacity or stability window specific to PPZ-4% can be extracted.

PPZ-6% (PVDF-HFP/PMMA–ZrO2-6%) is used as a gel polymer electrolyte for Li batteries. Table 2 reports its ionic conductivity of 1.46 mS cm−1 at 25 °C. The Li-ion transference number tLi+ is 0.50 (Figure 5c–e). LSV shows an electrochemical stability window up to ~4.65 V vs Li+/Li (Figure 5f). In Li|PPZ-6%|Li symmetric cells, stable plating/stripping is maintained for 400 h at 0.1 mA cm−2 with low overpotential and no short circuit up to 0.8 mA cm−2 (Figure 6a–d). In LiFePO4|PPZ-6%|Li cells, at 0.5 C the cell delivers 153.0 mAh g−1 and ~99% average Coulombic efficiency after 200 cycles; rate capacities are 160.2, 158.8, 154.5, 149.7, 140.3, and 127.9 mAh g−1 at 0.1–5 C, and at 1 C shows 150.2 mAh g−1 after 200 cycles with 99.8% capacity retention (Figure 7).
Table 2 reports ionic conductivity for PPZ-8% as 0.86 mS cm-1 at 25 °C (with values at higher temperatures and Ea = 0.151 eV also listed). The text notes PPZ-8% has lower conductivity than PPZ-6%.

No direct application performance is reported for carbon support C-1 alone. Performance data (e.g., discharge capacities) are given for the composites LFP/C-1, LFP/C-2, LFP/C-3, not for C-1 itself: “The obtained LiFePO4/C composites were denoted as LFP/C-1, LFP/C-2, LFP/C-3, which were prepared by using carbon supports C-1, C-2, and C-3, respectively.” and “Samples LFP/C-1 and LFP/C-2 deliver the initial discharge capacities of 68.1 and 106.5 mA h g−1… (Table 2).” For C-1, only textural parameters are provided (Table 1: Vtotal=1.04 cm3 g−1, SBET=563 m2 g−1). Therefore no application metrics can be assigned to C-1.

C-2 is used to make the composite LFP/C-2: "The obtained LiFePO4/C composites were denoted as LFP/C-1, LFP/C-2, LFP/C-3, which were prepared by using carbon supports C-1, C-2, and C-3, respectively." Table 2 lists rate capacities for LFP/C-2: "LFP/C-2 ... 0.1 C: 106.5; ... 1 C: 102.3 mA h g−1" and Fig. 6 note: "The specific capacity is based on the entire weight of a LiFePO4/C composite." Additionally, the text states: "Samples LFP/C-1 and LFP/C-2 deliver the initial discharge capacities of ... 106.5 mA h g−1 (based on the weight of composite...)."

C-3 is a coralloid carbon framework used only as the support; all performance data (capacities, rate, cycling) are reported for LiFePO4/C composites (e.g., LFP/C-3) and for pure LiFePO4, not for C-3 alone. Electrochemical tests are explicitly described for LiFePO4/C composites in coin cells, with no application metrics for bare C-3.

For sample LFP/C-1 used as a Li-ion battery cathode, Table 2 reports rate capacities (based on the weight of the LFP/C composite): 68.1 mA h g-1 at 0.1 C, 59.9 at 0.2 C, 53.8 at 0.5 C, 50.3 at 1 C, 43.9 at 2 C, 33.5 at 5 C, and 23.1 at 10 C. The text states: “Samples LFP/C-1 and LFP/C-2 deliver the initial discharge capacities of 68.1 and 106.5 mA h g−1 … with nearly 100% capacity retention over 50 cycles.” Test conditions (for context): CR2025 Li half-cells, 2.5–4.2 V, ~298 K, electrode composition 80 wt% active/15 wt% carbon black/5 wt% PVDF, electrolyte 1 M LiPF6 in DMC/EMC/EC (1:1:1).

LIB cathode performance for LFP/C-2: 'Samples LFP/C-1 and LFP/C-2 deliver the initial discharge capacities of 68.1 and 106.5 mA h g-1 (based on the weight of composite...)' at 0.1 C (Fig. 6a). Table 2 gives LFP/C-2 rate capacities (mA h g-1): 0.1 C 106.5; 0.2 C 105.8; 0.5 C 102.2; 1 C 102.3; 2 C 95.9; 5 C 78.4; 10 C 54.8. Conditions: CR2025 coin cells, 2.5–4.2 V vs Li/Li+, room temperature.

LFP/C-3 used as a Li-ion battery cathode (2.5–4.2 V, 298 K) shows discharge capacities based on the whole composite: 144.6 mA h g^-1 at 0.1 C; 128.4 mA h g^-1 at 1 C; 83 mA h g^-1 at 10 C; 60.4 mA h g^-1 at 20 C (Table 2, Fig. 6b). High-rate cycling: retains 96.7% of its initial capacity at 10 C over 1000 cycles with coulombic efficiency >99% and delivers specific power 5114 W kg^-1 (Fig. 8a).

Electrochemical tests (CR2025 coin half-cells, Li metal counter, 1 M LiPF6 in DMC/EMC/EC (1:1:1), 2.5–4.2 V, room temperature): “It is obvious that the pure LiFePO4 shows a lower initial discharge capacity of 70.4 mA h g−1 with a substantial drop in the plateau potential compared with LiFePO4/C composites.” (Fig. 6a; 0.1 C corresponds to 17 mA g−1). Table 2 lists Pure-LFP capacities: 0.1 C 70, 0.2 C 58.7, 0.5 C 46.8, 1 C 39.1, 2 C 31.2 mA h g−1.

MDPE is used as a solid polymer electrolyte (and binder) in all-solid-state Li metal batteries: "The cycling stability is significantly improved reaching 170 mAh g^{-1} at 0.1 C with a capacity retention of more than 80% and coulombic efficiency close to 100% at 60 °C." Additional performance details directly for MDPE include a wide electrochemical window (4.9 V vs Li+/Li), highest ionic conductivity 3×10^{-4} S cm^{-1} at 60 °C (70 wt.% LiTFSI), Li+ transference number t_Li+ = 0.76, stable Li/MDPE/Li cycling for 1000 h without short circuit, and >50 mAh g^{-1} at 2 C.

UPE is defined as the ultraviolet polymerized electrolyte membrane without UPy-CNTs and is evaluated as a gel electrolyte for Li metal batteries. Reported UPE-specific performance includes: Li+ transference number t_Li+ = 0.36; ionic conductivity at 30°C = 0.177 mS cm^-1 with activation energy Ea = 0.224 eV; critical current density (Li||Li) = 0.3 mA cm^-2. In Li||Li symmetric cells at 0.1 mA cm^-2, 0.1 mAh cm^-2, UPE-based cell cycles to 1795 h with overpotential rising to 108 mV; at 0.2 mA cm^-2, 0.2 mAh cm^-2 it short-circuits at 278 h (initial overpotential ~40 mV). SEM after 100 h shows initial dendrite formation on Li with UPE. In Li||LFP full cells, UPE shows poorer rate performance than UPEC1.0 and exhibits ~250 mV polarization at 2C. These data are all specific to the UPE sample as a Li+ conducting gel electrolyte.

UPEC_0.5 is a gel electrolyte for lithium metal batteries. Direct metrics reported for UPEC_0.5 include: (1) Critical current density (CCD) = 0.5 mA cm^-2: “The CCD for LE, UPE, UPEC_0.5, UPEC_1.5, and UPEC_2.0 is 0.2 mA cm^-2, 0.3 mA cm^-2, 0.5 mA cm^-2, 0.8 mA cm^-2, 0.7 mA cm^-2, respectively (Fig. 4d and S13).” (2) Li||Li symmetric cell cycling lifetime at 0.1 mA cm^-2, 0.1 mAh cm^-2: “cells with UPEC_0.5 … cycle to 1159 h … (Fig. S14)”. (3) Li||Li symmetric cell cycling lifetime at 0.2 mA cm^-2, 0.2 mAh cm^-2: “UPEC_0.5 … cycle for 88 h before a short circuit (Fig. S15).” Other quantitative properties (ionic conductivity, Li+ transference number, full-cell capacities) were not provided for UPEC_0.5 specifically; thus capacity/selectivity/breakthrough/regeneration are left null.

UPEC_1 corresponds to UPEC1.0 (1 wt% UPy-CNTs). Performance in Li metal batteries is repeatedly reported for this specific sample: Li||UPEC1.0||LFP shows specific capacities of 167, 160, 150, 141, 120 mAh/g at 0.1C, 0.2C, 0.5C, 1C, 2C, respectively; capacity returns to 166 mAh/g when back to 0.1C. It sustains 146 mAh/g after 200 cycles at 0.5C and 111 mAh/g after 100 cycles at 1C. Additional electrolyte metrics for UPEC1.0: ionic conductivity 0.226 mS/cm at 30°C; Li+ transference number 0.79; critical current density 0.9 mA/cm^2; Li||Li symmetric cells cycle stably 2500 h at 0.1 mA/cm^2, 0.1 mAh/cm^2 (overpotential 22→54 mV) and >850 h at 0.2 mA/cm^2, 0.2 mAh/cm^2 (32→45 mV). Coulombic efficiency in Li||Cu reaches 97.82% at the 100th cycle.

Critical current density (CCD) for UPEC_1.5 is 0.8 mA cm−2: "The CCD for LE, UPE, UPEC0.5, UPEC1.5, and UPEC2.0 is 0.2 mA cm−2, 0.3 mA cm−2, 0.5 mA cm−2, 0.8 mA cm−2, 0.7 mA cm−2, respectively (Fig. 4d and S13)." Symmetric-cell cycling lifetime for UPEC_1.5 at 0.1 mA cm−2, 0.1 mAh cm−2: "In Fig. S14, cells with UPEC0.5, UPEC1.5, and UPEC2.0 cycle to 1159 h, 1917 h, and 1727 h, respectively," under the test described in Fig. 5a (0.1 mA cm−2, 0.1 mAh cm−2). Additional condition-specific lifetime at higher current (not recorded above): "under the conditions of a current density of 0.2 mA cm−2 and areal capacity of 0.2 mAh cm−2, UPEC0.5, UPEC1.5, and UPEC2.0 cycle for 88 h, 500 h, and 360 h before a short circuit, respectively (Fig. S15)."

CCD: “The CCD for LE, UPE, UPEC0.5, UPEC1.5, and UPEC2.0 is 0.2 mA cm−2, 0.3 mA cm−2, 0.5 mA cm−2, 0.8 mA cm−2, 0.7 mA cm−2, respectively (Fig. 4d and S13).” Cycle life (Li||Li): “In Fig. S14, cells with UPEC0.5, UPEC1.5, and UPEC2.0 cycle to 1159 h, 1917 h, and 1727 h, respectively,” tested at 0.1 mA cm−2 and 0.1 mAh cm−2. At higher current density (0.2 mA cm−2, 0.2 mAh cm−2), UPEC2.0 cycles for 360 h before short circuit (Fig. S15). No specific ionic conductivity, Li+ transference number, or full-cell capacity values were reported for UPEC2.0.

SiO2@C (carbon-coated porous silica) was used as anode in Li-ion coin cells (2016 type) with Li metal counter electrode and 1 M LiPF6 in EC/DEC (1:1), cycled at 0.2 mA/cm^2 between 0.001–1.5 V vs Li/Li+. Reported specific discharge capacity for SiO2@C: first-cycle discharge capacity 281 mAh/g; during cycling, capacity evolved from 95 mAh/g to 222 mAh/g. Values for the boric-acid variant (SiO2@C + B) are excluded.

Li-ion battery anode testing for SiO2@C + B showed: (i) initial discharge capacity 382 mAh g^-1 for SiO2@C + B (Fig. 7b: “At the first cycle, the discharge capacity was ... 382 mAhg^-1 for SiO2@C + B”), (ii) capacity increased during cycling with “enhancement of the specific capacity from 195 mAh^-1 to 503 mAh^-1 ... especially from SiO2@C + B” (Fig. 8b discussion), and (iii) “the carbon-coated silica macroparticles processed with boric acid show highest charge capacity of 500 mAh g^-1 after 103 cycles” (Conclusions). Test conditions: coin cell (2016), Li metal counter, 1 M LiPF6 in EC/DEC (1:1), 0.2 mA cm^-2, 0.001–1.5 V vs Li/Li+.

file_name: Adv Funct Materials - 2024 - You - High‐Voltage All‐Solid‐State Lithium Metal Batteries Enabled by Localized.md

parse.why: NCM811/F-PISE/Li cell exhibits an initial discharge capacity of 157.6 mAh g^-1 at 0.5C and 30°C, a high average Coulombic efficiency of 99.88% and ~98% capacity retention after 500 cycles (Figure 6a). F-PISE shows an ESW up to 5.2 V vs Li+/Li at 30°C (Figure 4a,b), supporting its role as a high-voltage polymer electrolyte.

DFNCA electrolyte: 0.25 M LiNO3 + 0.9 M LiDFOB in DFNCA/FEC (v:v = 1:1). Li||Cu (Aurbach method) showed CE as high as 99% in the DFNCA electrolyte (Figure 2a). Long-term galvanostatic cycling: average CE ~97% up to 400 cycles at 0.5 mA cm^-2, 0.5 mAh cm^-2 (Figure 2b). Li||Li symmetric cells displayed stable overpotential (~50 mV) after >1000 h (Figure 2i). Li||NCM811 full cell delivered a specific capacity of 148.2 mAh/g after 100 cycles with 77% retention (Figure 4a–c). Anode-free Cu||LiFePO4 pouch cells achieved 212 Wh/kg and 64% retention after 50 cycles (Figure 4d).

Evidence specific to Carbonate electrolyte (1 M LiPF6 in EC/DEC 1:1):
- Li/Cu cells (Aurbach method): CE was 83%, and the voltage hysteresis suddenly increased after ~95 h, indicating prefailure (Figure 2a).
- Li/Cu long-term cycling at 0.5 mA cm^-2, 0.5 mAh cm^-2: CE plummeted after 75 cycles under the same test conditions (Figure 2b).
- Li/Li symmetric cells at 0.5 mA cm^-2, 0.5 mAh cm^-2: overpotential increased from ~40 mV at ~200 h to 313.4 mV at ~350 h due to gradual SEI thickening (Figure 2i).
- Li||NCM811 full cells (0.25 C after two cycles at 0.1 C, 3–4.3 V): capacity showed a sharp decline after 25 cycles and was nearly zero after 100 cycles (Figure 4a,b).
- Anode-free Cu||LiFePO4 pouch cells (0.3 C charge / 0.5 C discharge): capacity quickly fell below 50 mAh with 23% retention after 40 cycles (Figure 4d).
- Li+ transference number (t_Li+): 0.30 for the carbonate electrolyte (Figure 1f).
- Interfacial chemistry indicating poor stability in carbonate: higher organic content in SEI (C atomic ratio 18.6% outer layer, 15.3% after 60 s sputtering), higher RLi fraction (59%), and stronger C2H2- signals extending through the SEI, consistent with solvent (EC) decomposition and an organic-rich fragile SEI (Figure 3c–f; Figure S11–S12).

file_name: In situ cross-linked plastic crystal electrolytes toward superior lithium metal batteries.md

parse.why: CSPE is used as an in-situ polymerized quasi-solid electrolyte for Li metal batteries. R&D reports: ionic conductivity of CSPE at 30 °C is 0.32 mS/cm (Fig. 2a); at −20 °C it is 9.8×10^-5 S/cm; oxidative decomposition at 5.3 V vs. Li+/Li (Fig. 2b); Li+ transference number tLi+ = 0.58 (Figs. 2c–d). In cells: Li/CSPE/Li symmetric stable for 700 h at 0.5 mA/cm^2 (Fig. 2f); Li/CSPE/Cu shows ~90% Coulombic efficiency for 150 cycles at 0.1 mA/cm^2 (Fig. 2e). LCO/CSPE/Li coin cells at 4.3 V retain 80% capacity after 400 cycles with ~99.5% CE (Fig. 3a); rate performance: ~136.9 mAh/g at 1C and 102.7 mAh/g at 5C (Fig. 3b); 4.4 V cycling retains 130.5 mAh/g after 200 cycles (Fig. 3c); 4.5 V cycling delivers 146.7 mAh/g after 100 cycles with 99.3% CE (Figs. S12–S13). Safety: CSPE shows slow ignition (~5 s) and self-extinguishing (Fig. S20).

file_name: In situ cross-linked plastic crystal electrolytes toward superior lithium metal batteries.md

parse.why: Performance data explicitly reported for SN-LiDFOB electrolyte: (1) Ionic conductivity: “the ionic conductivity of CSPE and SN-LiDFOB electrolytes at 30 °C are 0.32 mS/cm and 0.34 mS/cm.” (2) Electrochemical stability window upper limit: “the CSPE shows an oxidative decomposition at 5.3 V (vs. Li+/Li), which is much higher than that of the SN-LiDFOB electrolyte (5.0 V vs. Li+/Li).” (3) Li+ transference number: “the (t_Li+) of CSPE and SN-LiDFOB electrolytes are 0.58 and 0.30, respectively.” (4) Li/Li symmetric cell stability: “the Li/SN-LiDFOB/Li cell fails in about 6 h” at 0.5 mA/cm^2. Additional context: a 4.3 V LCO/SN-LiDFOB/Li cell’s discharge capacity drops to nearly 0 after 20 cycles.

file_name: Journal of Polymer Science Part A  Polymer Chemistry - 2019 - Quast - Membrane preparation from hyperbranched.md

parse.why: The paper frames HBFP for proton-exchange applications: "so that the polymer can be used as the matrix for a proton exchange membrane." It concludes: "These new hyperbranched ionomers and copolymers are strong candidates for future use in anhydrous proton exchange membranes." It also states: "In a future study, the most promising membrane composition will be investigated for proton transport with varying amounts and types of proton transporting groups." No proton transport metrics are reported.

file_name: Journal of Polymer Science Part A  Polymer Chemistry - 2019 - Quast - Membrane preparation from hyperbranched.md

parse.why: In this paper LAE1 is used as a linear perfluorinated aryl ether polymer toughening agent within HBFP blends; no application performance (e.g., proton conductivity, adsorption capacity, catalytic conversion) is reported for LAE1. Evidence: “As a control, LAE1 was cast from DMAc without any HBFP, which resulted in a transparent film. The AFM micrograph (Fig. 5) showed a surface free of pores, but with cavities of >5 μm.” and “Without any crosslinking agent, the HBFP/LAE1 blends were brittle and turbid… DMA of the ethylene glycol crosslinked HBPF/LAE1 blends (Table 1)…” and “The TMP crosslinked membranes were characterized by DMA (Table 2).” These sections provide morphology and mechanical data only; no functional application metrics suitable for capacity/selectivity/breakthrough/regeneration were given for LAE1.

file_name: Journal of Polymer Science Part A  Polymer Chemistry - 2019 - Quast - Membrane preparation from hyperbranched.md

parse.why: LAE2 (a polyaryl ether sulfone) was used as a toughening linear polymer in membranes blended with HBFP and functionalized HBFPs, with mechanical performance characterized. Evidence: "To improve blending, the LAE1 polymer was replaced with the poly ether sulfone derivative, LAE2..." and "Blending/Crosslinking Functionalized HBFP with Linear Aryl Ether Polymer LAE2 and Various Crosslinking Agents". Tables 3 and 4 report DMA mechanical properties for 30FPSA50/70LAE2 and 30FPIM50/70LAE2 membranes. The conclusions state: "By using a more polar, polyaryl ether sulfone toughening agent (LAE2), improved phase separation and mechanical properties were seen." No adsorption/catalysis targets, capacities, breakthrough, or regeneration data were reported for LAE2.

file_name: Journal of Polymer Science Part A  Polymer Chemistry - 2019 - Quast - Membrane preparation from hyperbranched.md

parse.why: FPSA50 is the 50% sulfonated HBFP used in blends ("As a starting point, the FPSA50 (50% sulfonated HBFP) was used for FPSA/HBFP blends..."). The intended application is proton exchange membranes: "These new hyperbranched ionomers and copolymers are strong candidates for future use in anhydrous proton exchange membranes." and "As the planned future use for these membranes in this laboratory is proton exchange membranes..." No proton transport performance for FPSA50 was reported; only mechanical properties of 30FPSA50/70LAE2 blends were provided (Table 3), and films became brittle upon dehydration.

file_name: Journal of Polymer Science Part A  Polymer Chemistry - 2019 - Quast - Membrane preparation from hyperbranched.md

parse.why: Abstract: "These new hyperbranched ionomers and copolymers are strong candidates for future use in anhydrous proton exchange membranes." Results: "Polymers with three different sulfonate contents were prepared (50, 85, and 100%, designated as FPSA50, FPSA85, and FPSA100, respectively). Blends of 85% sulfonated polymer with LAE1 were prepared at 30 by 70% mass ratios, respectively. For each blend with LAE1 and no crosslinking agent, membranes were too brittle for mechanical analysis and phase separated on the macroscale. No sulfonated HBFP/LAE1 blend was further characterized." "When similar conditions were used for FPSA85 and FPSA100 blends with LAE2, all films were similar in appearance, although the mechanical properties were much worse. The blends could not be cut for mechanical testing at any crosslinking concentration (0, 10, 20, or 30% TMP)."

file_name: Journal of Polymer Science Part A  Polymer Chemistry - 2019 - Quast - Membrane preparation from hyperbranched.md

parse.why: FPSA100 is the 100% sulfonated HBFP prepared as an ionomer for proton-exchange membrane development: "These new hyperbranched ionomers and copolymers are strong candidates for future use in anhydrous proton exchange membranes." FPSA100-specific result: "When similar conditions were used for FPSA85 and FPSA100 blends with LAE2, all films were similar in appearance, although the mechanical properties were much worse. The blends could not be cut for mechanical testing at any crosslinking concentration (0, 10, 20, or 30% TMP)." No proton transport or related performance metrics were reported for FPSA100.

file_name: Journal of Polymer Science Part A  Polymer Chemistry - 2019 - Quast - Membrane preparation from hyperbranched.md

parse.why: FPIM50 is a 50% imidazole-substituted HBFP used in membranes: “An imidazole substituted HBFP was prepared… A 50% imidazole substituted HBFP (FPIM50) was blended with LAE2 with TMP crosslinking.” The intended application is stated: “These new hyperbranched ionomers and copolymers are strong candidates for future use in anhydrous proton exchange membranes.” and “We are planning to use imidazole to develop a dry fuel cell membrane.” No proton conductivity or related performance metrics were reported; only mechanical properties of FPIM50/LAE2 blends were measured.

file_name: Long-chain fluorocarbon-driven hybrid solid polymer electrolyte for lithium metal batteries.md

parse.why: LFSPE is a long-chain-fluorocarbon-driven hybrid solid polymer electrolyte used in solid lithium metal batteries. Its ionic conductivity is 6.78×10^-4 S cm^-1 at 25 °C and 1.37×10^-3 S cm^-1 at 80 °C; activation energy 0.12 eV (Fig. 4a,b). It remains electrochemically stable under 4.713 V (Fig. 4c) and exhibits t_Li+ = 0.47 (Fig. 4d). Symmetric PDDA-TFSI@Li|LFSPE|PDDA-TFSI@Li cells show low polarization at 0.05/0.1/0.2 mA cm^-2 (30/51/86 mV) and retain ~22 mV for >1200 h when current returns to 0.05 mA cm^-2; stable overpotential for 2000 h at 0.2 mA h cm^-2 (Fig. 4e,f; Fig. S4). In full cells at 25 °C, LFP|LFSPE delivers 163.8 mAh g^-1 at 0.1C and 138.8 mAh g^-1 at 1C, with 98.8% capacity retention after 500 cycles at 0.5C (150.4 mAh g^-1) and >99.5% coulombic efficiency (Fig. 5). LCO|LFSPE achieves 132.5/127.3/119.3/109.6 mAh g^-1 at 0.1/0.2/0.5/1C and retains 99.6 mAh g^-1 after 300 cycles at 0.5C (0.062% per-cycle fading) (Fig. 6a,c,e). NCM811|LFSPE shows 218.9/198.5/174.2/145.8 mAh g^-1 at 0.1/0.2/0.5/1C, recovers to 204.8 mAh g^-1 when rate returns to 0.1C, and retains >71.9% after 300 cycles at 0.5C (Fig. 6b,d,f).

PC-1 was used to prepare a solid polymer electrolyte (SPE) with 30 wt% LiTFSI. Its ionic conductivity at 25 °C was reported as 4.86×10^-6 S·cm^-1, measured by EIS. Evidence: “We then prepared SPEs by mixing the different polymers (PC-1-3) with 30 wt% LiTFSI… At 25 °C, an ionic conductivity of 3.75 × 10^-5 S·cm^-1 was measured for PC-3 which is 7.8 times higher than that obtained for PC-1 (4.86 × 10^-6 S·cm^-1).”

PC-2 (prepared from PEO 2000 g/mol) was formulated as an SPE with 30 wt% LiTFSI and evaluated by EIS alongside PC-1 and PC-3: "We then prepared SPEs by mixing the different polymers (PC-1-3) with 30 wt% LiTFSI ... The ionic conductivity of the three SPEs were evaluated ... The results are depicted in Figure 2". The trend shows increasing conductivity with PEO molar mass; a specific Tg for PC-2 SPE is reported: "The Tg of SPEs decreased with the molar mass of PEO, from −37 °C for PC-1, to −41 °C for PC-2 and to −44 °C for PC-3." Battery test conditions mention PC-2 as electrolyte material: "... battery performances were conducted ... using LiFePO4 as working electrode, Li metal as counter and reference electrode, and solid poly(β-oxo-carbonate) (PC-1, PC-2, PC-3) as solid electrolyte." No explicit ionic conductivity value is given in text for PC-2; values are only shown in a figure.

PC-3 was evaluated as a Li-salt-containing solid polymer electrolyte: "We then prepared SPEs by mixing the different polymers (PC-1-3) with 30 wt% LiTFSI... The ionic conductivity of the three SPEs were evaluated... At 25 °C, an ionic conductivity of 3.75 × 10^-5 S.cm^-1 was measured for PC-3... The ionic conductivity of PC-3 (3.75 × 10^-5 S.cm^-1 at room temperature and 1.86 × 10^-4 S.cm^-1 at 60 °C)".

PC-4 (crosslinkable poly(oxo-carbonate)) is used to form a cross-linked solid polymer electrolyte (SIN-PC-3) by photochemical crosslinking with diethyleneglycol diacrylate in the presence of PC-3 and LiTFSI (formulation example: PC-3 41.4 wt%, LiTFSI 29 wt%, PC-4 13.8 wt%, diethylglycol diacrylate 13.8 wt%, photoinitiator 2 wt%). For this PC-4–containing SIN electrolyte, the ionic conductivity is 1.11 × 10^-5 S·cm^-1 at room temperature (Figure 3a). The lithium transference number tLi+ is 0.28 at 70 °C (Figure S7). The electrochemical stability window is up to 5 V vs Li+/Li at 70 °C (Figure S8). With 10 wt% tetraglyme added to the same SIN system, the room-temperature ionic conductivity increases to 4.7 × 10^-5 S·cm^-1 (Figure S9).

F2DEM is used as a single-solvent ether electrolyte for lithium metal batteries at 1.75 M LiFSI / F2DEM. In Li||Cu half cells (modified Aurbach protocol), the average CE of F2DEM is 99.53 ± 0.03% (four cells). In Li||Li symmetric cells (3 mAh cm^-2 per cycle), F2DEM shows ~30 mV overpotential at 1 mA cm^-2 (about 50% lower than F5DEE’s ~55 mV). Transport numbers for F2DEM are >0.4 across tested concentrations. LSV indicates improved oxidative stability: leakage current onset ~6 V on Al and ~4 V on Pt for F2DEM. In Li||Cu long-term cycling, F2DEM reaches 99% CE in ~12 cycles under fast plating/slow stripping (1 mA cm^-2 plating, 0.4 mA cm^-2 stripping, 2 mAh cm^-2). In Cu||LFP anode-free pouch cells (2.5–3.65 V, 0.5 mL electrolyte, nominal 210 mAh), F2DEM shows higher capacity utilization and slower capacity loss than F4DEE/F5DEE across C/2→2C and C/2→C/5 protocols. In Li||LFP coin cells (20 μm Li, 3.5 mAh cm^-2 LFP, 2.5–3.8 V), F2DEM retains 80% capacity over 350 cycles at 0.4/2 mA cm^-2 (charge/discharge), over 200 cycles at 1/2 mA cm^-2, and over 125 cycles at 1.5/3 mA cm^-2; it also outperforms F5DEE and DEM at 0.75/1.5 mA cm^-2.

Electrochemical stability section reports DEM-specific performance: "The average CE of F2DEM was measured as 99.53 ± 0.03% for four cells, which is higher than ... 99.19 ± 0.07% (4 cells) with DEM." LSV onset values for DEM: "we observed a leakage current onset at around 6 V for both F2DEM and DEM (Figure 3d)" (Li||Al) and "the leakage current was observed at an onset voltage of around 4 V for F2DEM, which is higher than the 3 V observed with DEM" (Li||Pt). Long-term Li||Cu cycling note: "DEM fails to achieve a stable cycling over 99%" under fast plating/slow stripping conditions.

F5DEE is used as an ether electrolyte solvent (1.2 M LiFSI/F5DEE) in Li metal battery tests. Quantitative performance directly reported for F5DEE includes: (i) Li||Li symmetric cells (3 mAh cm^-2 per cycle): under 1 mA cm^-2, an overpotential of around 55 mV was observed for F5DEE; soft short occurred above 6 mA cm^-2 for both F5DEE and the comparator (Figure 2d, text: “Under a current density of 1 mA cm^-2, an overpotential of around 55 mV was observed for F5DEE, similar to our previous report… soft short … over 6 mA cm^-2”). (ii) Coulombic efficiency in Li||Cu (Aurbach protocol): previously observed for F5DEE as 99.46 ± 0.01% (3 cells) (text: “higher than the 99.46 ± 0.01% (3 cells) previously observed with F5DEE [22]”). (iii) Oxidative stability by LSV: in Li||Pt cells, leakage current onset around 4.5 V for F5DEE; in Li||Al cells, delayed onset around ~6.5 V for F5DEE (text: “With a higher degree of fluorination in F5DEE… onset voltage at around 4.5 V [Pt];” and “delayed voltage onset around 6.5 V for F5DEE [Al]”). (iv) Li||LFP coin cells (20 μm Li, 3.5 mAh cm^-2 LFP, 3.8 V cutoff): at 1.5 mA cm^-2 charge and 3 mA cm^-2 discharge, F5DEE capacity dropped below 80% retention after 100 cycles, whereas the comparator retained 80% to 125 cycles (Figure 5c-d). These data support F5DEE’s application as a Li battery electrolyte; no regeneration data apply.

For 1.75 M LiFSI / F2DEM: Li||Cu half cells (Aurbach method) reported average CE of 99.53 ± 0.03%. In Li||LFP coin cells, under 0.4 mA cm^-2 charge and 2 mA cm^-2 discharge, F2DEM can retain 80% of its first cycle capacity over 350 cycles. Additional evidence: under 1.5 mA cm^-2 charge and 3 mA cm^-2 discharge, F2DEM retained 80% over 125 cycles; under 1 mA cm^-2 charge and 2 mA cm^-2 discharge, 80% retention over 200 cycles. Overpotential at 1 mA cm^-2 was ~30 mV in Li||Li symmetric cells; LSV onset ~6 V on Al and ~4 V on Pt, indicating oxidative stability.

PU-SN-LiTFSI is used as a solid polymer electrolyte for Li batteries. Results: “Conductivity reaches its maximum (2.865×10^-4 S cm^-1) at room temperature when the SN content is 41.1 wt%.” The measured electrochemical window shows “no obvious current peak below 4.068 V.” The Li+ transference number t_Li+ = 0.511 at 41.1 wt% SN. Symmetric Li//Li cell: stable cycling for 600 h at 0.1 mA cm^-2. Solid-state Li//LiFePO4 battery: at 0.5C retains ~92% after 700 cycles; rate capacities: 150, 143, 134, 121, 88, 56, 33, 17 mAh g^-1 at 0.1C–5C.
LLZO-F0.2 fiber-based CSE displays the highest ionic conductivity of 5.37 × 10^−4 S cm^−1 at room temperature. The lithium-ion transference number of LLZO-F0.2 fiber-based CSE reaches 0.61.

LiBC-1 is used as a solid polyelectrolyte (and binder) in Li/LiBC-1/LiFePO4 cells. Table 1 reports its ionic conductivity at 25 °C: LiBC-1 = 2.3 × 10^-6 S cm^-1. The text further notes conductivity increases to the 10^-5 S cm^-1 level at 55 °C, an anodic stability up to 4.3–4.5 V vs Li+/Li at 70 °C (CV), and a Li+ transference number of 0.83 at 70 °C. Full cells Li/LiBC-1/LiFePO4 delivered up to 130 mAh/g_LiFePO4 at C/15 and 70 °C, demonstrating LiBC-1’s application as a battery electrolyte.

LiBC-2 is a single-ion conducting block copolymer electrolyte for Li+. Table 1 reports its ionic conductivity at 25 °C: σ = 1.5 × 10^-6 S cm^-1. Other electrochemical metrics (electrochemical window, Li+ transference number, cell cycling) were provided only for LiBC-1.

Table 1 reports for LiBC-3: σ at 25 °C = 1.2 × 10^-6 S cm^-1; [EO]/[Li] = 18; Tg = -41 °C. The text also states that “the conductivity values of the block copolymers increased … and reached the targeted 10^-5 S cm^-1 level at 55 °C,” which applies to all block copolymers (including LiBC-3), but no exact LiBC-3 value at 55 °C is given.

LiBC-4 is a single-ion conducting block copolymer electrolyte (poly(PEGM)-b-poly(LiMTFSI)) evaluated for ion conduction. Table 1 reports for LiBC-4: ionic conductivity at 25 °C = 1.6 × 10^-7 S cm^-1, [EO]/[Li] = 6, Tg = 0.6 °C. Conductivity was measured by EIS (Figure 3). Battery prototype testing and electrochemical stability window measurements were performed on LiBC-1, not LiBC-4; thus no cell performance data are available for LiBC-4.

CPE is used as the polymer electrolyte in Li batteries: “The assembly process of LFP/CPE/Li is as follows…” (Cell Assembly and Measurements). CPE-specific performance: ionic conductivity at 25 °C is 1.64×10^−5 mS cm^−1 (Figure 3 discussion); oxidation stability for CPE is 4.5 V vs Li/Li+ (Figure 3c); Li/CPE/Li shows a soft short after 30 h at 0.2 mA cm^−2 and shorts after 3 h at 0.5 mA cm^−2, with 255 mV polarization in the first hour (Figure 4a, text); LFP/CPE/Li has very poor specific discharge capacity at room temperature and cannot work at 0 °C, with high interface impedance Rct = 2264 Ω before cycling (Figure 6 text; Figure S7a). Schema numeric fields (capacity/selectivity/breakthrough/regeneration) are not directly applicable to electrolyte performance, so left null.

GCPE was used as the electrolyte in LFP/GCPE/Li coin cells. At room temperature, LFP/GCPE/Li cells have a specific discharge capacity of 164.7 mAhg^{-1} at 0.1C, with capacity retention over 99% and Coulombic efficiency over 99% after 100 cycles (Figure 6a,b). At 0 °C, LFP/GCPE/Li cells deliver 128.4 mAhg^{-1} at 0.1C, with >99% capacity retention and >99% Coulombic efficiency after 100 cycles (Figure 6d,e). Additional GCPE performance metrics: ionic conductivity 1.19×10^{-4} mScm^{-1} at 25 °C; electrochemical window 4.6 V vs. Li/Li^{+}; Li/GCPE/Li symmetric cell shows stable plating/stripping without short circuit for 700 h at 0.2 mA/cm^{2} with low polarization (Figure 4a; Figure 3c).

This work evaluates PVA10 (PVA:P(AMPSLi-IL)=10:0) as a polymer/gel electrolyte host in Li batteries. Table 1 reports its room-temperature ionic conductivity 1.80×10^-3 S·cm^-1 (25 °C), porosity 79.5%, and electrolyte uptake 498%. Electrochemical tests of the PVA10 gel electrolyte give a lithium transference number t_Li+ = 0.71 and oxidation potential 4.03 V vs Li/Li+ (Fig. 8d). In symmetric Li|PVA10|Li, the oscillation voltage suddenly increases at 1440 h, indicating dendrite issues (Fig. 9b). In Li|PVA10|LFP half-cells, the initial discharge capacity at 0.5C is 129.9 mAh/g, and after 100 cycles at 0.5C the capacity is 24.3 mAh/g (Fig. 10b,d).

For Li|PVA9-P(AMPSLi-IL)1|LFP half-cells at room temperature: “The LFP|Li half cell with the PVA9-P(AMPSLi-IL)1 electrolyte delivers an initial discharge capacity of 145.7 mAh·g−1 at 0.5 C with a capacity retention of 79.0% after 200 cycles.” Also summarized in the conclusion: “0.5 C 下电池的初始放电比容量为 145.7 mAh·g−1, 200 圈后的容量保持率为 79.0%.” Additional properties for this electrolyte include σ = 2.87×10−3 S·cm−1, t_Li+ = 0.85, oxidation limit 4.34 V vs Li/Li+, and symmetric Li|Li stability >1800 h, confirming its application as a Li+ conducting polymer electrolyte.

Table 1 shows data for PVA7-P(AMPSLi-IL)3 (PVA:P(AMPSLi-IL)=7:3): Porosity 78.4%, Uptake 518%, and room-temperature ionic conductivity σ = 2.12×10^-3 S·cm^-1 (25 °C). 原文："表1展示了不同纤维膜的孔隙率、吸液率及吸液后的室温离子电导率… 7:3… σ (25 °C) 2.12×10-3"。

PVA6-P(AMPSLi-IL)4 corresponds to the nanofiber membrane with PVA:P(AMPSLi-IL)=6:4. Table 1 reports for this exact sample: porosity 75.3%, electrolyte uptake 493%, and room-temperature ionic conductivity 1.83×10^-3 S·cm^-1 (25 °C). The membranes are used as gel polymer electrolytes for lithium batteries by in situ gelation in 1 mol/L LiFSI in EC with TPGDA (Section 1.2.5). Thermal stability applicable to all nanofiber membranes (thus including the 6:4 sample): significant thermal decomposition temperature at ~280 °C with main weight loss 300–500 °C. Morphology note for the 6:4 sample: solid particles are observed and non-uniform distribution leads to decreased mechanical properties. Cell-level electrochemical data were shown for the 9:1 sample; no cell performance values are provided specifically for the 6:4 sample.

Text states: "For GPE with a P(HFBMA - co - PEGMA) / PVDF mass ratio of 1.7 / 10, the ionic conductivity reached 3.19 mS cm^-1." This corresponds to the specified material composition and demonstrates its application as a gel polymer electrolyte for Li+ conduction.

Table 1 (Aminolysis and transesterification of poly(HFIPA)) reports: with benzyl alcohol (2 equiv) and DBU (0.5 equiv) in THF, 25 °C, 5 h gives 98% conversion; at 50 °C, 2 h gives 99% conversion. Text in Results: “a very efficient transesterification of poly(HFIPA) with BnOH was obtained under DBU catalyzed conditions … 98% conversion within 5 h at 25 °C … 99% conversion in 2 h at 50 °C.”
Table 2 (Aminolysis and transesterification of poly(HFIPMA)) shows: benzyl amine (2 equiv) with DBU (2 equiv) in DMF at 75 °C for 12 h gave 98% conversion for poly(HFIPMA) (Mn=7003, Mw/Mn=1.18).

In the paper, bis(4-methy benzene sulphonyl)imide (1) is only reported as a synthetic intermediate: 'Synthesis of bis(4-methy benzene sulphonyl)imide (1)... A white powder was obtained with a yield of 69% ... indicates high purity...' No application or performance data are provided for (1). All application results (ionic conductivity, Li+ transference number, ESW, cycling) pertain to later polymer membranes derived after further functionalization and polycondensation, not to compound (1).

No direct application/performance data are reported for the potassium(4-carboxyl benzene sulphonyl)imide monomer (2). The paper uses (2) only as a precursor for polycondensation to form the polymer (3)/(4) and the lithiated polymer (5), and all electrochemical/battery results (ionic conductivity, transference number, ESW, cycling) refer to membranes made from the lithiated polymer (5), not to the monomer (2).

The polymer (sample 1:3 PVdF-HFP:lithiated polymer) is used as a single-ion conducting polymer electrolyte membrane for Li batteries. Reported performance directly tied to the polymer electrolyte includes: ionic conductivity 0.5 mS/cm at 20 C and 1.08 mS/cm at 60 C (Fig. 5b; Section 3.1); Li+ transference number t_Li+ = 0.9 at 40 C (Fig. 7; Section 3.1); electrochemical stability up to 4.6 V vs Li/Li+ (Fig. 10; Section 3.3.1); stable Li/Li symmetric cell stripping-plating for 1100 h at 0.1 mA/cm2 and 60 C (Fig. 11; Section 3.3.2). In NMC111/Li metal cells using this polymer membrane, specific capacities were 163 mA h/g at 0.05C and 161 mA h/g at 0.1C at 60 C, and 142 mA h/g at 0.05C and 116 mA h/g at 0.1C at 20 C, with high coulombic efficiencies after formation (97.5 percent at 60 C; 99.2 percent at 20 C) (Fig. 12; Section 3.3.3). A 7Li self-diffusion coefficient of 4.6e-11 m2/s at 20 C is also reported (Conclusion; Section 3.1). No adsorption/catalysis-type capacity, selectivity, breakthrough, or regeneration metrics apply; therefore those fields are left null.

No application/performance data are reported for sample (4) itself. All membranes and electrochemical/transport measurements use the lithiated polymer (5) blended with PVdF-HFP (e.g., 'successful lithiation was achieved... The lithiated polymer (5)...', and 'four different membranes with corresponding PVdF-HFP to lithiated polymer ratios... were fabricated'). Electrochemical stability, conductivity, PFG-NMR, and cycling tests are explicitly on the swollen SIPE membranes containing (5), not on (4). Sample (4) is only an intermediate (H+-form) prior to lithiation ('Substitution of potassium with hydrogen ions... The yield of (4) was 95%').

The lithiated polymer (5) is used to form single-ion conducting polymer electrolyte (SIPE) membranes (blends with PVdF-HFP) for Li-based batteries. For the PVdF-HFP:lithiated polymer = 1:3 membrane, ionic conductivity is reported as 0.5 mS cm^-1 at 20°C and 1.08 mS cm^-1 at 60°C (Section 3.1, Fig. 5b). The lithium-ion transference number t_Li+ = 0.9 was measured for sample 1:3 (Fig. 7). The electrochemical stability window reaches up to 4.6 V vs Li/Li+ (Fig. 10). Symmetric Li/SIPE/Li cells show stable lithium stripping/plating at 0.1 mA cm^-2 for 1100 h at 60°C (Fig. 11). In NMC111/Li cells with this membrane, specific capacities of 163 mAh g^-1 (0.05C) and 161 mAh g^-1 (0.1C) at 60°C, and 142 mAh g^-1 (0.05C) and 116 mAh g^-1 (0.1C) at 20°C were obtained, with first-cycle CE ≈80% and subsequent CE 97.5% (60°C) and 99.2% (20°C) (Fig. 12).

PEL is a composite solid electrolyte for Li batteries. Reported metrics for PEL include: highest ionic conductivity of 5.1×10^-4 S/cm at 10 wt.% LLZTO (Table 1; also stated in Conclusions: "when LLZTO content is at 10 wt.% , the composite solid electrolytes exhibit the highest ionic conductivity of 5.1×10^-4 S cm^-1"). Lithium-ion transference number for PEL is 0.41 (Figures 5e,f: "for the composite solid electrolyte PEL, it is 0.41"). Additional PEL-related performance (not placed in fields): electrochemical stability window 4.6 V (Figure 5d), and in LFP/PEL/Li cells an initial discharge capacity of 132 mAh g^-1 at 0.1 C with 81% retention after 100 cycles (Figure 6c; Conclusions).

PMHM is used as an in situ polymerized electrolyte in Li metal batteries. For NCA/PMHM/Li at 0.5C, "a discharge capacity of 162.2 mA h g^-1 with a capacity retention of 91.8% is maintained after 200 cycles" (Fig. 3c and S13). Supporting electrolyte metrics include an electrochemical stability window of 5.5 V vs. Li+/Li (Fig. 2a), room-temperature ionic conductivity of 1.1×10^-3 S cm^-1 (Fig. S9), and stable Li/PMHM/Li symmetric cycling for 1000 h with ~4 mV polarization at 0.05 mA cm^-2 (Fig. 2e). Additional full-cell data: NCA/PMHM/Li at 0.1C delivers 176.5 mA h g^-1 after 100 cycles (Fig. 3a-b), and at 1C maintains 113.8 mA h g^-1 after 500 cycles (Fig. S14). LFP/PMHM/Li at 0.1C maintains 144.7 mA h g^-1 after 200 cycles (Fig. S11).

- PVDF-HFP-PEO-SiO2 GPE ionic conductivity: 1.12 × 10^-3 S cm^-1 (Fig. 4b; text: “The ionic conductivity of … PVDF-HFP-PEO-SiO2 GPEs can be calculated to be 1.12 × 10^-3 S cm^-1”).
- Li+ transference number tLi+: 0.49 (Fig. 5; text: “PVDF-HFP-PEO-SiO2 GPE reached a high tLi+ of 0.49”).
- Electrochemical stability window: anodic limiting potential 4.7 V (Fig. 4a; text: “PVDF-HFP-PEO-SiO2 GPE showed the highest anodic limiting potential of 4.7 V”).
- LiFePO4/GPE/Li cell performance enabled by this GPE: initial discharge capacity 152.5 mAh/g and initial coulombic efficiency 94.5% (Fig. 6b); 146.3 mAh/g after 200 cycles at 0.1C with ~100% retention (Fig. 6c); rate capability 115.5 mAh/g at 2C and recovery to 147.7 mAh/g when returned to 0.1C (Fig. 6d–e).
- Li/GPE/Li symmetric cell stability: stable plating/stripping for 1000 h at 0.1 mA/cm2 with low polarization and no short-circuit; also stable 600 h at 0.2 mA/cm2 (Fig. 7a–b).

Ionic conductivity: "The ionic conductivity of the ... pure PVDF-HFP ... GPEs can be calculated to be 2.46 × 10^-4 S cm^-1" (Fig. 4b). Li-ion transference number: "pure PVDF-HFP GPE (t_Li+ = 0.39)" (Fig. 5). Thermal stability context: pure PVDF-HFP showed only 4% shrinkage at 150 °C and no significant change at 175 °C, supporting use as a separator (Fig. 2a,b).

TF+HCE is a fluorinated gel polymer electrolyte for lithium metal batteries. It exhibits high ionic conductivity (4.02 mS cm^-1 at 30 °C) and enables long-term cycling (Li|Cu: 300 cycles at 2 mAh cm^-2; 1000 cycles at 1 mAh cm^-2, CE ≥ 98.73%) and high-rate/full-cell operation (≥80% retention over >500 cycles at 2–3 C; pouch cell energy density 465.63 Wh kg^-1; 2.5 Ah Gr|NCM811 pouch ~95% retention over 200 cycles).

For the PPMBB10 gel-polymer electrolyte in Li//LFP cells: initial discharge specific capacity is 146.8 mAh/g at 1 C with ~100% Coulombic efficiency and 76% capacity retention after 1000 cycles (Figure 5a,b). At 2 C, initial capacity is 151.5 mAh/g with 84% retention after 364 cycles (Figure 5c,d). PPMBB10 also exhibits ionic conductivity 1.03×10^-3 S/cm at 25 °C (Figure 3a), Li+ transference number 0.7901 (Figure 3c), and an electrochemical stability window of 5.5 V (Figure 3d).

FGPE is used as a gel polymer electrolyte for Li metal batteries. Key performance directly tied to FGPE: ionic conductivity 4.53×10^-4 S cm^-1 at 25°C; electrochemical window up to 5.0 V; Li+ transference number t_Li+ = 0.43; Li|FGPE||Li symmetric cell overpotential ~55 mV and stable plating/stripping for >2000 h at 1 mA cm^-2 and 1 mAh cm^-2; at 2.5 mA cm^-2 polarization was 162 mV. Li|FGPE||Cu nucleation overpotential 67 mV with CE_avg 98.8%, and stable CE 97.8% for 300 cycles at 0.5 mA cm^-2. In Li|FGPE|NCM811 cells, specific discharge capacities were 224.1, 218.3, 205.6, 195.5, 183.3, and 159.1 mAh g^-1 at 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1, 2, and 5 C, respectively; at 1 C the initial specific capacity was 201.7 mAh g^-1 with 78.6% retention after 200 cycles. A pouch cell with FGPE delivered 208 mAh in the first cycle and ~225 mAh stable for 40 cycles, with nonflammability (self-extinguishing time 0 s) also noted.

Text explicitly mentions 'preparation of PVT electrolyte' and 'leakage current curve of PVT electrolyte', along with electrochemical characterizations including EIS/LSV, temperature dependence of ionic conductivity curves, and 'lithium ion transference numbers' for samples—indicating PVT is used as a Li+ conducting electrolyte.
