P(HFBA1:1-co-MEA)/LiTFSI is used as a solid polymer electrolyte in SSLMBs. The paper reports: "The ionic conductivity of P(HFBA1:1-co-MEA)/LiTFSI reached 4.83 × 10^-5 S cm^-1" at room temperature (Figure 2a, Section 3.2). It is explicitly assembled in Li|electrolyte|Li and Li|electrolyte|LiNi0.5Co0.3Mn0.2O2 (and LiFePO4) cells, confirming its role as an electrolyte.

PMEA/LiTFSI was used as a solid polymer electrolyte in SSLMBs: “For comparison, a PMEA/LiTFSI-based SSLMB was prepared via the same procedure...” The electrochemical stability window for PMEA/LiTFSI was 4.2 V (“Unlike that of the PMEA/LiTFSI electrolyte (4.2 V)”). The Li+ transference number for PMEA/LiTFSI is 0.17 (Figure S1 referenced in text). In Li|PMEA/LiTFSI|Li symmetric cells at 0.05 mA cm−2, the cell failed after 109 h with an initial polarization ~160 mV (“the Lil PMEA/LiTFSIILi battery had an initial polarization voltage of 160 mV, and the battery failed after just 109 h”). In Li|PMEA/LiTFSI|LiNi0.5Co0.3Mn0.2O2 cells at 0.1 C, both electrolytes showed similar initial discharge capacities of ~120 mAh g−1 (“both batteries exhibited similar discharge capacities of ∼120 mAh g−1 in the initial cycle”), while the PMEA cell dropped to 7.8 mAh g−1 after 40 cycles (“the discharge capacity of LiPMEA/LiTFSI … is only 7.8 mAh g−1 after 40 cycles”).

Paper title states: "PVDF-HFP-based polymer electrolyte ... for solid-state lithium metal batteries," indicating PVDF-HFP is used as a polymer electrolyte for solid-state lithium metal batteries. No quantitative performance data provided in the excerpt.

The title states 'PVDF-HFP-based polymer electrolyte producing LiF-rich interface for solid-state lithium metal batteries', indicating FB-PVDF-HFP is used as a polymer electrolyte for solid-state Li metal batteries; no performance metrics are provided in the supplied text.

For the 75 vol% LiFSI/EMI-FSI freestanding electrolyte film in Li|LiFePO4 cells, the paper reports: “This value was 2.89 times higher than that at 2C (46 mAh g−1) for the battery sample using a freestanding electrolyte film with 75 vol% LiFSI/EMI-FSI.” (Fig. 2 caption/context, 35 °C; discharged at varied C rates after charging at 0.1C). It also states the 75 vol% sample “could not achieve 150 mAh g−1 even at 0.1C,” but gives no exact number at 0.1C.

The paper evaluates quasi-solid-state electrolyte films with 75–85 vol% LiFSI/EMI-FSI in Li|LiFePO4 cells. Explicit numeric battery performance (e.g., mAh/g at given C-rates) is only reported for 85 vol% and for groups described as ">80 vol%"; no specific capacity or rate metrics are given for the 80 vol% sample. Impedance and diffusion data include 80 vol% in comparisons (e.g., Table I reports D_Li = 2.6×10^-11 m^2 s^-1 and t_Li = 0.27 at 35 °C), but the schema targets battery performance metrics, which are not explicitly provided for 80 vol% in the text.

Electrolyte: HBPS-(PMMA-b-PPEGMA)30/BMImPF6/LiTFSI used in Li/LiFePO4 cells showed after 100 cycles a discharge capacity of 115.8 mAh g^-1 with ~96% retention at 0.1 C and 60 °C (Fig. 8). Electrochemical stability window (LSV, 30 wt% BMImPF6) reached 4.3 V vs Li+/Li at room temperature (Fig. 6). Ionic conductivity reported as 4.1×10^-5 S cm^-1 at room temperature for 40 wt% BMImPF6 and 3.0×10^-4 S cm^-1 at 60 °C (Fig. 4, Conclusion). Lithium-ion transference number t_Li+ = 0.03 at 60 °C for the BMImPF6 electrolyte (30 wt%) from Table 1.

Li/LiFePO4 cells based on HBPS-(PMMA-b-PPEGMA)30/BMImPF6/LiTFSI electrolyte at 60 °C and 0.1 C show: “After 100 cycles, discharge capacity can still reach 115.8 mAh g−1 with a retention ratio of about 96% (under the current density of 0.1 C),” and the same PF6-based electrolyte is explicitly used for these tests.

Battery tests were performed using the specific sample as electrolyte: “Li/40PDFP-POPM GPE/LiFePO4 battery… The discharge capacity of Li/40PDFP-POPM GPE/LiFePO4 reached 145 mAh g^-1 after the 100 cycles and the columbic efficiency was maintained over 95% during the 100 cycles.” The same section shows its role and stability as an electrolyte: electrochemical potential window over 4.7 V (vs. Li+/Li); lithium ion transference number for 40PDFP-POPM GPE is 0.692 (Table 3); symmetric Li cell polarization ~19 mV stable for 300 h. These results are directly tied to the 40PDFP-POPM GPE.

PE(F4) was used as the polymer electrolyte in a Li/PE(F4)/LiCoO2 cell. Table 5 reports Specific capacity = 42 mA h, Specific power = 130.2 mW h, Specific energy = 468.72 J, Potential = 3100 mV. Supporting performance for PE(F4): ionic conductivity 1.00×10^-4 S cm^-1 at 23°C (Table 4; Fig. 11), electrochemical stability window 2.5–6.0 V with ~15 μA cm^-2 at 6.0 V (Fig. 12), initial OCV 2.75 V and charge cut-off 3.1 V (Fig. 14).

UiO-66-(F)4 is used as MOF nanofiller in the composite solid polymer electrolyte (CSPE15-12 contains 12 wt.% UiO-66-(F)4). The electrolyte with MOF fillers exhibits an ionic conductivity of 3.01×10^-5 S·cm^-1 at 30 °C and a high t+ of 0.54. The electrochemical window of the electrolyte becomes broaden from 4.0 to 4.3 V. Li/CSPE15-12/Li cell stably cycled for more than 1,000 h at 60 °C (0.1 mA·cm^-2, 0.1 mAh·cm^-2). In LFP/CSPE15-12/Li cells, the capacity remained 141.4 mAh·g^-1 after 200 cycles at 0.2 C and 60 °C; at 0.5 C it delivered 107.6 mAh·g^-1 after 100 cycles.

SPE15-0 is the polymer electrolyte without MOF fillers. Reported metrics for SPE15-0: ionic conductivity 7.53×10^-6 S·cm^-1 at 30 °C ("By contrast, the ionic conductivity of SPE15-0 was only 7.53×10^-6 S·cm^-1 at 30 °C."); Li+ transference number tLi+ = 0.23 ("the tLi+ value ... is improved from 0.23 to 0.54 after composition"); Li/SPE15-0/Li symmetric cell short-circuited after 396 h at 0.1 mA·cm^-2 and 0.1 mAh·cm^-2 at 60 °C ("the Li/SPE15-0/Li cell encountered short-cut after cycled for only 396 h"). Additional context directly for SPE15-0: electrochemical window 4.0 V vs Li+/Li at 60 °C; tensile stress 0.211 MPa; elongation at break 28%; LFP/SPE15-0/Li fails after 26 cycles.

CSPE15-9 is explicitly prepared as a free-standing composite solid polymer electrolyte (9 wt.% UiO-66-(F)4) for all-solid-state Li metal batteries (Li/electrolyte/Li and LFP/electrolyte/Li configurations). Performance data in the text are given mainly for CSPE15-12; CSPE15-9 appears only among the compositions in Fig. 5(a) ionic conductivity plots without numerical values reported in text or tables.

CSPE15-12 is a composite solid polymer electrolyte membrane (12 wt.% UiO-66-(F)4). The paper states: 'The highest ionic conductivity (3.01×10^-5 S·cm^-1 at 30°C) is obtained when 12 wt.% UiO-66-(F)4 is added. Therefore, the CSPE15-12 membranes are chosen to conduct the following tests.' Additional performance relevant to this electrolyte in Li metal batteries includes: 'the t_Li+ value ... improved from 0.23 to 0.54 after composition'; 'after the addition of the MOF fillers, the electrochemical window ... broaden from 4.0 to 4.3 V'; 'the Li/CSPE15-12/Li cell stably cycled for more than 1000 h at a current density of 0.1 mA·cm^-2 and a cycle capacity of 0.1 mAh·cm^-2 (60°C)'; and in full cells, 'The LFP/CSPE15-12/Li cell remained a high capacity of 141.4 mAh·g^-1 after 200 cycles (0.2 C, 60°C)' and 'At a higher current rate of 0.5 C, the LFP/CSPE15-12/Li cell shows a capacity of 107.6 mAh·g^-1 after 100 cycles.'

CSPE15-15 is explicitly prepared as a free-standing electrolyte membrane containing 15 wt.% UiO-66-(F)4: “Free-standing electrolyte membranes SPE15-0, CSPE15-9, CSPE15-12, and CSPE15-15 … were obtained…” It is evaluated among “the prepared electrolytes” for ionic conductivity (Fig. 5(a)), where “A lower ionic conductivity of CSPE15-15 may be caused by the aggregation of excessive nanofillers.” The work targets all-solid-state lithium metal batteries; Li+ conduction is explicitly stated: “The EO units in PEG segments can serve as Li+ conductor,” and electrochemical tests are performed in Li/electrolyte/Li and LFP/electrolyte/Li cells. No specific numeric performance values are reported for CSPE15-15.

Sample labeling: “With the lithium contents of Li-X, the samples were marked as pristine (without Li-X), Li-X-1%, …” (Section 3.1). Electrochemical window: “the current signal of pristine dramatically arises above a low voltage of 3.8 V vs. Li|Li+ resulting from DMF decomposition” (Fig. 4a text). Li|Li symmetric cells: at 0.1 mA/cm2, “the overpotential of pristine electrolyte dramatically decreased and eventually short-circuit” (Fig. 4d text). Full cell: “The pure PVDF-HFP electrolyte exhibits rapid capacity fade over 50 cycles under a current density of 0.1 C … at 0.5 C … pure PVDF-HFP electrolyte with a 113.8 mAh/g capacity” (Fig. 5a,b text).

Li-X-1% is defined as one of the Li-X@PVDF-HFP composite electrolytes: “the composite electrolytes with different content of Li-X (0 wt.%–4 wt.%) in PVDF-HFP were marked as pristine, Li-X-1%, Li-X-2%, Li-X-3%, and Li-X-4%.” Electrochemical window: “Figure 4(a) shows the LSV curves for different electrolytes… after introducing zeolite, the LSV curves are stable and smooth up to 4.7 V vs. Li|Li+,” which applies to all Li-X-containing samples, including Li-X-1%. No specific ionic conductivity or t_Li+ values are reported in the main text for Li-X-1% (only Li-X-3% values are given), so numerical fields are left null.

Li-X-2% is explicitly defined as a Li-X@PVDF-HFP composite electrolyte sample ("the composite electrolytes ... were marked as pristine, Li-X-1%, Li-X-2%, Li-X-3%, and Li-X-4%."). It is used as a solid-state electrolyte in coin/pouch cells (sections 2.5–3.4). Electrochemical stability (LSV) is stated for all zeolite-containing electrolytes, which includes Li-X-2% ("after introducing zeolite, the LSV curves are stable and smooth up to 4.7 V vs. Li|Li+"). However, specific ionic conductivity, t_Li+, cycling, or full-cell performance values are only reported for the Li-X-3% sample; no distinct quantitative performance is provided specifically for Li-X-2% in the main text. Therefore, numeric fields are left null.

Specific to Li-X-3%: (1) Ionic conductivity: “the Li-X-3% electrolyte exhibits the highest ionic conductivity of 1.98×10^-4 S/cm at 80 °C” (Fig. 4b text). (2) Li+ transference number: “Li-X-3% exhibits … the highest t_Li+ of 0.55” (Sec. 3.3; Table S5/Fig. S4 referenced). (3) Electrochemical window: after introducing zeolite, LSV curves are stable up to 4.7 V vs Li/Li+ (applies to Li-X-3%) (Fig. 4a text). (4) Symmetric cell stability: “Li symmetry cell with Li-X-3% electrolyte … worked smoothly over 700 h at 0.1 mA/cm^2” (Fig. 4d). (5) Critical current density: “limit current density of Li-X-3% is 1.8 mA/cm^2” (Fig. S5c). (6) Full-cell results using Li-X-3% electrolyte: Li|Li-X-3%|LiFePO4 shows >500 cycles at 0.1 C with ~99.6% CE; 153.8 mAh/g at 0.5 C; maintains 154.2 mAh/g when rate returns to 0.1 C; stable 300 cycles at 0.5 C and 60 °C; Li|Li-X-3%|LiCoO2 delivers 119 mAh/g for 150 cycles at 0.5 C (Sec. 3.3–3.4).

The sample set includes Li-X-4%: “the composite electrolytes with different content of Li-X (0 wt.%–4 wt.%) in PVDF-HFP were marked as pristine, Li-X-1%, Li-X-2%, Li-X-3%, and Li-X-4%.” (Section 2.3). These Li-X@PVDF-HFP materials are used as composite polymer electrolytes in solid-state lithium batteries (Sections 2.5–3.3). After introducing zeolite (which includes the 4% sample), “the LSV curves are stable and smooth up to 4.7 V vs. Li|Li+” (Fig. 4(a), Section 3.3). However, specific quantitative performance values (e.g., ionic conductivity, t_Li+, cycling) are reported for Li-X-3% and not for Li-X-4%; the text notes that “excessive zeolite (such as 4%) could impede the migration of lithium ions and increase their migration energy barrier” (Section 3.3). Therefore only the application scenario and target species are extracted for Li-X-4%.
Specifically, the liquid electrolyte absorptivity of the PPEM, PSPEM-1, PSPEM-2, and PSPEM-3 are 158%, 180%, 209%, and 222%, respectively.
Figure 3(c) reports the liquid electrolyte absorptivity (uptake) of the membranes: PPEM 158%, PSPEM-1 180%, PSPEM-2 209%, PSPEM-3 222%.

As shown in Fig. 3(c), the addition of SN significantly increases the liquid electrolyte absorptivity of the composite polymer membranes... Specifically, the liquid electrolyte absorptivity of the PPEM, PSPEM-1, PSPEM-2, and PSPEM-3 are 158%, 180%, 209%, and 222%, respectively.
The sample of pure PVDF-HFP was named as PPEM. The liquid electrolyte absorptivity of the PPEM, PSPEM-1, PSPEM-2, and PSPEM-3 are 158%, 180%, 209%, and 222%, respectively.

Direct metric for PSGPE-1: "At 30 °C, the ionic conductivities for PGPE, PSGPE-1, PSGPE-2, and PSGPE-3 are ~0.81, 1.0, 1.07, and 1.35 mS·cm^-1, respectively." Additional context of use in LIB cells: "2032-typed coin cells were assembled with LiFePO4 and graphite... under 2.50–3.65 V at room temperature (25 °C)." Oxidative stability includes PSGPE-1: "The oxidation voltage slightly decreases to 5.17 V (PSGPE-1 and PSGPE-2)."

PSGPE-3 is a PVDF-HFP/SN gel polymer electrolyte used in LiFePO4//graphite Li-ion cells. At 1 C, the cell with PSGPE-3 delivered a specific capacity of 109.5 mAh·g^-1 ("the specific capacities ... are 98.3, 106.2, and 109.5 mAh·g^-1, respectively" for PSGPE-1, -2, -3). Additional performance for PSGPE-3: ionic conductivity 1.35 mS·cm^-1 at 30 °C, and anodic limiting potential 4.92 V. Cycling at 0.5 C showed capacity retention of 56.4% after 350 cycles for PSGPE-3.

PGPE is the PVDF-HFP gel polymer electrolyte without SN. At 30 °C, the ionic conductivities for PGPE, PSGPE-1, PSGPE-2, and PSGPE-3 are ~0.81, 1.0, 1.07, and 1.35 mS·cm−1, respectively. LSV shows PGPE exhibits the highest oxidation voltage (5.34 V). The t_Li+ of PGPE is 0.58. After activating at 0.1 C, the cell using PGPE delivers 125.8 mAh·g−1 at 0.5 C.

The sample 35TFMA-0.5LF-0.5LB was selected and abbreviated as SGPE: “Since it shows exceptional high ionic conductivity (0.93 mS cm^-1 at RT), 35TFMA-0.5LF-0.5LB GPE was selected for subsequent tests and abbreviated SGPE.” Its measured ionic conductivity: “SGPE exhibits high ionic conductivity of 1.03 mS cm^-1 at 30 °C.” Additional performance context for this electrolyte includes: “The t_Li+ of SGPE … is calculated up to 0.41,” and “The SGPE has an obviously higher initial oxidation potential of 5.1 V.” Cell-level demonstrations using SGPE: “The Li|SGPE|Li cell presents highly reversible Li stripping/plating performance for over 1000 h at 0.1 mA cm^-2,” and “LiCoO2|SGPE|Li battery delivers high average specific capacity >120 mAh g^-1 over 200 cycles.”

CGPE is the comparative gel polymer electrolyte (HEMA-based): “The comparative sample CGPE was prepared by replacing TFMA with an equal volume of HEMA in SGPE.” Performance (electrolyte property): “the ionic conductivity of 35HEMA-0.5LF-0.5LB GPE electrolyte is calculated to be 0.37 mS cm−1 and the electrolyte is denoted as CGPE.” Temperature comparison also states: “CGPE (0.39 mS cm−1 at 30 °C).” Additional CGPE-related metrics (for context): Li+ transference number tLi+ = 0.11; oxidative stability onset 4.2 V; Li|CGPE|Li symmetric cell shows sharp voltage fluctuations at 0.15 mA cm−2 (critical current density limit) and fails in <500 h with polarization >200 mV; LFP|CGPE|Li full cell delivers 88.8/54.6/21.3/12.6/6.6 mAh g−1 at 0.1/0.2/0.5/1/2 C and drops to ~26 mAh g−1 after 250 cycles at 0.3 C; flammability: CGPE shows fierce combustion.

The sample 35TFMA-65TEP was prepared solely for FTIR anchoring-effect verification and not tested as an electrolyte in cells: 'The samples for FTIR characterization were prepared by mixing TEP with TFMA ... the finally obtained samples were named 35TFMA-65TEP, 40TFMA-60TEP, 60TFMA-40TEP and 80TFMA-20TEP' (Section 2.2.2). Later discussion references only its anchored-TEP fraction: 'the 35TFMA-65TEP sample used in SGPE dedicates coordinated TEP up to 55.6%' (Fig. 7c text). All electrochemical performance data (conductivity, tLi+, cycling) are reported for SGPE (35TFMA-0.5LF-0.5LB), not for 35TFMA-65TEP.

The material 40TFMA-60TEP is only prepared and used for FTIR verification of the anchoring effect (Section 2.2.2: samples named 35TFMA-65TEP, 40TFMA-60TEP, 60TFMA-40TEP, 80TFMA-20TEP). No electrochemical/battery application performance data (conductivity, cycling, etc.) are reported specifically for 40TFMA-60TEP.

The material 60TFMA-40TEP is only described as an FTIR verification sample without electrochemical or battery performance tests: “The samples for FTIR characterization were prepared by mixing TEP with TFMA… the finally obtained samples were named … 60TFMA-40TEP …” (Section 2.2.2). No ionic conductivity, cycling, or other application metrics are reported specifically for 60TFMA-40TEP.

The composition 80TFMA-20TEP appears only as an FTIR verification sample prepared by mixing TEP with TFMA and polymerizing ("the finally obtained samples were named ... 80TFMA-20TEP"). These FTIR-only samples contained no lithium salts and were not evaluated in electrochemical/battery tests. All performance data (ionic conductivity, cycling, Li|Li and LFP/LCO full cells) are reported for SGPE/CGPE formulations (e.g., 35TFMA-0.5LF-0.5LB, denoted SGPE) rather than 80TFMA-20TEP. Therefore, no application performance metrics specific to 80TFMA-20TEP are available in Results/Discussion, figures, or tables.

35HEMA-65TEP was prepared for spectroscopic verification and not evaluated in battery tests. Evidence: “The samples for FTIR characterization were prepared… the finally obtained samples were named 35TFMA-65TEP… Samples of 35HEMA-65TEP… can be obtained by replacing TFMA with HEMA under the same synthetizing process.” (Section 2.2.2). Performance data reported in the paper refer to SGPE (35TFMA-0.5LF-0.5LB) or CGPE (35HEMA-0.5LF-0.5LB), e.g., “the ionic conductivity of 35HEMA-0.5LF-0.5LB GPE electrolyte is calculated to be 0.37 mS cm−1 and the electrolyte is denoted as CGPE,” not to 35HEMA-65TEP. No application metrics (conductivity, cycling, etc.) are given for 35HEMA-65TEP.

40HEMA-60TEP was prepared only for FTIR (and Raman) verification as an HEMA–TEP model sample: “Samples of 35HEMA-65TEP, 40HEMA-60TEP, 60HEMA-40TEP and 80HEMA-20TEP can be obtained by replacing TFMA with HEMA under the same synthetizing process.” No electrochemical/battery performance was reported for 40HEMA-60TEP. The text notes that in the HEMA–TEP system the -P=O peaks “barely show any shifts… (Fig. S21),” but provides no application metrics. All performance data (conductivity, tLi+, cycling, etc.) are given for SGPE (TFMA-based) or CGPE (35HEMA-0.5LF-0.5LB), not for 40HEMA-60TEP.

The material 60HEMA-40TEP appears only as an FTIR verification sample: "Samples of 35HEMA-65TEP, 40HEMA-60TEP, 60HEMA-40TEP and 80HEMA-20TEP can be obtained by replacing TFMA with HEMA under the same synthetizing process." (Section 2.2.2). No electrochemical/battery performance tests or application metrics were reported for 60HEMA-40TEP specifically.

The material 80HEMA-20TEP appears only as an FTIR characterization sample: “Samples of 35HEMA-65TEP, 40HEMA-60TEP, 60HEMA-40TEP and 80HEMA-20TEP can be obtained by replacing TFMA with HEMA under the same synthetizing process.” (Section 2.2.2). It is not used in electrochemical/battery tests; performance data (conductivity, cycling, capacity, etc.) are reported for SGPE and CGPE, not for 80HEMA-20TEP. Therefore, no application performance can be extracted for 80HEMA-20TEP.

The sample labeled 1 M-SGPE was prepared specifically for Raman analysis (TEP:TFMA=65:35, 1 M LiTFSI, 1% AIBN; polymerized at 60 °C for 12 h). The paper reports that “TFSI ions interact with more Li ions in 1 M-SGPE compared with the 1 M-CGPE” (Fig. 7g), indicating its role as a Li+ conducting electrolyte. No explicit electrochemical performance metrics (ionic conductivity, transference number, cell cycling, etc.) are provided for 1 M-SGPE; those values are reported for the 35TFMA-0.5LF-0.5LB SGPE instead.

The specific sample "1 M-CGPE" is only defined and used for Raman characterization: "The samples for Raman characterization were prepared ... The final samples ... were labeled as 1 M-SGPE and 1 M-CGPE" (Section 2.2.2). In Results, it is only discussed qualitatively: "TFSI ions interact with more Li ions in 1 M-SGPE compared with the 1 M-CGPE" (Section 3.5). No electrochemical application metrics (ionic conductivity, t_Li+, oxidation window, cycling performance, etc.) are reported for 1 M-CGPE.

F-QSCE@30 shows ionic conductivity 1.21 mS cm^-1 at 25°C (Fig. 2b; Highlights/Abstract). It has an oxidation stability ~5.20 V (Fig. 2a). Li/F-QSCE@30/Li symmetric cell exhibits low overpotential (24.2 mV) and stable cycling for >4000 h (Fig. 2h). t_Li+ = 0.41 at 60°C (Fig. S5). In full cells at 60°C: LFP/F-QSCE@30/Li delivers 151.3 mAh g^-1 initially with 99.5% CE and 98.8% capacity retention after 460 cycles at 0.5C (Fig. 3a-b). NCM622/F-QSCE@30/Li achieves 139.6 mAh g^-1 after 180 cycles (97.6% retention) at 0.2C, and 94.6 mAh g^-1 after 350 cycles (~100% retention) at 0.5C, with good rate capability and capacity recovery (Fig. 3g-i). All these performance results are specifically for F-QSCE@30.

The fluorine-free QSCE is denoted as QSCE@30. Performance data specific to QSCE@30 include: ionic conductivity 0.34 mS cm^-1 at 25 °C (Fig. 2b); electrochemical stability window with oxidation at 4.50 V (Fig. 2a); exchange current density I0 = 0.016 mA cm^-2 (Fig. S4); Li+ transference number t_Li+ = 0.34 at 60 °C (Fig. S5). Li/QSCE@30/Li symmetric cells show higher overpotentials and limited stability: overpotentials 47.9 mV (0.1 mA cm^-2), 106.1 mV (0.2 mA cm^-2), 571.9 mV (0.4 mA cm^-2) (Fig. 2e–g); initial overpotential 42.5 mV and short circuit after 897 h (Fig. 2h). Full-cell results at 60 °C: Li//LFP with QSCE@30 delivers 86.2 mAh g^-1 (first cycle at 0.5C) with 98.7% CE and 53.2% capacity retention after 150 cycles (Fig. 3a); polarization 77.4 mV (1st, 0.1C), 413.2 mV (50th, 0.5C), 410.0 mV (100th, 0.5C) (Fig. S7); rate-recovery capacity 134.3 mAh g^-1 when returning from 2C to 0.1C (Fig. 3c). EIS after 50 cycles shows R_ct = 412.9 Ω (Fig. 3d–e). These results evidence QSCE@30 as a lithium battery electrolyte with measurable ionic transport and cycling characteristics, but the provided schema has no dedicated fields for conductivity/window/overpotential, so those values are recorded here.

SiO2@IL was prepared and "stored for use as the fillers to prepare QSCEs" (Sect. 2.2). In the optimized electrolyte composition, it is included as a filler ("together with 5 wt% SiO2@IL"; Sect. 3.1). The text links it to Li+ transport pathways: "The weakened coordination number is conducive to the Li+ transport in the IL on the surface of SiO2@IL or in the polymer chains" (Sect. 3.3). No standalone performance metrics specific to SiO2@IL are reported; therefore capacity/selectivity/breakthrough/regeneration are left null.

21-β-CD-Br was synthesized as a tailor-made multifunctional macroinitiator and "acted as a highly efficient multifunctional macroinitiator for initiating the ATRP of monomers with double bonds"; "All hydroxyl groups of β-CD were substituted with bromine groups, which were subsequently utilized as ATRP initiating sites". No quantitative performance metrics (rates, yields, etc.) for 21-β-CD-Br as an initiator were reported; quantitative electrochemical performances pertain to downstream 21-β-CD-g-PTFEMA-based FMC-ASPEs, not to 21-β-CD-Br.

21-β-CD-g-PTFEMA is a key component of the fluorine-rich macromolecule-containing all-solid-state polymer electrolytes (FMC-ASPEs) used in Li metal batteries. Ionic conductivity of FMC-ASPE-Li reached 6.43×10^-4 S cm^-1 at 80 °C ("the ionic conductivity reached 6.43×10^-4 Scm^-1 ... at 80 °C for FMC-ASPE-Li"). The cation transference number was high ("The calculated t_Li+ of FMC-ASPE-Li was 0.88"). Additional performance context: oxidation stability extended to ~4.7 V vs. Li+/Li ("The oxidation potentials of the FMC-ASPEs increased to ~4.7 V in the AS-LMBs"), and Li|FMC-ASPE-Li|Li symmetric cells cycled >3800 h at 0.1 mA cm^-2 and 70 °C ("exhibiting long cycling stability for more than 3800 h ...").

PEO-ASPE-Li is an all-solid-state polymer electrolyte for lithium metal batteries. Its Li+ transference number is reported as t_Li+ = 0.21 ("The determined t_Li+ of FMC-ASPE-Li (0.88) was ~4.4 times larger than that for PEO-ASPE-Li (t_Li+ = 0.21), see Fig. 5a, Supplementary Fig. 53, and Supplementary Table 11"). The LSV discussion notes the oxidation potential of PEO-ASPEs is ~3.8 V vs. Li+/Li (Fig. 2a text), but only the transference number is explicitly quantified for PEO-ASPE-Li in the main text.

PEO-ASPE-Na specific metrics reported: (1) Sodium-ion transference number: “The calculated t_Na+ value of FMC-ASPE-Na (0.71) was 3.5 times larger than that for PEO-ASPE-Na (t_Na+ = 0.2, see Fig. 5a, Supplementary Fig. 54, and Supplementary Table 14).” (2) Oxidation stability: “The oxidation potential of PEO-ASPEs has been verified to be ~3.8 V vs. Li+/Li (or ~3.5 V vs. Na+/Na).” Additionally, full-cell results indicate baseline DCR with PEO-ASPE-Na: “...NVOPF|FMC-ASPE-Na|Na … improved from 61 to 89% and … NCNFM|FMC-ASPE-Na|Na … from 60 to 83%,” implying PEO-ASPE-Na DCR baselines of 61% and 60% under the stated conditions.

FMC-ASPE-Li is the Li-version all-solid-state polymer electrolyte. Reported performance includes ionic conductivity reaching 6.43×10^-4 S cm^-1 at 80 °C for FMC-ASPE-Li (Fig. 3a; text: “the ionic conductivity reached 6.43×10^-4 Scm^-1 … for FMC-ASPE-Li”). The Li+ transference number t_Li+ was calculated as 0.88 (Fig. 3c,d; text: “The calculated t_Li+ of FMC-ASPE-Li was 0.88”). Oxidation stability ~4.7 V vs Li+/Li is also reported (Fig. 2a), but no regeneration procedure/data were given.

FMC-ASPE-Na is used as an all-solid-state polymer electrolyte for sodium metal batteries. Reported metrics include: (1) ionic conductivity: “the ionic conductivity reached 8.43×10^−4 S cm^−1 at 80 °C for FMC-ASPE-Na” (Fig. 3b); (2) cation transference number: “the t_Na+ of FMC-ASPE-Na was ~0.71” (Fig. 3e,f and text). Additional context: the electrochemical oxidation stability for FMC-ASPEs in AS-SMBs increased to ~5.0 V (Fig. 2b), and full-cell cycling with FMC-ASPE-Na showed improved discharge capacity retentions (e.g., NVOPF and NCNFM cells; Fig. 7). Only Na-related data were used for this material.

P(IL-OFHDODA-VEC) is a solid polymer electrolyte for Li metal batteries. In Li|P(IL-OFHDODA-VEC)|NCM523 full cells (cutoff 4.5 V, 30 °C), it delivered an initial discharge capacity of 164.19 mAh/g at 0.5 C and retained 146.96 mAh/g after 200 cycles. Rate performance reached 185.80 mAh/g at 0.1 C and ~104.82 mAh/g at 2 C. Supporting electrolyte metrics include ionic conductivity 1.37 mS/cm at 25 °C (optimized composition), Li+ transference number ~0.40 at 25 °C, ESW ~5.08 V vs Li+/Li, and stable Li|Li cycling with ~43 mV polarization up to 2500 h at 0.1 mAh/cm^2. These results are explicitly reported for P(IL-OFHDODA-VEC) in the manuscript figures and text.

Fig. 3b reports the electrochemical stability window (ESW) of P(OFHDODA-VEC) as 5.12 V (vs. Li+/Li). No ionic conductivity, cycling, or other battery performance data were provided specifically for P(OFHDODA-VEC); those results pertain to P(IL-OFHDODA-VEC).
Figure 3b shows that the extracted ESW of PIL ... is 5.51 V (vs. Li+/Li).

Figure 3b reports the electrochemical stability window (ESW) measured by LSV for several polymers; POFHDODA shows an ESW of 5.13 V (vs. Li+/Li). This directly reflects POFHDODA’s applicability as a solid polymer electrolyte component with high oxidative stability.

Figure 3b shows that the extracted ESW of PIL, POFHDODA, PVEC, P(IL-VEC), P(IL-OFHDODA) and P(OFHDODA-VEC) is 5.51, 5.13, 4.35, 4.39, 5.31, and 5.12 V (vs. Li+/Li), respectively. Compared with PIL and POFHDODA, PVEC possessed the narrowest ESW.

PVCA is used as a polymer electrolyte component for Li-metal batteries: "To demonstrate the potential of PVCA as a polymer electrolyte component of high-energy-density LMBs, we selected PVFH ... as the polymer matrix." PVCA participates at both interfaces: "PVCA participates in the formation of the cathode-electrolyte interphase (CEI), suppressing electrolyte decomposition and ensuring the structural stability of the LRMO" and "PVCA has the lowest LUMO energy level and a relatively high HOMO energy level, indicating its ability to preferentially participate in the formation of SEI and CEI." Its function relates to Li+ transport: "PVCA activates the dissociation of Li salts, leading to a greater number of mobile Li+." All reported numerical electrochemical performances (e.g., ionic conductivity, cycling stability) are for the PVFH-PVCA blended electrolyte, not PVCA alone; therefore no quantitative metrics are extracted specifically for PVCA.

PVFH soaked in the liquid electrolyte (PVLE) was used as a PVFH-based control electrolyte: "PVFH soaked in the LE (PVLE) and Celgard with 60 μL of the LE (CLE) were selected as control samples." The ionic conductivity and Li+ transference number reported for PVLE are: "The PVFH-PVCA gel electrolyte exhibited a significantly higher ionic conductivity at 25 °C (2.04×10^−3 S cm^−1) than that of PVLE (0.94×10^−3 S cm^−1; Fig. 3b). Furthermore, it presented a higher Li+‑transference number (0.61) than those of CLE (0.34) and PVLE (0.31) (Fig. 3c and Supplementary Fig. S15)." Additional PVFH-related battery performance (for LMNCO|PVLE|Li): "the LMNCO|CLE|Li and LMNCO|PVLE|Li batteries exhibited lower capacity retentions of only 64.0 and 33.9% after cycling under the same conditions (400 cycles, 2.1–4.6 V, 1C)."

PVFH-PVCA is used as a gel polymer electrolyte for Li-metal batteries. Reported metrics: ionic conductivity at 25 °C of 2.04 × 10^-3 S cm^-1 for the sample with 15 wt% PVCA (Fig. 3b; also noted in Supplementary Fig. S6). It also presented a higher Li+ transference number (0.61) than CLE (0.34) and PVLE (0.31) (Fig. 3c and Supplementary Fig. S15). Additional performance context (not separately fielded): oxidation onset ~5.3 V vs Li+/Li (Fig. 3a); Li||Li symmetric cells cycled stably >4500 h at 8 mA cm^-2 and 4 mAh cm^-2 (Fig. 5a); overpotential ~32 mV at 10 mA cm^-2 (Fig. 5b); full cells with various cathodes show high capacity retention (e.g., LMNCO 84.8% after 400 cycles at 2.1–4.6 V, 1C; 78.8% after 340 cycles at 2.1–4.8 V).
